chapters.title: Chapter 1
chapters.content: Blue on Blue

chapters.title: Port of Baltimore, Container Yard, 3:41 am
chapters.content:
The 9mm’s blast broke the shadows for a split second. A shape came from the right directly into her sight line. Bill had come out of cover and stepped in front of her. A plume of blood and brain matter geysered from his head. He collapsed like someone cut his marionette strings. 
She replayed the scene from the beginning. 
Bill was yelling something to her, but with the gunshots clapping and her eardrums ruptured, she couldn’t hear him. He motioned away from her. 
A shot hit the edge of the container she was behind, sparks flying into her face, the ricochet zooming past. She recoiled. Miranda leaned and returned fire to cover Bill. 
Wait? Did Bill motion toward her? Did he motion to her or away from her? 
Her stomach dropped. 
To her? 
Away from her?
“Oh God, did he motion he was going my way?” she gasped.
Miranda grimaced in concentration and dread.
“Miranda?” 
She sighted her 9mm where she thought the shooter was. The pistol barked. 
“AGENT CRUZ!”
Miranda turned to see Justin Hart, her supervisor, her friend, motioning to her. He turns to talk to someone who has just arrived.
Miranda slowly walked to him. Each step felt like quicksand. 
What will I tell Shelley?
She stops beside Justin, awaiting the gallows. 
“Miranda, are you ok?”
Miranda opened her mouth, then closed it again. How do you even answer that question?
“Agent Cruz, I know you’re in shock, but are you injured?”
She looked at her arms and chest. “N-No.” She croaked.
“You have a scratch above your eye that needs to be looked at.”
Before she could process it, she touched it. There was pain, but it felt distant, cold. The blood on her fingers was wet but not warm. 
“I’m sorry about Bill.”
Miranda flinched. The world came into focus.
Sirens. Shouting. Flashing lights.
Miranda stood in front of Justin. She hugged herself.
“Right. You got one of them. Another is in an ambulance on his way to the hospital. We think at least one got away.”
Miranda waited. All of this was important to her before Bill’s head was blown apart. Now, none of it seems that important. It was certainly not worth Bill’s life.
He seemed to get the hint.
“The container had 14 women and young girls in it. It was going to Thailand. ICE is here to process them.”
Justin kept talking. Miranda wasn’t listening. Fourteen lives forever changed. Was it worth the cost? Bill would say yes. If the roles were reversed, she would say yes. But being on this side, she’s not sure. 
“...Formal board of inquiry will be convened immediately. The investigation will take a little time. Until then, you are suspended with pay.”
Miranda gave him a blank look.  
“It’s standard procedure.”
“I understand,” Miranda wheezed.
“I need your gun.”

Moussa's Sacrifice

chapters.title: Moussa's Sacrifice
chapters.content:
## Bamako, Mali — June 16, 7:03 PM
Moussa didn’t like the pickup point. His ancient Soviet MI-8 squatted next to him on a field, rotors glinting in the rising moonlight, waiting to work. 
Magnambougou soccer field was dark, uneven, and too far from anything official. No lights. No uniforms. Just four men standing apart from each other like they’d been told not to look like a group.
They paid well. Too well.Cash. All in advance.
Enough for a year.
That should have been a hint to say no. But he has a family to feed and a helicopter to maintain. 
His pride and joy. 
La Vieille was painted by the cockpit. Old Lady. She was old, cranky, and always threatening to die. Well past her prime, but too stubborn to die. 
A drunk Russian pilot crashed her just outside the city ten years ago. They left her to rot. Moussa found her and, for the next five years, competently nursed her back to health. Now his project became his life and livelihood. 
She’s ugly, only a parent could love her, but she’s a beast of a machine, built for survival, not looks, like most things Russian. Big bulbous nose, ungainly tail, landing gear that he still couldn’t get to retract. She was his beast, and he loved her more than she deserved. 
He walked the helicopter. He ran his hand along the fuselage, checking panels and switches he already knew were correct. Bought time. 
His phone buzzed once in his pocket. It was probably Aïssata, his wife, reminding him to call when he landed.
He didn’t answer.
Moussa, watched the men. They were waiting for something, and it seemed to be arriving. They were all military types. Lean, short beards, muscles. Two of them carried Pelican cases. He saw pistols on all of them. 
A Land Rover was coming down the road. The men started moving around, getting their items together with silent precision.
One of them, he assumed the leader, he never got his name, spoke to him in French, “We are almost ready, get the helicopter ready to take off as soon as we are ready.”
Moussa nodded.
Ten minutes later, they were airborne.
Moussa loved the lurch of a helicopter taking off. He kept the altitude low. The destination wasn’t far, just 100 km east of the city limits. He had looked it up, a farm field in the middle of nowhere. An odd location to be certain, but not completely unusual. 
City lights slid past beneath them, Bamako flattened into amber and shadow. As they crossed the river, he saw his neighborhood in the distance. A familiar rooftop. He changed his channel to the channel he used to talk to his family when he was working. 
“Be home for bedtime mes enfants.”
He could hear his children giggling as they grabbed the transmitter. 
“We miss you Papa.”
He turned the radio dial, and accidentally clicked one too many times. 
“Bomb is at the market” one of them said in French.
“We’ll be well past the minimum safe zone.”
Moussa stiffened.
He had patched into their internal channel without announcing himself. They were speaking English, they thought he didn’t speak it, he never corrected them. 
“Anders, put the bomb under your seat. Set the altitude trigger for thirty meters AGL.”
Not a charter. Once he takes off, he can’t land. The trees are over thirty meters tall in every direction. But, If he lingered too long, they would just shoot him.  
He eased the helicopter off course—barely enough to notice, just enough to buy seconds.
Behind him, the air changed.
After just a couple of minutes, a voice came through in French.
“Correct your heading,” it calmly said.
Moussa got back on course. 
As they neared the destination, he saw it.
A C-130 on the ground. Propellers already spinning. He could see the lights on a large vehicle entering the cargo bay.”
These weren’t terrorists. 
Moussa did the math. He had to make his death worth something. 
He whispered into the mike, “I’m sorry n muso kɔrɔ. Je t’aime, mon amour.”
“What did you say?” One of the passengers growled.
He froze. He looked at the radio. He hadn’t switched channels. 
No matter, he knew what he had to do. He rolled the helicopter hard left, pushed the throttle to the maximum, and set his sights on the aircraft on the ground. 
In the rear, chaos erupted. Voices boomed at once, men, accustomed to lives of inflicting violence on others, scrambled for their lives. 
One grabbed his pistol. One reached for Moussa. One tried to get into the co-pilot seat. 
A gunshot tears through the cockpit. The bullet exiting the windshield just centimeters from Moussa’s head. One of the men grabs the throttle, thinking it’s something else. He pulls it back, stalling the helicopter.
“NO!” One of the men screams.
The Old Lady drops from the sky like she’s had a stroke. 
As she falls, Moussa realizes they’re not going to make the plane. “Fine,” he thinks. “I’ll just get these assholes.”
From the field,  the commander watches the helo fly erratically through his binoculars, and then drop from the sky like a sack of potatoes. The helo banks nose down and impacts the ground. the entire front of the aircraft crumples like an accordion, then falls on its back. A second later, a small fire, then an explosion as it finally dies.
The commander lowered his binoculars as the wreckage burned, fuel popping in sharp reports. No one spoke. One of his men looks at him, worry etched into his face, “Tomas?”
“Go,” he finally said. “Nothing has changed.”
They returned to work, and started entering the C-130. The commander entered last, taking one last lingering look at the city.
A device clung to the side of a cell tower closest to the Bamako Coura Artisan Market, its dull metal casing camouflaged against the tower's weathered gray infrastructure. Inside a pair of glass cylinders glowed faintly in the dim interior. One, a viscous purple liquid that caught what little light penetrated the casing, shimmering with an iridescent quality reflected light in colors that didn’t exist elsewhere. The other held a black oily substance that absorbed all light, as still as death itself. Between them, a digital timer displayed: 0:02.
A click and whir as a small centrifuge starts to spin.
0:01
A fan powers up, powerful for its size, but quiet. 
0:00
A glass seal breaks at the bottom of the box, in between the two cylinders. The fluids mix in a larger central centrifuge. They combine. Chemicals react, a gas, heavier than air, forms and flows to the bottom, where the fan expels it into the air, where it immediately starts to drift to the market below.
At the market, Ibrahim Diarra came to look for a gift for his daughter. Her birthday was tomorrow, and he had just gotten paid from his job at the Bramali plant. He was lucky; he got here just before closing. Not that it looked like it was settling down anytime soon. 
He went to a leather stand. Crocodile belts and purses hung outside. A small handbag hung from the roof, square and colorful, with abstract flowers. It was perfect for Hawa. 
He and the stall owner started bargaining. It slowly came to a close, as they were not going to make a deal. Not a problem, plenty of other stalls. Ibrahim laughed, shook his head, and walked away with the vendor still shouting after him.
Still laughing, Ibrahim walked towards a shirt vendor. Then he slowed.
Something in the air had changed.
Cinnamon. Cloves. Sweet, but wrong at the same time. A fine yellow mist. 
He turned the corner and coughed once. Then again. His chest seized as if clamped shut. He dropped the bag, clawing at his throat.
Across the street, someone else went down.
Then another.
The market dissolved into panic.

chapters.title: The Grove
chapters.content:
## Three Days Earlier, Loudoun County, VA, 6:03 AM
Noah Hammond walked the ridge trail above Gray Manor, Martin Wessex trailing behind at a pace that was deliberate in its slowness. The morning air was cool. The kind of temperature that made you forget Virginia turned into a steam bath by August. Noah wore hiking clothes, nothing expensive. Boots that had seen real miles. A small pack with water and trail mix he wouldn't eat. He was walking but said little to his companion.
“You don’t usually let the trees win.”
Noah smiled, “I’m fine. Just… Tired.”
Martin stopped and gestured at the trees. "You see where we are?"
Noah stopped. Looked around. The trees here were different. Older. The trunks were massive, the bark deeply furrowed. American Chestnuts. The only remaining grove in Virginia. Maybe the only one east of the Mississippi that wasn't a managed plantation. Scientists didn't even know it existed. He'd spent a fortune keeping it that way.
He bent down, grabbed an old chestnut husk from the ground. The shell was brittle. Empty. He turned it over in his palm. "Some things are worth saving."
 "Yes." Martin said. His brow furrowed, “This isn’t you just being tired.”
“Just having a day when nothing seems worth it.”
Noah looked up at the canopy. Light filtered through in broken columns. The air smelled like loam and living things. He closed his eyes. The pressure hit like a spike behind his left eye. Not pain. Worse than pain. Weight. His breath stopped. Yellow. The first thing that resolved was yellow. A fine mist, heavy in the air, clinging to skin and fabric. A bridge. Colorful market stalls. The sound wasn't panic at first. It was confusion. People coughing. A woman fell. Screams. Then a child. The market kept trying to function while something invisible killed it. Noah's knees buckled. His hand found a tree trunk. Rough bark against his palm. Grounding. "Noah?" Martin's voice. Far away. Crowds. Civilians. The yellow gas. It couldn’t be what he thought it was. That didn’t exist except in stories and legends now. He forced his eyes open. The grove was still there. Martin was beside him now, hand on his shoulder.
"How bad?" Martin asked.
Noah straightened slowly. His pulse hammered. "Africa. I heard French and Bambara. Maybe Mali. A bridge. Market. Chemical fog. A lot of people are about to die" He paused.
Martin's face went still. "When?"
 "Soon. I think." Noah's jaw tightened. "Days. Maybe less." Noah turned back to the manor and started at a blistering pace. Martin struggled to keep up.
They reached the main house and walked directly to the study. Martin followed. The staff knew better than to interrupt. Noah picked up the phone. Dialed. The call went unanswered. He dialed again. This time it rang longer.
"Mr. Hammond," the operator said. Professional. Tired. "The President is unavailable."
"Just go to your appointment priority field and enter Y-9-W. Yankee-Nine-Whiskey."
"Those are five-digit codes, sir. What are the other two characters?"
"Just enter it."
A pause. Mouse clicks. Keys tapping. The operator's breath changed when she saw what came up. "Someone will be in touch in thirty minutes," she said. Her voice was unsteady now.
When the line went dead, Martin exhaled slowly. "The National Security Provision? You're certain?"
"I've been wrong before," Noah replied. "This isn't one of those times."
Twelve minutes later, the new President appeared on the secure screen already irritated. Jacket off. Tie loosened. Expression set to dismiss. "You're calling me at sunrise. You'd better have something real."
"I do," Noah said. "But I don't have proof yet."
The President laughed once. Sharp. Dismissive. "Then you don't have anything."
"There's going to be a WMD mass-casualty event in Bamako, Mali," Noah said. His voice was level. Precise. "Civilian market. Chemical weapon. One you’ve never seen before."
"That's convenient."
"It's dangerous," Noah replied. "Which is why I'm asking for—"
"No."
The word was flat. Noah blinked once. "I haven't finished."
"I have," the President said. "You don't get to move assets on a feeling. That’s not how I work. You give me proof, facts, targets, and I’ll consider it. I’m not the last president, I don’t move national assets on your feelings."
"This isn't a feeling."
"You don't have proof. You don't have a suspect. You don't have a delivery mechanism. You don’t even know what weapon it is." The President leaned forward. "You don't have anything I can justify to Congress, the NSC, or even my wife."
Noah leaned forward too. "People are going to die."
The President shrugged. "People die every day. Today is no different. If it happens, then we’ll revisit this."
The screen went dark.
Martin stared at it for a long moment, as if waiting for it to come back on and apologize.
“That was not what I was expecting,” he said.
Noah didn’t answer. He remained forward in his chair, elbows on the desk, looking at the dead screen like it had committed a personal offense. He brought his hands through his hair in frustration. He sighed. “Send an advance team.”
Martin didn’t move. “Without any official support?”
“No other choice. Obviously he’s not taking this seriously. Small team. Hopefully they can find the device before it goes off. My vision was at sunset, make sure they’re gone before then.” Noah’s voice was flat, but his jaw had set. “If they get eyes on a device, they call it in to the embassy. If they can pull it without being seen, they pull it. No heroics.”
Martin’s eyes held his for a beat. “You’ll own it.”
Noah nodded once. “I always do.”
Martin lifted the handset on his side of the desk and dialed a number from memory.
“Sabrina,” he said when the line connected. No pleasantries. “Pack light. Bamako. Four-person element. I’ll brief you in full on your way to Dulles.”
He hung up.
Noah had not moved. His hands were still on the desk, fingers spread as if bracing for impact.
“They’ll be there in twelve hours,” Martin said.
Noah’s eyes were distant. “They won’t be early enough.”
“We don’t know that.”
Noah didn’t argue. He stood and went to the window. The chestnut grove sat beyond the manicured grounds like a patch of darker history. Unbothered. Unimpressed.
“Sometimes you save what’s left,” Noah said.
Martin didn’t reply. He didn’t like Noah when Noah talked like that. It meant Noah was already counting the dead.
Three days later, the news alert came without ceremony.
Noah was back in the study when Martin entered. His face had changed—not grief, not fear. The expression of someone walking in to deliver a number.
“Chemical attack reported in Bamako,” Martin said. “Preliminary count is climbing.”
Noah didn’t turn from the window. “How many?”
“Eight hundred and change, and rising.”
Noah closed his eyes. The vision returned—not as images, but as a weight settling into the body.
“Our people?” he asked.
Martin hesitated just long enough to make it cruel.
“Sabrina survived,” he said. “Two didn’t.”
Noah turned. His voice stayed level. “Names.”
“Palmer. Rios.” Martin’s gaze dropped for half a second, then returned. “They were at the market perimeter when the release started. They tried to move civilians out. They didn’t get far.”
Noah’s hand clenched on the windowsill. The wood creaked.
“They were supposed to be gone before dark.”
“They couldn’t not try to save people.” Martin did not add anything else. He didn’t need to.
Noah went very still. For a moment it looked like the room might fracture around him.
Then he crossed to the desk and struck it hard enough to spill the coffee, not hard enough to be satisfying.
Noah’s voice dropped. “I sent them without cover, without authority, without backup, because I couldn’t stand being told no.” He stared at the spreading coffee as if it were blood. “That makes it mine.”
Martin didn’t contradict him. He didn’t offer absolution. He knew better than to try to take guilt away from a man who hoarded it.
The television in the corner ran silent footage: bodies on a bridge; people stumbling through air that looked clean.
A knock at the door.
A staff member stepped in, hesitant. “Mr. Hammond. There’s a package. Express delivery.”
Noah didn’t look up. “I’m not expecting anything.”
“It’s addressed to you, sir.” The staffer swallowed. “Postmark is Bamako. Three days ago.”
The room went still.
Noah’s voice cut through it. “Get out. Now.”
The staff member set the package down and left.
Martin stayed. He wasn't everyone.
Noah produced a small pocketknife.
He opened the lid. Inside was a smaller container, open-topped, filled with a velvet pillow. Laying inside was a knife. Ancient. Obsidian blade. Bone handle wrapped in leather that had turned dark with age.
"No,” he whispered as he dropped the box.
An envelope fell out. Intricate calligraphy addressing it to Noah.
Martin handed it to him. "Apparently, it's for you."
Noah stared at the calligraphy. “This is from Tomas.”
“Fuck,” Martin said before thinking.
Noah felt the envelope in his hands. Finally, he opened it. Read. Handed it to Martin without a word.
The letter was written on expensive hemp paper. Handwritten in an old style.
“Memini Aquileiae, Marce. Iterum fit. Scis quomodo hoc finiatur.”
I remember Aquileia, Marcus. It’s happening again. You know how this ends.
Noah sat in his chair. His eyes were distant.
"It's time to bring her in."
"Who?"
"Miranda Cruz."
Martin froze. "That HSI agent? She's mid-level. Sidelined. Her file—"
"I know."
"Then why her?"
Noah didn't answer right away. " I’ve seen what happens without her. We need her."
Martin waited.
Noah looked up at Martin, "Every outcome that isn’t catastrophic runs through her. Bring her in."

chapters.title: The First Nail
chapters.content:
## Oval Office 4:23 PM

“Rick, why the hell am I talking to Noah Hammond before seven in the morning?”
Noah had warned the President in his first year in office that aligning so closely with the Israelis would be the domino that overturned his presidency. Thus far, Noah's warning was an empty threat. His popularity was rising, and his Israeli friends had handled more than one potential scandal for him. 
Rick Hodge took his traditional seat across from the Resolute Desk.
“He invoked the National Security Provision in his contract.”
“The what?”
“He has a provision that allows him immediate access to the President in the case of a national security threat.”
“He what? Who the hell authorized that?”
“It’s been there for years. He’s never used it.”
The President turned his chair to look out the window. 
“Why not?”
“Doesn’t matter.”
“I suppose not. Can we just put this off?”
“Sure, I guess, but there’s a clause that works in our favor.”
The President turned to Hodge.
“Really? What is it?”
“If the President determines after 30 days that the use of the clause was capricious or unwarranted, you have discretion to invalidate his entire agreement.”
The President stood and started laughing.
“So you’re telling me he gave us a way to get rid of him forever?”
Hodge nodded.
The President smiled and turned back to the window. Today marks the first nail in Noah Hammond’s coffin.

chapters.title: The Exile
chapters.content:
## DEA Special Operations Division, Chantilly, VA 2:11 PM 
Miranda Cruz is on her third lunch of the day. This was her vending machine lunch. She punched in the numbers from memory: A102, C104, J111. Sour Gummy worms, Cool Ranch Doritos, Coke Zero. The third lunch of champions. 
First lunch was a brisk walk around the grounds while munching on beef jerky and a Clif Bar, washed down with a Coke Zero. 
Second lunch was Mongolian Beef at the Chinese place down the road, with a Diet Coke, because they didn’t have Coke Zero. 
She’d had to up her gym routine. All of this boredom eating was starting to get to her. Her CrossFit routine was also a great outlet for aggression. She had a lot of aggression nowadays.
She grabbed her items and walked back to her cubicle. She passed some colleagues on the way. They were discussing a human trafficking case. As soon as they saw her, they stopped and stared as she went by. 
Elaina, one of her oldest friends in the agency, gave her a sad smile, as if to say, “Sorry, I know you don't deserve this.” Ever since the investigation, this has been her life. 
Bill would never have let this happen. He would have shut it down, corrected it, and protected the junior agents from exactly this kind of quiet punishment. The thought hit harder than she expected. Her chest tightened.
Shelley was the only one who still spoke to her. Bill’s wife. 
She didn’t soften it. She never did. She was a Navy Corpsman stationed with the Marines in Iraq and Afghanistan. She wasn’t touchy-feely.
“What happened was either not you,” Shelley said, “or not your fault. And honestly? It doesn’t matter.”
Miranda looked at her, eyebrows raised.
“It was war,” Shelley continued. “That’s the part nobody wants to say out loud. I’ve watched people literally kill themselves with grief. That doesn’t help anyone.”
She leaned closer, voice lower. “It’s not what I want. And it sure as hell isn’t what Bill would want.”
Shelley met her eyes, steady and unflinching. “You don’t get to bleed out over guilt,” Shelley said. “I won’t allow it.””

Miranda felt the sting behind her eyes and crushed it down before it showed.
Not you. Not here. Don’t give them anything.
She straightened her shoulders and kept walking.
Part of her understood their doubts.
The bullet was the same caliber as her service weapon. An agent was dead, shot from behind, from the exact position where his partner was.
There had to be answers. Failing that, there must be accountability.
The investigation dragged on longer than it should have. There was always a reason; someone unavailable, paperwork missing, another affidavit to confirm. Delay stacked on delay.
Agents thought she was hiding something. That it really had been her fault.
A few went further. They couldn’t prove intent, but the implication lingered. That cut the deepest, as though she would deliberately kill the man who had been more father than partner.
If the roles were reversed, she would’ve had doubts too. Not murder. But fault? Probably.
The report concluded it was a ricochet.
That felt like weak sauce to her.
Reports are written to close cases.
Punishing her wouldn’t fix anything. But blaming the traffickers they’d caught, already guilty, already condemned, put them away for life and gave the agency its closure.
She let that version stand.
For now.
So now she’s at the Special Operations Division, THE joint operations nerve center for federal law enforcement. This should be a triumphant posting. Instead, she sits at a cubicle, collecting a paycheck for turning Oxygen into Carbon Dioxide. No supervisor will assign her to an investigation because no agent will take her on board. 
She sits at her desk and logs into her computer. Her browser is still on her Amazon cart. She spends most of the day filling it up and emptying it again. She hears the tone of an email receipt. She looks at the cubicle across from her, then realizes she received the email.
She switches to Outlook and sees it’s from the Special Agent in Charge (SAC) of SOD, Rick Dawson. She’s met him once, when she got there. It didn’t go well. She knows she’s not his favorite person. 
The subject line reads, “MY OFFICE. NOW.” The body is blank. Her stomach drops. She starts to sweat a little. 
“Fuck.”
Miranda walks to the elevator and heads up to the fourth floor. The doors open, and she walks into the SAC’s office. His secretary, April, looks at her, “He’s expecting you, go right in.” 
“FUUUUUCK.” Miranda thinks to herself. This cannot be good. She wipes her palms across her slacks in case she has to shake his hand. 
She opens the door and walks in.
Rick Dawson was one of the good old boys from the DEA. He started in the 90s and was mentored by some of the legendary first maverick agents of the DEA after it was first formed. He styled himself after them, but he’s more political opportunist than accomplished agent. 
He is looking at his computer and doesn’t even bother to look up. 
“You’re being assigned to an interagency assignment. I don’t know what it is. I don’t fucking care. Pack your shit, and there will be a car outside for you in 30 minutes.”
Miranda can't help but jump in shock. “Oh… ok.”
Dawson looks at her from his screen, “This is the moment where I am supposed to say it’s been great working with you, and you’ll be missed. But we both know that’s bullshit. See you around.” 
Dawson looks back at his screen and starts typing an email.
“See you around Rick.”
Miranda walks out and back into the elevator. She goes back to her floor, and goes straight to a stall in the bathroom. She lets out an involuntary breath and starts to cry. All of the emotions she didn’t know she was holding in come flooding out at once. 
There’s a knock at the stall door. “Are you ok?” It’s Elaina. Of course it’s Elaina. 
“Elaina?” Miranda sheepishly asks.
“Miranda?!” Elaina yelps.
Miranda opens the door. 
“I’m leaving.”
“What? Why?”
“Some bullshit interagency assignment. They’re putting me out to pasture somewhere.”
“Fuck Miranda. I’m sorry.” Elaina hugs her.
“Not your fault,” Miranda says through tears.
“I wish there was something I could have done.”
“I know you tried, I heard you arguing with Eric. He’s a douche.”
“He is such a douche.” Elaina laughs. Miranda notices she’s crying too. They’ve been friends since FLETC. They both thought they’d be speaking at their respective retirements. 
Miranda calms herself. “I’ve got to get it together. I’m apparently leaving in 20 minutes.”
“Fuck! Already?! We can’t even do a going away?”
“I think we just did. You were the only one who was going to show anyway.”
Elaina laughs. 
“Keep in touch Cruz, let me know how this assignment goes, whatever the hell it is.”
“I will. Thanks, Rodrigues.”
Miranda takes a moment in the mirror and walks to her cubicle. 
It doesn’t take long to pack up her desk. A few knick-knacks, a lip gloss, and her favorite pen. Every trace of her existence fits in a small backpack. She leaves her business cards. They sat in her drawer, box unopened.
She walks outside. A black Suburban is already parked out front. A trim Indian man in a navy suit is waiting outside of it. 
“Ms. Cruz, you’re early. Please get in, and we’ll be on our way.” He opens the back door for her.
Miranda gets in, and the door closes. They begin the drive.
She’s been in government Suburbans too many times to count. This one feels different. She can’t put a finger on why; it’s something about how it rides. 
It feels heavy. Smells different too. She’s used to suburbans smelling like sweat and gun oil. This one has a faint trace of cigars.
“Where are we going? I was told it’s an interagency task force, which agencies?”
“I don’t know, ma’am, I’m just a driver. I was told to take you to Langley.”
“Langley Air Force Base? That’s a hell of a drive from here. Aren’t we going the wrong way?”
The driver snickers, “No. CIA Langley.”
Miranda sits back slowly, “Oh… That Langley.”
“Yes, ma’am, that Langley.”
“Oh Jesus fucking Christ” Miranda mutters.

chapters.title: That Langley
chapters.content:
## CIA Headquarters, Langley, VA 4:29 PM
Miranda Cruz had never been to Langley before, and she already hated it.
Her badge got her into courthouses, task force offices, and border facilities. Places that smelled like burned coffee and stress. Langley didn’t smell like anything. It absorbed sound, light, and confidence with equal efficiency.
The black Suburban dropped her at the entrance and drove off without a word. No name. No goodbye. Just gone.
A young woman intercepted her before she could decide whether to admire or resent the architecture.
“Agent Cruz? Hi. Sorry, we’re running late. I’m Mackenzie. Please follow me.”
They moved fast. Too fast for questions.
Miranda caught sight of the memorial wall as they passed through the main foyer. White stars carved into dark stone. She slowed without meaning to.
“Most people do,” Mackenzie said, already walking again. “There are one hundred forty. I always pause, just a second. Okay, Pass and ID and then… Oh, we’re going a lot of places.”
The pause was over.
Miranda surrendered her phone, her watch, her badge. Three checkpoints. A retinal scan. Palm print. Someone took a photograph of her ears.
Her sense of humor didn’t survive the second ID check.
They descended into a subbasement hallway with no windows and no visible exits. A red-lit door waited at the end, marked only by a brass placard:
SECURE CONFERENCE ROOM
“This is you,” Mackenzie said. “Someone will be with you shortly.”
“How shortly?” Miranda asked.
Mackenzie smiled apologetically and left without answering.
Miranda waited.
No phone. A clock was vaguely ticking on the far wall. No sound but the hum of the lights.
She sat. She stood. She sat again. Eventually, habit took over. Cops learned early how to steal sleep.
She woke to the sound of the door opening. The clock said she’d been here ninety minutes.
A woman entered. Shorter than Miranda, but somehow Miranda felt like she was looking up at her. Immaculate, blonde hair pulled tight into a bun that suggested regulation rather than taste. She was pretty, but Miranda found herself intimidated, that didn’t happen often. She closed the door behind her. The lock sealed with hydraulic finality.
“Agent Cruz,” she said. “I’m Nicole. the FSO.”
“FSO?” Miranda asked.
“Facility Security Officer. I decide who knows what,” Nicole said evenly. 
She placed a slim orange folder on the table between them. In bold letters at the top and bottom:
TOP SECRET//SI//TK//HCS//SAR-OV//NOFORN
SPECIAL ACCESS REQUIRED / ORACLE VEIL (OV)
Before Miranda could touch it, 
Nicole slid paperwork to sign in front of Miranda, “You are being provisionally read into a Special Access Program. Above Top Secret. Your clearance is not finalized. Someone more senior will explain why that’s acceptable.”
Miranda picked up the pen and signed the forms. Nicole moved to the door.
“One question,” Miranda said.
Nicole paused.
“Why am I here?”
Nicole turned back. For the first time, emotion. Unfortunately, a sad smile crossed her face.
“You were requested by name.”
The door closed. Louder than she remembered the last time.
The door opened again.
The CIA Director walked in and sat across from her.
Peter Langford didn’t look like a politician at first. Broad-shouldered, with a nose that had been broken in the past. She’d read somewhere he was a tight end at West Point; she believed it. He was in his early sixties, maybe late fifties. His suit was dark, expensive, and unremarkable in a politician sort of way. He looked like an intimidating accountant. 
Langford studied her, then sat back. He slid the folder toward her an inch and smiled faintly. It felt like the smile a lion gives a gazelle.
“Ms. Cruz,” he said calmly. “I want to be very clear.”
He rested one hand on the folder.
“If you open this folder, your career in federal law enforcement is over.”
Miranda felt the words land physically.
“This assignment is not temporary,” Langford continued. “It is not rotational. You do not come back from it.”
He placed a small black phone beside the folder.
“I’ll require daily check-ins,” he continued. “Brief. Factual.”
He handed the phone to her, “Satellite-enabled. Secure. The number is saved.”
Miranda slowly placed it into her pocket, “And if there are none?” she asked.
“Then you aren’t doing your job.”
He leaned forward.
“You are not there to advocate. You are not there to become attached. On paper, you’re legal counsel. In reality, you’re a record.”
“A record of what?” Miranda asked.
“When laws are broken,” Langford said. “And why.”
Her stomach tightened.
Langford’s voice softened. Not kindly, but precisely.
Langford stood. and walked to the door.
“I thought,” Miranda said, “that he chose me.”
Langford stopped.
“We let him choose you,” he said without turning around.
The door closed.
Miranda sat alone with the folders.
She opened the file. The first paragraph gave her chills.
ORACLE VEIL is a compartmented Special Access Program established to manage, protect, and integrate intelligence derived from a unique long-term U.S. strategic capability operating under legacy authorities. The Subject operates under a protected identity and designated call sign: DARKWELL.
Information produced under ORACLE VEIL is assessed as having high-impact national security relevance and is disseminated to select elements of the Intelligence Community on a need-to-know basis.
Her hands were sweaty, her heart was hammering in her chest. She signed.
Nicole returned minutes later.
“I see you’ve accepted,” she said. “Your designation is Delta. Here is your role briefing.”
Miranda skimmed the briefing.
ORACLE VEIL (OV) POSITION: DELTA FUNCTION: EMBEDDED LEGAL AUTHORITY ACCESS: PROVISIONAL (PENDING ADJUDICATION) SCOPE: COMPARTMENTED / NEED-TO-KNOW
ROLE SUMMARY: DELTA is designated as on-site legal authority for ORACLE VEIL operations. DELTA is empowered to provide contemporaneous legal guidance and approval for activities conducted within established operational parameters.
DELTA determinations constitute standing authorization and do not require pre-approval by senior Department or Agency representatives, provided actions remain within program scope.
LIMITATIONS: DELTA is not authorized to expand program scope, initiate new operational authorities, or brief external entities without explicit approval.
REPORTING REQUIREMENTS: DELTA will document approvals, denials, and material deviations in routine secure reporting. Documentation serves as the official legal record of authorization.
NOTE: This assignment is non-rotational.
“Am I reading this right? I’m not a lawyer.”
Nicole’s look was pure pity. “Apparently, you’re close enough. Your position has been vacant for a number of years.”
Sweat was forming on Miranda’s forehead. 
“I’ll escort you out,” she said. “A vehicle will take you home. Your arrival instructions are in your email.”
“My car’s still at SOD,” Miranda said. “I’m not even sure I can get back in.”
Nicole sighed faintly. “Call the number provided. It will be handled.”
They walked to the front of the headquarters building again.
Miranda retrieved her phone and watch from the Faraday bag.
She looked up.
Her car was waiting.
Miranda stared.
“How the hell did you get my car here?”
A tall black man in a suit smiled from beside it.
“Darkwell sends his regards.”

chapters.title: Ghost Fobs and Indian Food
chapters.content:
## Front of CIA Headquarters, Langley, VA 7:11 PM
“Hi, Ms. Cruz, my name is Ben Acevedo. I work for Mr. Hammond. He arranged for your car to be brought over.”
Ben was a dressed in a plain, but expensive-looking black suit with a white shirt, and expensive-looking leather shoes. About 6’ tall, he was smiling like he thought this situation was hilarious, but didn’t want to embarrass her. 
Miranda stared at him blankly, her eyebrow raised. 
“Were you expecting an Uber?” 
Miranda laughed, despite, or maybe because of, the ridiculousness of the situation.
“Thanks. I guess. How did you break into my car and get it here? It better not be hotwired.”
“No, ma’am, Mr. Hammond arranged it.”
“So you ghosted my fob?”
“You’ve had a hell of a day. Would you like me to drive you?”
“Normally, I would tell you to fuck off. But it’s been a bitch of a day, and I don’t feel like dealing with traffic while armed. You better believe if you ever do this again, it will be a different conversation.”
“Of course,” Ben said. Ben moved to open the passenger door for Miranda.
“This was intended as a courtesy.”
“No.” She said curtly, “I’ve got it.” Gesturing at him to go to his side.
Ben moved to the driver’s side and entered. He started the car, and she noticed the seat and mirrors were already adjusted for him.  
Miranda sighed, leaned her head back, and closed her eyes. “I guess you already know where we’re going.”
“You would be correct.”
She leaned the seat back, and closed her eyes. 
“Excellent, I’m just going to wake up from this really weird dream and go back to my boring life.”
They pulled from the forested drive of CIA Headquarters onto the busy George Washington Parkway. 
Miranda noticed that Ben drove in complete silence—no music, no small talk. His eyes tracked everything at once. He reacted to traffic a beat ahead of other drivers, keeping his speed high but controlled.
“You’ve taken a driving course, or two, haven’t you?”
“Eight years in the Army’s Protective Services Battalion.”
“Never heard of it.”
”Not surprised. I hadn’t either until I was recruited for it. Think of them like personal security for the military and associated dignitaries.”
“How exactly does one get recruited for something like that?”
“By taking your First Sergeant’s car in Thailand and driving 8 miles in crazy-ass Bangkok traffic in 14 minutes.”
“So felonies get you special treatment?”
“In my defense,” he said, “she was in labor at the time.
Miranda laughed. She would really like to dislike this car thief, well, not thief exactly, but car-borrower without permission, but it’s not working. She switched gears.
“Have you worked for Hammond long?”
“Four years now.”
“What’s he like?”
Ben focused on the non-existent traffic.
“Ben?”
“Yes?”
“What’s he like?”
“I’m trying to think of the best way to phrase it. Let’s just say, whatever you’re thinking, he’s not. I’ll let you draw your own conclusions when you meet him.”
“I’m getting the feeling there is a lot here people aren’t telling me.”
Ben remained focused on the road. 
“There is. But there is a time and place.”
Ben drove for a few moments.
“It’s not boring.”
Miranda laughed again. Ben might be ok.  
“Hey, I’m starving. There’s an Indian place up here on the right, pull in there.”
Ben nodded, and pulled into the parking lot. He accompanied Miranda inside. The restaurant, Saffron Court, was packed. The smells of curry lamb, and rice mixed with the sounds of a bollywood movie playing on TVs around the restaurant. 
“Miranda! Welcome!” a stately Indian gentleman exclaimed at her arrival. His smile beaming.
“Hi Ravi. Business looks good.”
“Business always good. Chicken vindaloo, extra spicy, extra naan?”
“You know me well, thanks! Oh, a mango lassi too.”
“What about your date, what…”
“Oh he’s not my date! Just some guy that kidnapped me.”
Ravi laughed, “What you like?”
Ben looked like a deer in headlights. “I uhhh…”
“He’ll have the same. You like spicy right?” 
Ben smiled, “Sure.”
Ben moved to pay, Miranda stopped him. “First one’s on me.”
They waited in silence.
After their food came out, they walked to the car. Ben moved to the driver’s side. 
“Nope. I’ve got this.”
Ben stopped, and awkwardly moved to the passenger’s side.
“This is a little unusual for me.” He said. 
“Yeah, well, now you know how it feels.” Miranda smiled. 
The rest of the drive passed quietly. Soon, they were in front of Miranda’s townhouse in Herndon. A red Mercedes S-Class sat in a visitor’s spot.
“That’s you, isn’t it?”
“Yup.”
“Isn’t it a little showy?”
Ben laughed, shaking his head. 
Miranda looked at him, head askew.
“That will be incredibly funny to you by this time tomorrow.”
They exited the car. Ben handed her a nondescript black key fob. 
“Have a good night, Ms. Cruz.”
“You can call me Miranda.”
“Alright. Have a good night, Miranda. We’ll see you tomorrow.”
“Good night, Ben.”
She started up her steps, and stopped to look back.
“Thanks for the ride.”

chapters.title: CrossFit and Gunpowder
chapters.content:
## Miranda’s Home, Herndon, VA 4:43 AM
Miranda barely slept; the sleep she did get was full of dreams of being arrested for various federal crimes at this new job and of Bill dying. 
Her email had an address near Leesburg and a 9 am arrival time. 
Miranda was completely awake, staring at her ceiling. 4:43 am. 
She was long past trying to will herself to sleep, and it was too late for chemical intervention.
She wearily got out of bed. Put on some exercise clothes, grabbed her water bottle and headed out to the gym. 
She made it just in time for the 5 am CrossFit class. This class is always full of hardcore people. The ones who take their workouts seriously and aren’t there to socialize or get a date. She always went to this class, or the 6 am class. They were a little less hardcore in that class, but it still worked for her. 
She pushed her limits for an hour. Overhead presses, burpees, pull-ups, box jumps. A seemingly endless routine of sweat, pain, and lactic acid. Finally, the buzzer went off, signaling an end to this morning’s atonement. 
She didn’t bother drying off too much; the late spring morning air felt good on her skin. She drove home and slowly started piecing together her day. Protein shake, toast with peanut butter, and NPR Morning Edition. She showered, lingering a bit to feel her muscles loosen. She ironed her blouse and pants and threw her jacket in the dryer. She was ready to go. 
7:13 am. 
She had an hour before she had to leave for work. 
She always got to SOD early, 7:30, 8 at the latest. Before that, before Bill, everyone knew she was the first in and one of the last out. She’d be in the office by 6 and still there at 6. Now she had more personal time, but nothing to fill it with. 
She looked around her spartan townhome. Barely anything on the walls, furniture she bought 10 years ago, in her spare room she still had boxes that hadn’t been opened. 
“This really isn’t any way to live.”  She told herself. 
She winced, “I sound like my mother.” 
She grabbed her range bag, her purse, and her phone and headed to the firing range. 
Iron Sights, owned by a former cop, was popular with law enforcement in the area. They opened early, stayed open late, and had great tech on their range. She had a membership, so she moved through quickly and was putting rounds through paper 20 minutes after she left home.
The movements were practiced, precise, thoughtless. 
Put paper up. 
10 yards.
Draw, aim, fire. 
10 rounds center mass.
New paper. 
20 yards.  
Draw, aim. 
Bill’s voice telling her that 20-yard shots were performative. Fire.
Her shot went wide of the target.
“Fuck.” Miranda growls.
Draw. 
Aim. 
Bill laughed as he put up a 20-yard target and made a smiley face like Lethal Weapon.
Her arms started shaking. Miranda took a deep breath. Closed her eyes and opened them, and slowly squeezed the trigger. 
Fire.
9 rounds center mass. 
8:08 a.m.
Miranda merged onto the freeway and headed west. She was moving against traffic for once. The drive was fast, quiet.
She’d looked up the address the night before. What little she could see showed a large house on a wide parcel of land. The main structure was blurred, deliberately so. Two outbuildings. A helipad.
She told herself not to read into it.
The road narrowed as she left the highway. Trees closed in. The houses thinned out.
8:48 a.m.
The gate appeared.
Two guards waited beside it. After a brief exchange and a glance at her ID, they waved her through and sent her up the road.
The drive continued for some time, wide and immaculately paved. The kind of road whose cost was obvious without needing to be explained. The forest pressed in on both sides as she descended into a shallow valley, then climbed again.
8:55 a.m.
At the crest of the hill, the trees parted.
A palatial estate spread out below her.
A fieldstone fence traced the visible perimeter. At the road, heavy iron gates stood ajar. A ribbon of asphalt cut through a manicured emerald lawn, drawing the eye toward the house beyond.
Pale pillars ringed the front of the home.
The manor sat anchored against a backdrop of spring forest—a sprawling structure of ash-colored stone and dark gables. Miranda had expected something guarded. Fences. Cameras. Maybe a modern glass fortress pretending to be invisible.
Instead, the house sat in the open.
Stone. All of it structural. Thick walls in pale, uneven blocks that looked older than they had any right to be, as if the building had been pulled from the land rather than built on it. The façade stretched wide, symmetrical in the way only expensive things ever were. Balanced. Deliberate.
The columns at the entrance bothered her. Too classical. Too confident. White and clean against the stone, like someone had decided centuries ago that this was what authority looked like and never felt the need to revise it.
Windows watched her approach—arched on the lower floors, rectangular above. None of them felt decorative. They felt placed. Measured. Sightlines.
A covered drive cut into the structure itself, not added on. Built to receive people without exposing anyone inside. Even the driveway curved just enough to slow you down, force you to look longer than you wanted to.
She noticed what wasn’t there.
No visible cameras. No guards. No warnings.
That didn’t mean she wasn’t being watched.
Stone griffons flanked the entrance, weathered but intact. Not ornamental. The kind of thing that had stood watch for a very long time and expected to keep doing so.
This wasn’t a home built for comfort.
It was built to endure.
“This is it,” she said quietly. “I guess it starts now.”
A man waited at the entrance as she pulled up, hands folded loosely in front of him. Miranda parked and shut off the engine. The silence settled immediately, heavy and complete, as if the house absorbed sound by design.
He was in his mid-to-late fifties, tall without being imposing, dressed in a dark, impeccably tailored suit chosen for longevity rather than fashion. Not flashy. Not modern. The kind of suit that never went out of style because it never tried to be in one.
His hair was neatly combed, more gray than not. His face carried the calm symmetry of someone accustomed to being listened to, not warmth, but polish. He watched her approach with professional interest, as if she were an expected variable finally resolving itself.
He held a slim leather-bound notebook in one hand, fingers resting on it the way other people rested their hands on weapons, not threatening, just practiced. He looked less like the owner of the estate and more like its steward. Or its gatekeeper.
“Ms. Cruz,” he said, stepping forward as she exited the vehicle. His voice was smooth, precise, and unhurried. “It’s nice to meet you. I’m Martin Wessex.”
He gestured toward the house behind him, “Welcome to Gray Manor.”

chapters.title: We're Going to Bamako
chapters.content:
## Gray Manor
The silence inside the house felt curated.
Not quiet like no one is around, quiet like a library. Footsteps vanished into thick rugs. Doors closed with a soft pressure‑seal hush. Somewhere, air moved, but she couldn’t hear vents or compressors. The whole place was like a “shush” come to life.
Martin walked half a step ahead, not hurrying, hands clasped behind his back.
“The main wing,” he said, “is this level and two above. Family and senior staff quarters up top, formal spaces here. The east wing is guest suites. West is operations and support.”
“Operations,” Miranda repeated. “Is that what we’re calling this?”
“You’ll see soon enough.”
They passed a dining room big enough for a cabinet meeting. A pair of staff in dark polos were resetting place settings from breakfast. movement efficient, eyes lowered, antennae clearly up. One gave Miranda the kind of flicker‑look cops gave new partners: weighing, filing away.
Martin nodded as they went by. “You’ll meet most of the team later today,” he said. “For now, you need bearings more than introductions.”
“What exactly is ‘the team’?” Miranda asked. “I keep getting different answers. Task force, assignment, babysitting gig…”
“A little of all three,” Martin said. “We have in‑house systems specialists, analysts, logistics, medical. External liaison slots for Langley, HSI, and the Bureau.” He glanced back at her. “You’re the first of those to actually turn up.”
“So far,” Miranda said. “You sure you want to show me where you keep the silver?”
“Very sure,” Martin said. “You’ll be expected to use it.”
He stopped at a set of double doors in dark wood, opened them, and gestured her through.
The conference room was narrower than she expected and deeper. One long table, not too glossy. No wall of screens—just a single large display at the far end, currently black, and two smaller monitors recessed into a side credenza. Floor‑to‑ceiling windows looked out over a sweep of forest, morning light filtered green through the glass.
On the far wall, opposite the windows, hung three framed prints: an old city under siege, ships in a crowded harbor, a grainy black‑and‑white photo of a market square.
The market square looked familiar in the way nightmares did.
“Bamako?” Miranda asked.
Martin followed her gaze. “Correct. Preliminary overhead from two days ago, just after the incident.”

“Nice decor,” she said. “Very cheerful.”
“The cheerful art is in other rooms,” Martin said. “This one is for work.”
He moved to a small sideboard, opened a drawer, took out a slim black keycard and a heavier ID badge on a lanyard.
“This is your access credential for the house systems and the lower levels,” he said, handing over the card. The badge followed. “And this is your official identification for the program. Please don’t leave either in your car.”
She turned the badge over. The plastic was heavier than issued DHS IDs, matte instead of glossy. Her photo had been pulled from some government database that caught her between expressions; the legend read:

MIRANDA CRUZ
SA / DHS–HSI
SPECIAL LIAISON – DARKWELL

“Noah likes dramatic names,” Martin said. “We argued for months about that one. He won.”
“How generous,” Miranda said. “Nice to be special.”
“It’s not a compliment,” Martin said. “It’s a warning label.”
Before she could answer, the door at the far end opened.
Noah came in without fanfare, a mug of coffee in one hand, a file in the other. No entourage, no security at his shoulder—just Martin’s quick glance to check him over, like a mechanic listening to an engine.
In person, he looked both exactly and nothing like his file photo. The same silver hair, the same stillness. But the photo didn’t capture the way the room adjusted to him, the small gravitational shift as attention recalibrated.
“Ms. Cruz,” he said.
He didn’t offer a hand; he nodded once. She respected that. She wouldn’t have taken his hand anyway, not without choosing it first.
“Mr. Hammond,” she said. “Quite the Airbnb you’ve got here.”
One corner of his mouth moved. “We decided the ‘rustic cabin’ aesthetic sent the wrong message to visiting dignitaries.”
“Shame,” Miranda said. “I packed flannel.”
Martin cleared his throat, that subtle little cough British men used instead of a memo.
“Noah,” he said. “Miranda arrived on schedule. She’s been read into the basics from Langley. I’ve given her a preliminary tour. She hasn’t seen the lower levels yet.”
“Good,” Noah said. He set the file on the table, flipped it open, and turned the first page so it faced Miranda.
Bamako. Market. Smoke. Bodies.
“You know the broad strokes,” he said. “Chemical release in Bamako Coura. Civilian casualties. No claim of responsibility yet. Your working assumptions from HSI?”
“Not my swim lane,” Miranda said. “But if you force me? Someone with money and patience. Maybe a state, maybe a group playing one. Testing something ugly far enough from Europe and CONUS that most of our people can pretend it’s background noise.”
“And you?” Noah asked Martin.
“Same,” Martin said. “Plus or minus politics.”
Noah nodded once.
“Your President,” he said, “has decided this does not yet merit deviation from ‘the usual way.’”
“Meaning?” Miranda asked.
“Meaning he declined my request to stand up a joint cell here with full access and a clean mandate,” Noah said. “He prefers to let Langley, DIA, and DHS fight over jurisdiction while he waits for a narrative that polls well.”
Miranda snorted. “Sounds familiar.”
“So,” Noah said, “we will do this without him.”
She looked at him. “We who?”
“Us,” Noah said simply. “You. Me. Martin. A handful of people in this house and a few outside it who still remember why they joined the services they did.”
Martin folded his hands. “You were told this assignment would be career‑limiting, Ms. Cruz,” he said. “That part was accurate. The bit they left out is that it might be life‑limiting as well.”
“Comforting,” Miranda said. “You recruiting or trying to talk me out of it?”
“Recruiting,” Martin said. “If I were trying to dissuade you, we’d be having this conversation at your car with the engine still running.”
Noah watched her quietly.
“The clause bought me a hearing,” he said. “It did not buy cooperation. The agencies will send people, eventually. Liaison, observers, cleaners. They will be here to watch, to control, and to report back to their principals more than to help. What we do in the next seventy‑two hours will matter more than whatever committee they stand up in three weeks.”
Miranda looked down at the photo again. The false‑color overlays, the density of bodies in the market square.
“What exactly are we doing in the next seventy‑two hours?” she asked.
“We’re going to Bamako,” Noah said. “Starting tonight.”
Her stomach did a small, involuntary drop. She covered it with sarcasm.
“Great,” she said. “Hot, muggy, possible chemical exposure, and whoever did this still out there. You really know how to show a girl a good time.”
“We don’t have the luxury of distance,” Noah said. “The longer this stays an abstraction, the easier it is for the usual suspects to misfile it as ‘terrorism with unusual characteristics.’ I need someone in country who isn’t already invested in proving their own theory right.”
“And that’s me,” Miranda said.
“You were selected for a reason,” Noah said. “By me, and—after some resistance—by the people who signed your reassignment memo. You’ve worked trafficking and organized crime. You understand systems that treat human beings as expendable units. Bamako is what happens when those systems gain access to new tools.”
“That’s flattering,” she said. “In a deeply depressing way.”
“It means you’ll see things other people won’t,” Noah said. “Or won’t let themselves see.”
He closed the file.
“You’ll have a cover commensurate with your HSI credentials,” he said. “Officially, you’re part of a DHS liaison team on a joint investigation, attached to the embassy. Unofficially, you answer to me while you’re there. Not Langley. Not the ambassador. Me.”
“Sounds like a jurisdictional nightmare,” Miranda said.
“It will be,” Martin said. “We’ll do our best to keep you out of the worst of the bureaucratic crossfire. The rest is above the paygrade of everyone except the two men in this room.”
Noah pushed his coffee mug aside.
“Go upstairs,” he said. “Martin will show you your quarters. Pack for hot, humid, and possibly unpleasant. Long sleeves you can roll, nothing you’d be sad to burn. The jet leaves at twenty hundred. Wheels up no later than twenty‑thirty.”
“That’s…today,” Miranda said.
“Yes,” Noah said.
“Any chance I get a day to, I don’t know, read in, sleep, maybe Google ‘Bamako’?”
“Briefings on the flight,” Noah said. “Sleep when you can. You’ll have a window for calls before we taxi. Use it.”
He stood, the conversation clearly over.
“One more thing,” he said.
She waited.
“When you get there, you will be tempted to treat this like every other assignment you’ve had,” Noah said. “Find the bad guys, build a case, put cuffs on someone, feel a little better.”
“That is kind of the job description,” Miranda said.
“Not this time,” Noah said. “Whoever did this is not afraid of courts. They are not afraid of signatures on indictments. They are testing how far they can go before anyone with real power decides this is their problem. Your job is to help me shorten that distance.”
Miranda held his gaze for a beat.
“Hot and muggy,” she said. “Got it.”
Martin opened the door.
“This way, Ms. Cruz,” he said. “Let’s get you a room before you change your mind.”
“I’m not changing my mind,” she said automatically.
“Good,” Martin said. “It makes the next part much simpler.”
She followed him out, badge warm against her chest, the image of the Bamako market fixed in her mind like an afterimage from a flashbang.

chapters.title: Michelin Stars at 40,000 Feet
chapters.content:
## En-route to Dulles International Airport
The drive from Gray Manor to Dulles would take about forty minutes. Time on the road was moving glacially. The silence in the Suburban’s air was like cold butter.
Miranda didn’t understand the subtext behind the tense atmosphere. She wasn’t sure anyone did, except maybe Hammond. His mood over the whole thing was the anchor weighing everyone else down. Martin told her to bring her weapon; its familiar weight secure in her holster. She’d traveled with her weapon often, flashing her credentials breezed her through security regardless of the metal detector’s whine. Martin had said her credentials wouldn't be necessary, but she'd brought them anyway, tucked in her suit jacket pocket. She felt naked without them.
No one said much. Most of the talking came from Martin, aimed at Noah. Miranda sat back and read her new team like subtitles.
“Confirmed priority departure…crew is aboard…engines turning.”
It sounded more like confirmation of logistics than planning, but the way Martin kept glancing at Noah between updates made it feel like something more. He moved with his usual efficiency, yet the quick looks, the lowered voice, and the pauses before he spoke all leaked unease.
Ben drove the way he had the night before, smooth, practiced, hands steady on the wheel. Almost. Every so often, he hesitated half a second before a lane change, or bit down on his lower lip and caught himself, as if his body were arguing with his training.
Noah’s assistant, Sabrina Walsh, sat behind him. She was about Miranda’s age, maybe 35 or 36. Dark shoulder-length hair, tanned like she spent time outside for real, not hiking because you didn’t know what else to put on your Tinder profile. There was a quick introduction yesterday, nothing more. 
She was on the phone as they got into the Suburban, “Yes, babe, his test is tomorrow. Work on fractions with him; he’s still not getting it. I sent you a video. I don’t know when I’ll be back, but it shouldn’t be too long. I’ll email you when we get to the hotel. I love you too.” she blushed as she got into the car. As she opened her laptop, a YouTube video on teaching children fractions was up. She quickly switched to a briefing. As they drove on, she kept switching between the same two slides without making any edits. 
Noah was the hardest read. His face gave nothing away; he could have been content, furious, or bored. At first.
The micro‑tells betrayed him if you watched long enough. An extra blink here. A brief twitch at the corner of his mouth. A finger tapping against his knee in an uneven rhythm that never quite settled. When he answered Martin, his voice was precise, almost mechanical, as if he’d packed every stray emotion into a box and shoved it somewhere out of reach.
When the Suburban doors finally opened at the private terminal at Dulles, the air hit like a cool wave. Miranda drew a long breath, shook out her shoulders, and started toward the back of the vehicle for her bag.
“Ms. Cruz, this way, please.” Martin’s voice cut across the pavement.
Everyone else was already moving inside. A young guy in a dark suit was unloading the luggage into a golf cart with the casual efficiency of someone who did this a lot.
Miranda hesitated, then left her bag and followed.
Inside, the terminal looked more like a quiet hotel lobby than an airport, soft lighting, polished floors, a single security checkpoint staffed by TSA agents whose eyes tracked every movement like junkyard dogs did not look bored. She watched as Noah, Ben, and Sabrina walked straight past the scanner without breaking stride. No trays, no belts off, no IDs flashed.
“Wait. Martin.” Her voice came out sharper than she intended.
He stopped and turned back to her with the patient look people saved for children who’d wandered off in grocery stores.
“Ms. Cruz,” he said, “please keep up. All of the arrangements have already been made.”
She fished her passport out of her pocket anyway. “But my passport…”
“Has already been provided to the appropriate personnel,” Martin said. “Along with your access credentials and travel documentation.”
Miranda stood there for a beat, passport in hand, mouth half open. She hadn't given him her passport.She slowly slid it back into her pocket and walked after him.
The Bombardier 8000 waited like a predator in white paint, all business class and bad intentions. Once aboard, Miranda saw they’d given a tech wizard a war chest with an unlimited budget and zero adult supervision; then hired a stylist to rein him in. A conference table took up the front section, flanked by wide leather seats and warm teak paneling. Multiple flat‑screens were tucked into the walls and bulkheads, all dark for now but one showing the intended flight path.
Through the cabin window, the jet looked bigger than Miranda expected. Sleek and self‑assured to the point of near arrogance on the tarmac, engines already wound up to a high, impatient whine.
By the time she stepped into the cabin, their luggage was already gone, swallowed by the plane before she’d even had a chance to reach for her bag.
They were taxiing before she could finish fastening her seat belt, and wheels up twenty‑two minutes after they’d rolled through the security gate.
As Virginia fell away under the wing, some of the tension in the cabin loosened a notch. No one relaxed, exactly, but shoulders dropped, jaws unclenched. At least they were moving.
Two flight attendants in understated, expensive uniforms appeared with practiced smiles, setting down drinks only after the captain’s chime signaled cruising altitude. The attendants distributed leather-bound menus in front of each of them. Miranda flipped hers open and had to stop herself from laughing.
Wagyu steak. Fresh sushi. Lobster salad.
Since when was airplane food Michelin-starred?
She picked the steak and asked for the lobster salad as a side. It’s my first time flying private. I’m doing this.
Martin stepped aside, clearing the path to the conference table. “Let’s get started.”
Noah had already taken the chair at the head of the table. Ben slid into a spot with a clear view of both the aisle and the galley. Sabrina settled beside Noah, leather planner and tablet in front of her like a shield. Miranda picked a seat opposite Noah.
Noah reached for a small control recessed into the tabletop and tapped it. A flat‑screen on the forward bulkhead woke up with a soft chime. Sabrina rose and plugged her laptop into the table. 
The Bamako market filled the screen.
First, an overhead shot, recent, after the attack, casualty density mapped in angry reds and oranges, the kind of imagery she’d seen in task‑force briefings but never this raw. Then ground‑level stills: people collapsed on the pavement, others frozen mid‑run, faces blurred by motion or mercy.
Acid churned in her gut, sharp enough to taste. Miranda felt her stomach tighten. She’d seen violence. She’d stood in shipping containers with women who had been sold into slavery. This was different. There was nothing to rage against here. Just bodies and air.
“This is what you’ve seen,” Sabrina said, her voice composed, steady, analytical. “Mali. Bamako Coura Artisan Market. Sixteenth June. Nineteen‑hundred local. I've received information from local hospitals and morgues, mapped cellphone data to bodies, and used weather patterns to pinpoint where the device was planted.”
“We used our social media software to find images and videos taken since the attack.” She flicked to the next slide. Two images of a cell tower. One dated 31 May, the next 17 June. In the June image, a box was clamped beneath the dish, which was not present in May’s image. Scorch marks clearly visible on the tower underneath it.
Sabrina's voice stayed steady as she walked them through the casualty density map, but her hand trembled slightly when she advanced to the next slide. Eight hundred people. She'd cross-referenced every hospital record, every morgue intake, every cell phone that never moved again after 19:00 local. She knew their names now, their faces, some of their stories. These people weren't 679 of 800 to her anymore; they were Mohammad, Aisha, even Trevor, the volunteer from Boise, there to buy his Malian girlfriend a gift for the holiday.
“I passed this to the State Department, they had local CIA assets retrieve the unit. They were kind enough to send us this image.”
The device is shown opened, two large glass cylinders, mixing cylinder, baffle system, and fan.
“As you can see, it’s strikingly similar to the package sent to Gray Manor.”
Miranda interrupted, “What package?”
Noah put a hand up to silence her, “That’s a later discussion.”
Miranda's finger went up, then down.
“Not at all, Miranda. I didn’t know you weren’t briefed.” A pause. “There... was an incident. Something from Bamako.” She glanced toward Noah. “Yesterday, a package with a similar configuration was sent to Mr. Hammond from Bamako. The gas almost killed Noah. Fortunately, it appears to have only been sent as a warning.”
“Holy shit.” Miranda blurted out. She glanced at Noah. No bandages, no pallor, no sign he'd been near death forty-eight hours ago. He sat upright, hands steady, expression calm.
How is he even here?
“Indeed, Ms. Cruz. Now, continue, Sabrina.” Noah’s expression had gone winter-still, the kind of quiet that made conversation feel like it was flat, his eyes boring through Miranda.
“Any lab results yet?” Ben asked.
“Plenty,” Sabrina said. “None that mean anything.”
Martin cocked his head, “Explain.”
She switched to a slide of dense text and charts, CDC, Edgewood, USAMRIID, another half a dozen acronyms Miranda recognized, a few she didn’t.
“The labs found compounds, but nothing that should have killed anyone. No nerve agents, no toxins, nothing in their databases that matches. That's deeply concerning. This could be the perfect chemical weapon. Something that kills quickly and is gone from the environment just as quickly, so that nothing else is contaminated. This would be…”
“Devastating.” Noah interjected, hands steepled in front of his face.
Noah continued, “Government agencies worldwide will kill for this agent. It’s the perfect chemical weapon. Over the next few weeks, they’ll run every standard panel they have. They’ll plug in exotic agents, industrial chemicals, weaponized pesticides, and novel nerve compounds. At the end of it, they’ll publish a highly classified report that says, in careful language, ‘unknown agent, no match to existing libraries.’ At the same time, they'll be scouring the earth to find it; they will not stop.”
Miranda frowned. “You’re saying they won’t find anything? At all?”
“They’ll find compounds, just not any compounds that make any sense combined or separately,” 
“You sound like you’ve seen this before.”
“I have. A very long time ago. It was devastating. We eventually found the source and destroyed it. We thought forever.”
“When was this?”
“Long before I was working with the United States.”
“Where was the source?”
“As I said, the source was destroyed forever.”
“Yes, but where was it?”
Noah sighed. His fingers drummed on top of his hand. Once. Twice. “Where present-day Lake Manatali is. When they constructed the dam, the source was flooded. It’s under 200 feet of muddy water and debris.”
“May I?” Noah gestured to a remote. Sabrina smiled and sat down.
The image on the screen changed. The resolution dropped, replaced with the browned edges and uneven ink of a photographed manuscript. Latin, cramped and dense. A translation ran in smaller text beneath it:
…and a thick cloud, sweet on the tongue and burning in the chest, did fall upon the men of the Third, such that they clutched their throats and died where they stood, though no wound was upon them…
Ben shifted in his seat. “What is this?”
“An account from a Roman campaign near Aquileia,” Noah said. “First century. The historian who copied it assumed the writer was being poetic. ‘Poisoned wind,’ that sort of thing. Historians call it exaggeration, metaphor, artistic license, or bad translation. Obviously, that’s not the case.”
Miranda stared at the words. “You’re saying this is the same gas as the market?”
“I’m fairly certain,” Noah said. “Poison. Delivered from the air. Sweet smell. People choking.”
Sabrina looked closely at the translation on the screen. "How can you—" She paused, glanced at Miranda, then back to Noah. "How can you be sure that it's the same thing we're dealing with here?"
Noah's pause gave Miranda's stomach a lurch. He gave Sabrina a long stare—not angry, but pointed.
"You're right, Sabrina. I can't be certain. But I believe I can find alternate sources that would confirm it."
Sabrina gave the faintest of nods.
Miranda looked between them, eyebrows furrowed. Sabrina and Noah both met her look blankly. 
Noah switched the screen off. The cabin felt smaller without the light.
“What you need to know: this was a test run. They were verifying their prototype works and modeling how much damage they could do. They chose a venue where the world could afford to look away. They will choose differently next time.”
Miranda sat back in her seat. She pressed a button, and a small rectangular table folded out from the wall in front of her. The attendant placed her meal in front of her. The steak was perfect, the lobster fresh, the wine the best she'd ever tasted. She managed four bites and two sips before pushing the plate away.
Eight hundred dead in Bamako. A Roman account from two thousand years ago. A chemical weapon that left no trace that any government would kill for, including her own.
And Noah Hammond, who'd been gassed two days ago and looked like he'd just stepped out of a spa.
She closed her eyes and tried not to think about what she'd signed up for.

chapters.title: Heat and Beige
chapters.content:
## Modibo Keita International Airport, Mali
They landed at Modibo Keita International Airport right on time. The touchdown was so light Miranda wasn't even sure they had landed at first. They taxied to a private terminal near the Air Force's hangars.
Rows of tan and brown camouflaged MI-24 helicopters sat on the tarmac, stub wings bristling with rocket pods, chin-mounted guns angled forward. Next to them, Y-12 cargo planes and single-propeller fighters—A-29Bs, Sabrina said—with drop tanks and rocket pods mounted underwing. Freshly painted, no oil stains on the ground, guards stationed nearby. 
As they pulled up to the terminal, Miranda could see a white canopy, a white canopy, blindingly clean against the dust and smog. Underneath, another black Suburban awaited, the driver already standing outside. 
Mali greeted them before the plane’s doors even fully opened. Heat and damp air rolled in together, heavy and clinging, catching in the throat—the taste of metal left too long in the sun. Every place had its own smell; Bamako’s was diesel exhaust, smoke, wet dust, and the slow breath of the river.
The driver had the doors of the idling Suburban open before they reached the bottom of the stairs. Another loaded bags into the back. The walk from the stairs of the jet to the air-conditioned vehicle was about ninety seconds. Miranda could already feel the sweat pooling in her armpits.
The driver pulled out, Miranda fell backwards in her seat, and grabbed the handle above the window before they were out of the canopy's shadow. The security gate opened as they approached and had already started closing before they were fully out from under it. They pulled out of the airport compound and into traffic that barely qualified as lanes. Motorbikes threaded between trucks with the casual precision of insects. Battered sedans drifted across asphalt as if guided by instinct rather than rules. The city moved in fits and surges, drivers communicating in shouts and blaring horns, stopping without warning, accelerating just as suddenly. Their driver quietly and smoothly changed lanes, accelerated, decelerated, seemingly without losing momentum. Ben sat next to her, smiling. Miranda wanted to hit him. 
“This is insanity. Why are you smiling?”
“He’s good. I’m appreciating a master at work.”
Miranda saw the edges of a smile on the driver’s face.
Asshat. He’s doing this on purpose.
“I’ll take your word for it.”
Noah watched Bamako pass by through the tinted glass. Concrete buildings gave way to roadside stalls, then to stretches of nothing punctuated by rusted signage and faded political slogans. The air-conditioning howled while it fought the city. Sabrina cracked a bottle of water; the plastic snapped loudly in the quiet.
Sabrina laid her phone down on her lap. “We’ll need to get to the location before 1 pm. Thunderstorms after that. Looks like rain on and off the entire week. It’s the wet season here.” She frowned, “Doesn’t make sense.”
Martin turned, facing her, “What?”
“Deploying that weapon now, in the most humid time of the year. It would be better in the dry season. More predictable weather, gentler winds. Why now? Why couldn’t they wait?”
Miranda furrowed her brow. 
“That means one of three things. First, they’re amateurs and didn’t know how these weapons work. Incompetence doesn’t seem to be their thing. Second, There was something about this location, time, or weather that they’re trying to emulate. That’s possible, but it will take a lot of research to see what, where, or when.”
Miranda nodded, “And the third?”
Sabrina took a deep breath, "That they couldn't wait." She met Miranda's eyes. "That's the one that terrifies me. Because it means they're on a clock, and now, so are we."
Miranda felt her palms get sweaty. “Is it always like this?” 
Martin chortled from the seat in front of her.
Sabrina gave her a weak smile. "Not always. But when it happens, it tends to happen like this."
Miranda sat back in her seat, rubbing her temples.
The driver slowed as they approached the hotel. If Miranda had to describe Bamako so far, she'd use one word: tan. The winds from the Sahara bring a yellow-tan haze to the entire city. The dust coats everything—every car tinged with it, every building the same shade. Apartment blocks, high-rise offices, local shops, shanties. All tan.
As they pulled up, a guard post stood just off the street. It was white. Seemingly, the only thing in Bamako that wasn't tan.
The driver rolled his window down, exchanged a few words and some papers with the attendant in Bambara. The heat rolled in immediately. The hotel stood behind, seven stories of rosy beige with the porte-cochère in front. It looked… plain. Almost nondescript. A  bright sign announced its presence, otherwise Miranda would have missed it. As they pulled up to the front, a small army of valets, dressed in burnt-orange button-up shirts and khakis, poured out like ants to greet them.   
Doors were opened, luggage removed and placed on carts, and pleasantries exchanged as they exited the vehicle. They seemed to know Noah. Miranda caught the word "Hammond" several times. Noah spoke Bambara with them. The language flowed like water. Miranda could hear Bonjour, and a few other French words, but that was all she could hear as the words blended and rolled out like a bolt of silk. 
They entered the hotel, and whatever her impression of the outside was, the inside was like walking into another world. Marble floors, abstract African art, and wooden paneling around marble columns adorned the lobby. Soft piano music came from the bar area, and multiple languages could be heard as diplomats, NGO representatives, government officials, and their interpreters huddled around the leather chairs and tables set up in the common area. The ozone smell of air conditioning pushed to its limits permeated the air, along with the delicate smell of hundreds of orchids spread throughout the lobby. 
Miranda was quickly summoned to check in with a cheerful associate. Sabrina tapped her on the shoulder and pointed to the TV behind the desk.
A local news show was on with English subtitles. MALI MARKET DEATHS LINKED TO PESTICIDE SPILL – SOMAPRA COMPANY UNDER INVESTIGATION.
The footage cut to a press conference. A man in a suit, a Malian government official, gesturing calmly. The subtitles: "…no evidence of terrorism… industrial accident… full investigation underway…"
Noah had appeared behind them. His expression didn't change. Noah gestured at the screen.
“The Romans had a rule.” He looked at them, “What must be hidden is explained first.”
"See?" he said quietly. "The cover-up begins."
Miranda stared at the screen. Eight hundred people. A pesticide spill.
"That was fast," Ben muttered.
"It always is," Noah said.

chapters.title: The Spectre
chapters.content:
## Ivans Monumental Apartments, Fortaleza, Brazil, June 15, 10:12 pm 
From thirty floors up, the tower looked like what it was: alabaster concrete, imported glass, arrogant curves flaring white against the night. Not Fortaleza. Not anything that belonged here. Just another monument to wealth and ego. From the street it was impossible to miss—thirty-plus stories shadowing the beach like a sentinel, every unit facing the Atlantic.
​The apartment sat high above the sand, glass‑walled and lit by the city’s amber glow, the ocean sprawled out like a map. Clean lines: polished concrete, white walls, . A low leather couch faced the water. Not for guests. For watching. For thinking. The balcony doors were open, letting in warm, salt‑heavy air from the Northeastern Brazilian shore.
