The wall of screens pulsed behind a short Brazilian woman in a Pokémon halter top and a green cap with the flag stitched on it. Blue‑white light carved her face into angles. Her fingers moved over the mechanical keyboard with factory‑line precision , each keystroke calculated, executed, forgotten. A flicker on her C2 dashboard. The Trojan she’d planted last week phoned home. One window. Maybe thirty seconds. She tunneled in, Burp Suite mapping the attack surface, traffic bouncing across three continents, a privilege‑escalation exploit unrolling in one terminal.
​Her secure phone rang. She glanced at the number. Thirty seconds, maybe less. She let it ring. It rang again. Same number. Pushy client. If the money weren’t so good, they’d already be blocked. But it was useful when they learned she wasn’t exclusive. Third call. She hit return, watched the payload commit. Package delivered. Then she answered and tapped the voice scrambler.
“Yes?”
A synthetic male voice, clipped and flattened, “When we call you Spectre, we expect you to answer.”
To the world, Silvana Carolina da Silva no longer existed. She had erased herself years ago; only her family and her priest still remembered the name, even though none of them wanted to. Her life lived in fragments now. Tthe lease on this apartment belonged to Lívia Moura, the Audi in the garage to Talita Corrêa, the servers to Renata Vilar, the wallets to ten more aliases. Skins, nothing more. She was Spectre. When she thought of herself at all, that was the name that fit.  
“If you don’t have an appointment,” she said, voice flat, “you’re not guaranteed the first ring. Now stop whining and tell me what you need.”
Silence. Long enough she checked the connection. Still live. So that was how he wanted to play it. Fine. She could wait.
“We have a job,” the voice said at last. “I’ve sent a secure email. This needs to be handled immediately.”
Of course it did. They always did.
“Understood. You know my rates. I assume the retainer’s been sent?”
A single chime from the encrypted wallet.
“Very well. I’ll leave in the morning.”
“There is a jet waiting at Pinto Martins.”
She hesitated, a bead of sweat breaking on her forehead. Her actual location was not something she advertised. Her actual country wasn’t even something she advertised. How the hell did they find me? She took a quick breath before the silence was noticed, “I’ll need three hours, I have preparations to make.”
It was their turn to pause, “Acceptable.” The line went dead.
She looked around the apartment. If they knew her city and airport, this place was burned. Merda. I really loved this place too.
She stared at the terminal. The job specs were already decrypted: name: Noah Hammond, location: Bamako, Mali, Azalai Hotel. She looked at the photo, Male, 40s, touch of silver in his hair, tanned skin. Attractive, if she had been into that thing at all. She hadn’t had sex for anything other than operational reasons in years. Never for enjoyment, she didn’t see the point in it. 
Noah was just another unlucky soul. He'd obviously done something bad to warrant powerful people wanting him dead. She'd killed dozens like him. This one wouldn't be any different. 
This job would be up-close. She opened her pistol safe and grabbed her custome Ruger Tactical MK IV pistol, Red Dot sight, integrated suppressor, and subsonic ammo. She also grabbed a few extra toys she would need. 
She walked to her server racks, flipped a plastic cover, and pressed the button underneath three times. The wipe script was running now—encryption keys deleted, drives overwriting themselves with random data. It would take hours, but she wouldn't be here to see it finish.
She pulled the two most critical drives from their bays, dropped them into a Faraday bag. She'd destroy them properly once she was clear. She had multiple backup locations, but she had to assume these were compromised too. No matter, everything would be sold. Pennies on the dollar to be sure, but she already had plenty to start over again. 
She used to clean, mechanical, detached. Lately, the names felt heavier. She touched the gold crucifix at her throat. Habit, nothing more. the little bit of comfort she got from this work was that it made the lives of others better, even if she was damned. 
The church in her hometown got anonymous donations; they’d just added a new school building.Her old family got gifts and expenses paid for by anonymous donors. They had to know where it was coming from, but never made any efforts to look for her. They also never returned them. 
She opened a new email, typed in a nonsensical combination of letters and numbers at a Gmail account, and put one word in the subject line, “Curitiba.” Done. 
That would start the process of selling assets, burning identities, and restarting her life. She looked at her gun collection. Not small, but not American big. All of them dipped in blood. She loved her weapons. They were the closest she would ever get to children, but she also hated what they represented. 
She logged in her laptop to the old folder she pretended she didn’t need. Her sister’s quinze anos. Blue dress, too much tulle, happiness cheap and bright. Their parents had saved for months to buy that joy. She kissed two fingers and pressed them to the photo. “Sinto muito. Tenho tanta saudade de ti.” 
I’m so sorry. I miss you so much.
“I’m still good at this,” she whispered. 
It sounded like a sales pitch, even to herself.

chapters.title: Trouble at the Market
chapters.content:
## Bamako Coura Artisan Market
Ben eased the B6 armored Land Cruiser up to the outer cordon and let the V8 engine idle at a low growl. Blue tape and sawhorses blocked the road ahead. Beyond them, the skeleton of the Bamako Coura market sagged in the heat, half‑open, half‑ruin. Tarps hung slack. A few vendors sat on crates at the edges, watching the uniforms and the charred patches of ground with equal suspicion. The rest of the stalls further into the market were toppled, broken, or torn.
Two gendarmes and a city cop stood at the checkpoint, rifles slung, sweat darkening their collars. They looked tired, underpaid, and their eyes tracking every movement of our gear cases, fingers drumming against rifle stocks like they were calculating exchange ratespainfully alert to opportunity.
“No official plates, no embassy car, no letters of introduction,” Miranda said. “We’re security consultants, caught in the fallouttourists with bad timing.”
“Tourists don’t come here now,” Martin said. “Ben?”
Ben rolled forward, stopped just short of the tape. Martin stepped out into the heat, jacket on, hands visible. Ben joined him a second later, just close enough to be read as backup.
Miranda watched in the side mirror.
Martin led with French. “Bonjour, messieurs. Désolé de déranger.” Shoulders rounded forward, palms turned up like he was carrying something fragile. His voice dropped to the register of a man asking directions from a stranger’s doorstep Polite, deferential. He flashed a laminated card that looked vaguely like an NGO credential and didn’t quite give them enough time to read it. “Nous sommes avec une organisation médicale. On veut comprendre ce qui s’est passé ici. L’ambassade n’a pas encore tout autorisé, mais il est peut‑être déjà trop tard pour les preuves.”
Martin translated: “We’re with a medical NGO. We want to understand what happened.” The embassy hasn’t cleared everything yet, it might already be too late for evidence.
The senior gendarme gave the card a cursory glance, then looked past it to the car, to Noah in the back seat, to the foreign‑looking equipment cases stacked behind them. His expression said he’d seen this maneuver before—foreigners, urgency, no paperwork.
“C’est zone fermée,” he said. “Pas pour les étrangers.” 
Closed area. Not for foreigners.
“Of course,” Martin said. “We understand. It’s just…” He let the sentence hang and shifted his body slightly, enough that the officer could see the edge of the envelope in his hand. “Nous n’avons pas beaucoup de temps.” 
We don’t have a lot of time.
The cop’s eyes flicked to the envelope. A small, domestic calculation clicked into place. He scratched his cheek, looked at his colleagues, shrugged.
“SiIf quelqu’un demande,” he said, “je dis que vous avez poussé.” 
If someone asks, I say you pushed.
“Bien sûr,” Martin said. The envelope changed hands. The tape lifted.
Ben drove them through.
The market had the hollowed-out feel of a place where something vital had been carved away
Scorched patches mottled the packed dirt. Some stalls had been stripped completely; others still held half‑sold goods. Small plastic markers where bodies had been. Someone had tried to wash the worst of it away. Not all of it. A faint, sweet‑spiced smell lingered under the stench of bleach, death, and sun‑baked garbage. Sabrina walked slowly, like she was in a cemetery. She stopped occasionally and looked longingly at some detail no one else could see. 
Official forensics crews had clearly been and gone. Bits of tape still clung to the walls. Numbered evidence flags lay trampled in the mud. Empty swab packets glittered underfoot.
Noah didn’t look at any of it for long. He was looking up.
“There’s the tower” he said quietly. 
“Miranda followed his gaze. A cell tower rose above the market, with its metal lattice and dishes pointing out over the city. It wasn’t the only infrastructure around, but it was the tallest thing near the densest chalk circles. The device was gone, but the scorch marks, like demonic claws, on the tower reminded them of what had been there.
Sabrina walked by, her phone to her ear. “Martin, Miranda, we’ve got company.”
Martin looked up at her and nodded. 
“10 o’clock, building with the red windows. Roof. Two men.”
Miranda pulled out her phone like she was taking a selfie and shot a photo with the building in the background.
“Not the least classy selfie I’ve ever taken.”
Sabrina snorted. Martin’s hand went to the bridge of his nose, fingers pressing hard against skin like he could squeeze out whatever he’d just heard. His eyes went flat, the kind of stare that measured you against every disappointment he’d ever filed away.
Miranda huddled with them. They looked at the image zoomed in.
“There they are. Who do you think they are?”
Sabrina whispered, “White guys, watching us specifically? Not DGSE, the French don’t know we’re here. The only ones who reasonably would know and care are from back home. That means…”
“CIA,” Martin whispered.
“I thought the CIA was helping us?” Miranda snuck a glance at the building.
Martin looked at her, “Parts of the CIA are helping us.” He surreptitiously pointed his thumb over his shoulder at Noah, “Other parts want us to fail.”
Ben walked up to them. “Is this like 6th-grade recess? Am I not one of the cool kids again?”
Sabrina put her arm around Ben, who looked confused, and said quietly, “CIA is watching us.” She stopped to pick up a newspaper on the ground.
His face turned serious immediately, “Oh. I see.”
Sabrina was looking at the newspaper, one of the thousands of bits of trash that had accumulated in the market in the days after the attack. “Bamako Aujourd’hui, this didn’t turn up in my OSINT search.” She started looking through it, and stopped, studying intently. Miranda couldn’t see exactly what she was looking at, and her French wasn’t the best, but she did see the title at the top of the page, “Death Notices.” There were pages and pages of them. Sabrina seemed to be studying each one. “That name is new.”
Martin looked at her, “Find something?”
“Maybe, do you read French?”
“Afraid not.”
“There’s an death notice here, Moussa Diarra. Died on the day of the attack in a helicopter crash.”
Noah had joined them, “I’m not seeing anything we didn’t already see.”
Sabrina handed him the paper and pointed at the death notice.
“That’s awfully coincidental. Do we have anything about a crash? I’m assuming not since you hadn’t mentioned it.”
Sabrina flinched just a little, like she’d been zapped by static electricity. “No. This newspaper seems to be small and hyper-local. I’m guessing it doesn’t have a digital presence.”
She looked at her phone, “Looks like its offices are just a few blocks away. Maybe they have some info on the crash.”
Miranda whispered, “What about our friends?” thumbing at the building behind them.
Martin smiled, “We won’t do anything. If they don’t follow us, they’re just watching the market in general. If they do…”
“Then we know they’re here specifically for us.” Ben finished.
Sabrina started walking towards the Land Rover, “Let’s go.”
They all got into the Land Rover.
“There’s our answer.” Martin said, gesturing at the building.
The watchers were gone.

chapters.title: Thunderstorms and Newspapers
chapters.content:
## Bamako, Mali
Ben pulled up about a block away from the offices. Thunder was already rumbling, and people were quickly gathering up what they could and getting inside. Ben walked over to a pair of teenagers sitting just inside the store next to where they parked. He gestured, eyes, then vehicle, American Dollars were exchanged. The boys didn’t smile or look excited; they just nodded. 
They walked, single file, through the thinning streets as thunder rumbled across the Niger River from them. Rain began to pelt them. Slow at first, then a deluge as a faucet seemed to turn on. A brief flash as lightning crashed nearby. Thunder boomed loud enough to shake windows. The streets were empty now. They stayed under awnings and were mostly dry, but no one had thought to bring an umbrella.
“How much further?” Martin asked Sabrina.
“Around the corner.”
Martin smiled, “The bright side is that we know our friends won’t be watching from rooftops, and they’re not going to be able to sit in a car and watch.” 
The rain was coming down in sheets, the streets were becoming deltas as brown water and trash flowed downhill away from them towards the river. They stopped at the last awning, which was threatening to burst any minute from the hammers of rain falling on them. 
Noah walked to the front of them, “Are you all afraid to get a little wet?”
Miranda scoffed, “Nothing little about how wet we’re going to get.” She ran to the newspaper's door and threw it open. 
Noah smiled at her.
The rain stopped. Not a gentle slowing down, just…gone. In the space of less than a minute. 
Miranda, hair pasted to her head, clothes as wet as if she had jumped in the river, threw her hands up to the sky, “Oh come on! You have got to be fucking kidding me.”
None of them could help but laugh, even Miranda joined in. 
A young Malian came to the door, towel in hand. 
“Puis-je faire quelque chose pour vous, madame?”
”Is there something I can do for you madam?”
“Parler anglais?”
Speak English?
“A little” the man replied. 
Miranda pointed to herself, “My name is Miranda.” She introduced the rest of them in turn. 
Sabrina came to her rescue, “Avez-vous des anciens numéros? Nous recherchons des informations sur un accident d'hélicoptère survenu la nuit où tout s'est passé au marché.”
Do you have back issues? We are looking for a story about a helicopter crash from the night everything happened in the market.
“Oui, he gestured to them to come in. “Je m'appelle Oumar Sidibé. Je suis le rédacteur en chef du journal.”
My name is Oumar Sidibé. I am the editor of the paper.
Sabrina and Oumar talked for several minutes. Miranda looked around the paper's offices—spartan, to be sure.
The main workroom was barely larger than a two-car garage. Concrete floor, scuffed smooth by years of foot traffic. A ceiling fan rattled overhead, oscillating with a low, uneven hum that suggested it had been threatening to quit for years. The windows were open, metal bars across them, letting in the damp post-rain air and the faint smell of diesel from the street.
Three mismatched desks filled the space—metal legs, laminate tops scarred with cigarette burns and scratches. Plastic chairs, some cracked, some with cushions tied on with twine, were scattered around them. Behind a half-wall partition at the back, a slightly better chair marked the editor's claimed territory.
Two ancient desktop computers sat on the desks, their towers humming loudly, fans working overtime in the heat. One monitor had a dead pixel line running down the center. Keyboards were missing letters—the "E" on one, the "S" on another. A single inkjet printer perched on a small table against the wall, its tray half-open, a ream of paper jammed inside at an angle.
Paper was everywhere. Stacks of previous issues leaned against the walls. Proof pages covered in red pen marks sat in precarious piles on the desks. A corkboard on the wall held handwritten schedules, phone numbers scrawled in multiple inks, a faded press credential, and one official-looking government notice curling at the edges.
Ethernet cables snaked across the floor, taped down in places, loose in others. A router was duct-taped to the wall near the ceiling, its lights blinking weakly. Power strips and extension cords formed a tangled jungle under the desks, one surge protector doing God's work to keep everything running.
On a shelf near the editor's desk, a small radio played softly, tuned to a news station in Bambara. The air smelled faintly of paper, ink, cigarette smoke, and old coffee. It was the kind of place held together by duct tape, determination, and the certainty that if the power went out—and it would—someone already knew where the breaker was.
Ben was watching the street. Martin was thumbing through old papers scattered around. Noah stood near the back, staring at Oumar with a distant, unfocused look, not listening, just... elsewhere. After a moment, he sat down heavily and rubbed his eyes and temples.

He beckoned Martin over and whispered something in his ear. Martin's expression didn't change, but he nodded and moved to the window, positioning himself where he could watch both the street and Noah.
"Something wrong, Noah?" Miranda asked.
He startled, blinking like he'd forgotten she was there. "What? Oh no. Just a headache." He tried for casual. "Dried off?"
"Not even close." Miranda smiled, but kept watching him. That wasn't just a headache.
Sabrina returned with a printout. “Here’s the article.” She handed it to Noah, who translated.
### Pilot Killed in Helicopter Crash Near Monzou
Monzou — A helicopter crashed approximately 12 kilometers east of Monzou late Monday evening, killing the pilot, Moussa Diarra, 42, of Bamako.
The aircraft, a Soviet-era MI-8 transport helicopter, went down in a remote agricultural area around 19:45 local time. Local residents reported hearing the aircraft's engine before the crash, followed by an explosion and fire.
Authorities have not yet determined the cause of the crash. Malian Civil Aviation officials stated that weather conditions were fair at the time. Mechanical failure is suspected.
Diarra, an experienced pilot and independent charter operator, was the sole occupant of the aircraft at the time of the crash. He is survived by his wife, Aïssata, and three children.
An investigation is ongoing. The wreckage has been secured by local authorities.

Ben interrupted, “Heads up! Our escorts are looking for us.” 
Two men in polo shirts, one green, one brown,  were walking the streets outside, trying very hard to make it look like they weren’t looking for someone. They glanced at the newspaper office and kept walking down the street. 
“Did they see us?” Noah asked.
“Absolutely.” Sabrina stated, “They’ll circle back around shortly and split up on two different corners.”
“Right. Well, we don’t want to lead them to this, do we? I saw we head back to the hotel and prepare for the morning.”
They all thanked Oumar and walked out. 
Miranda looked back. Martin was talking with Oumar and writing something down. The shocked expression and smile on Oumar’s face said it was good news, whatever it was. Martin shook his hand, and joined them outside. 
Miranda walked close to Martin and spoke lowly, “What was that about.”
Martin’s eyebrows raised, “What? Oh that? Noah wanted to reward the man for his help. If we hadn’t found his paper, we wouldn’t have found out about the crash. Since he didn’t have a digital edition, Noah gave him enough money to get his paper a digital edition.”
Miranda smiled, “That was good of him.”
Martin nodded, “Let’s catch up to the others.”
Miranda picked up her pace. She looked across the street.. Green shirt was talking on a phone, pacing them.

chapters.title: The Exterminator
chapters.content:
## Bamako, Mali
They drive back to the hotel in silence punctuated by a few unimportant questions. Miranda spent the time looking out the windows trying to find their CIA observers. She was unsuccessful. They agreed to head up to their rooms, shower and change, and meet back at the bar in two hours.
Miranda peeled off her still-damp clothes and melted into the hot water. The heat sank into her muscles, washing away the rain, the dust, the tension of the day. Martin had booked executive suites for all of them. Hotel rooms bigger than her first apartment with large beds, views of the city, and separate workspaces. Definitely a step up from the standard government rooms she was used to.
She had changed and was doing makeup when there was a knock at her door. Ben was there, holding a black wand device in his hand. Before she could ask, he held up a small index card: MAKE NORMAL CONVERSATION. She nodded, eyebrows raised. She invited Ben in. 
He started waving the wand around. “Hey Miranda, I was just coming to check on you. Wanted to see how you’re holding up.” It flashed red immediately at the lamp near the door. Miranda cocked her head. Ben pulled a small silver disk and placed it in a Faraday bag like the one she was given at the CIA. 
“That’s sweet. I’m fine. A bit jet-lagged. Not used to travel like this.”
Ben furrowed his brow as the wand blinked red again, “Yeah, no one really does.” He pulled a screwdriver from his pocket, opened the electrical socket, and pulled another, different device from the plug connection. He kept walking around the room, grabbed the chair at her desk and brought it under the smoke detector, “What’s your impression of everything?”
“It’s been an experience, that’s for sure. I’m not used to this kind of stuff.” Miranda mouthed to him, What is going on here?
Ben held up one finger, while he removed the smoke detector, the wand didn’t blink red, but he still pulled out a small camera. He grabbed a strap of some kind and wrapped it around the speakers on her television. “There, now we can talk.”
“Was someone bugging me?”
“Hard to say. This hotel is used by a lot of people from a lot of different places, so those may have just been here as standbys in case someone some agency wanted to know about stayed in the room. The camera wasn’t active, but that doesn’t mean they couldn’t turn it on later. The CIA would use the speakers on your TV and not even bother with bugs, so the strap should make that a lot harder for them.” He handed her a small blue device that looked sort of like a smoke detector, it didn’t have any cords.  “Don’t use the hotel’s WiFi, use this. The codes are on the bottom. Turn it off when you’re not using it. It’s fully charged, if you need a new one, just ask me, and I will get you a backup, but it should be fine for the entire stay. Unless you’re watching a ton of porn.” He smiled. 
Miranda laughed, “I save it to my phone so I don’t have to go looking for it.”
It was Ben’s turn to laugh, “I like a woman that plans ahead.”
They smiled at each other. Ben went to go out the door, and stopped, “I forgot, the hotspot can be a little tricky the first time you try to log in with it, have you ever used a phantom network before?”
“A phantom what? Just show me.” She grabbed her laptop from her bag, logged it in and handed it to Ben. “How long you think we’ll be here?”
Ben started the WiFi access, “Dunno. We could leave tomorrow, or a week from today. You never know when we go on these things.”
“This happen a lot?”
“Sometimes yes, sometimes no. We can be gone for months at a time, or not leave for months at a time.”
“Makes dating…”
“Fucking impossible!” Ben laughed.
"Good thing my mom isn't ready to be a grandma yet." She smiled. "Or maybe she is, and she's just polite enough not to say it. Nevermind, she’s Puerto Rican, that’s genetically impossible for her."
Ben laughed, “Speak for yourself. My dad is asking for grandkids every time I call. My mom tells him to shut up, that I’m working and I’ll get to it when I’m ready.” Ben talked her through the process for logging in. It wasn’t nearly as difficult as he made it sound. “Alright, you’re all set. See you downstairs in a few.” "Alright, you're all set. See you downstairs in a few." He walked out and closed the door.
Miranda smiled to herself. Ben was good at his job. And funny. And not bad to look at. She shook her head and went back to her makeup. Focus, Miranda.

chapters.title: Somewhere I Belong
chapters.content:
While Mali is a majority Muslim country, the bar at the Azalai hotel, though small by Western standards, was well-stocked and luxurious. The walls behind the bar were arranged like large square shelves, lit with bright white lights, and stocked with bottles from around the world. 
A French woman and her diplomat husband sipped wine in the corner. A local woman drank a cocktail with some sort of star-shaped fruit on the rim. NGO workers drank beers from various nations. Bambara, French, English, all mixed into a sweet pastry of words that gave a comforting sound to the bar.
The five of them sat at a table together. Noah with his scotch, Martin with an old-fashioned, Ben with a local beer, Sabrina with a glass of white wine, and Miranda with the same beer as Ben. 
Miranda observed the team like a stakeout. They all traded stories, unfamiliar names, places, but the beats and rhythms were much the same as those she’d seen agents tell, as she used to tell. They had obviously spent a lot of time together. 
Sabrina took a call, “Hi sweetie. How was the test? 86! That’s great!” Ben was trying to get Martin to watch a funny video, he did, but didn’t get the joke, which exasperated Ben. Martin started laughing, Ben looked at him, “I knew you thought it was funny!” Noah smiled, but was focused on everything else around them. The bartender, the hallway, or the pool they could see from their table, seemingly anything but the table in front of him.
Sabrina moved close to Miranda, “My kids want to see who I’m with, take a selfie with me.” She held up the phone, took the photo, and looked at it, “Perfect. Thanks.” She went back to her phone to send the photo, typing a short message. She looked at Noah, “You asked me to remind you about running payroll.”
Noah started, “I did? Oh, I did. Thank you for reminding me.”
“No problem, it’s in your email.”
Noah opened his email, went to the drafts folder and opened the email titled “Payroll.”
CIA observers are behind us in the photo.
Noah opened the photo, a dark-haired woman and two men in button-up shirts with khakis. Their drinks barely touched. He deleted the email.
“Thank you Sabrina, looks good to me.”
“I’ll let them know to run it.” Sabrina looked at Miranda, “You’ve been awfully quiet.”
Miranda’s cheeks flushed, and she shrugged, “Sorry. I just don’t know you guys very well.”
Sabrina smiled, “Well, then get to know us.” She leaned towards Miranda, “As you have heard, I’m sure, I’ve got two kids, Avery, she’s 14, and Lincoln, he’s 8, and having trouble with fractions, at least until today. I’ve been married for eighteen years. My husband, Peter, works at the NSA. I used to work at the CIA. We met in Istanbul when I was on an assignment. We tell people that we could tell people how we met, but we’d have to kill them.”
Sabrina’s smile was earnest. “I’ve been with Noah for eight years. Best mistake I ever made.”
Martin raised his glass. "I'll drink to that. “Catherine used to say the same thing about me, that I was the best mistake she ever made."
Miranda noticed the wedding band on his finger. “How long have you been married, Martin?”
Martin looked down and twisted the gold band on his finger. Noah put a hand on his shoulder. 
Miranda’s heart sank. Oh God, can I leave this dimension, please?
“It would have been 32 years next month. She passed three years ago. Breast cancer. But we had twenty-nine glorious years together. We have two amazing children. Bryce, you might have seen him around the Manor, and Sarah, she’s in school at Stanford.”
The silence stretched. 
Miranda winced, “I am so sorry, I just…”
Martin smiled, “Not at all, Miranda. I enjoy speaking of her. She would have liked you, I think. If she were here, she’d probably be trying to set you up with Bryce.”
Sabrina laughed, “Martin, they’d already be engaged!”
Miranda laughed, “He is pretty cute,” she murmured. 
Martin and Sabrina both laughed till they had tears in their eyes. 
Their appetizers came to the table. Chicken and lamb skewers with a delicate sauce, fried pastels scented with spiced meats, charcuterie, and spiced river shrimp. Miranda’s stomach growled. 
When was the last time I ate? The plane?
Miranda unabashedly shoved a shrimp in her mouth. “How long have you been doing this Noah?”
He exhaled in exhaustion, “Long enough that I’ve watched gratitude turn into expectation. Expectation into resentment.” 
He met her eyes.
“Nations age faster than people think. And they forget even faster.” Noah looked down, avoiding eye contact, “It’s hard to want to help, and be told to shut up and color.”
I can relate.
Noah pointed at the skewers, “Ben, if you could pass them, please.” As he was passing them over, Noah said, “Ben has had quite an adventurous path.”
Miranda’s eyebrows lifted, “Really? What about you, Ben? Where’s home originally?”
He smiled, “Stockton, California.”
“Really? California? Not where I would have imagined.”
“I get that a lot. People say I have a New York vibe.”
“I’m from Queens. I wouldn’t call it a NYC vibe. Maybe… Topeka?”
Everyone laughed.
“What’s Stockton like?”
“There are a few things to know about Stockton. The Cops TV show has a full-time crew there, because we’re some of their best material, and apartments give away bulletproof vests with a new lease.”
“That bad?”
“Not all of it, not really. It’s also getting better. My parents worked hard to make sure that we didn’t live near those places. My dad is a janitor at the University of the Pacific. My mom is a Dental Hygienist. They made sure my sisters and I got a good education, and there was always food on the table. I played football in High School, Wide Receiver, had a scholarship to Berkeley. Played half a season, blew out my achilles and lost my scholarship. On the bright side, I did play long enough and well enough to be in the NCAA video game!”
“So how does a jock from Stockton end up in the Army and then here?”
Ben chuckled, “It’s a ride. After I lost my scholarship, I joined the Army. GI Bill, you know? Figured I'd do my time, get back to school. Ended up going infantry, because they had the shortest wait. I served for 4 years, crossed over to Military Police. Got stationed with a battalion that was doing a stint in Thailand for an exercise with their military. My Staff Sergeant was pregnant, 7 months. She was due to fly back to Germany to deliver the baby the next week. Her husband was already there. We all go out for dinner after work one night. She orders the spiciest pad thai on the menu. Like, the kind where the waiter looks at white people weird and warns them twice. Twenty minutes later, contractions. Her water breaks in the back of a tuk-tuk on the way to her apartment. We’re in the middle of Bangkok, it’s 8 pm on a Friday. The streets are packed. I grab her keys, throw her in the car, and haul ass for the nearest hospital.”
“Holy shit! Did you make it in time?”
“5 minutes to spare, she delivered on the way to the room. Baby boy. Curtis Benjamin Stokes. Future president's name.”
Sabrina laughed. "I'm just impressed you didn't pass out. Peter almost fainted when Avery was born, and he was just watching."
"Did you at least get a medal or something?" Martin asked.
Ben grinned. "Better. I got a bottle of bourbon, a thank-you card with a tiny handprint, and the honor of having a future president with my name as his middle name.”
Martin smiled as Ben continued, “From there, I was reached out to by a Lt. Colonel with the Protective Services Battalion. Three months later, I was in training. Two years later, I’m driving this Noah guy around in Qandahar. We’re going all sorts of weird places. Dodging IEDs, Taliban, even our own people. When my time to reeenlist was coming up, I got a call from Martin. The job offer was in my inbox when he called. I said yes, and here I am.”
“So what’s your story, Miranda?” Sabrina asked. 
Fuck. I should have seen this coming.
“I’m an HSI, Homeland Security Investigations, agent. I worked mostly on human trafficking and trade-based money laundering. I had an… incident almost two years ago. Actually,” Miranda looked at her watch, “18 months, 5 days, and sixteen ago, East Coast time.”
She sighed, tears welling in her eyes.
Sabrina spoke, nearly a whisper, “You don’t have to talk about it.”
Miranda waved it away gently, “No. It’s ok, you’ll find out eventually anyway, might as well get it out of the way.”
“We were busting a human trafficking ring. Three a.m. raid on the Baltimore waterfront. We got into a shootout. My partner and I had a miscommunication, and…: I accidentally shot him.”
A tear fell down her cheek. She quickly wiped it away. 
“He was my best friend and mentor.”
Ben placed his hand on top of hers. Miranda flinched for a second, but relaxed. “I am so sorry for what happened to you, and…”
“Bill.”
“Bill. It wasn’t your fault, I’m sure of that. Things like that. I’ve heard the stories from other soldiers. It’s the worst kind of luck how things can happen. I’ve seen people drink themselves to death over things like that. The fact that you're still here, still standing. That says everything I need to know about you.”
Who’s chopping onions here? I’m not chopping onions. Get in control.
“Thank you, Ben. That means a lot.”
She looked up to the others at the table. Sabrina’s eyes held hers for a moment. She nodded her head slowly. Martin smiled at her. Caring, almost fatherly. Noah’s eyes looked deep into her, and he gave a slight nod of respect. 
Miranda’s heart felt like she was falling from the top of a roller coaster. She couldn’t remember the last time she felt anything close to the “us” she felt with her fellow agents. 
Miranda stood, her legs were shakier than she expected, “If you guys will excuse me, I need to go powder my nose.”
She walked to the bathroom. 
At the table, Sabrina watched Miranda leave and walk around the corner. She also saw the dark-haired foreign woman follow just a few seconds behind her. She leaned in to Martin, “The CIA just followed her into the bathroom.”
Martin nodded, “Let’s see if she flops or flies.”
Miranda stood in front of the mirror, touching up her lip gloss. The door opened, and she barely noticed the woman standing next to her.
“Hello, Miranda. The Director would like to know why you’re shirking your responsibilities.”
Miranda dropped the gloss into the sink. “Who the fuck are you?”
“I’m the one running the team that’s been chasing you all over Bamako in the heat and rain all day long.”
“What do you want?
“Your questions aren’t the ones getting answered here. Tell me why you and your team are in Bamako. Why are you going to the market? Why that crappy newspaper? The editor said you were looking for old death notices. Your arrival here isn’t coincidental to the atta… pesticide spill.”
Miranda’s eyes darted left and right in rapid strokes; her mouth went dry, she almost dropped her lip gloss again.
She said team. That’s your team.
“I'm here to remind you what happens if you don't deliver.”
“I…We…”
She crossed her arms, “Go on.”
“We were looking into the attack.”
“Pesticide spill.”
Miranda put her hand up, shaking her head, “We didn’t find anything. Then Noah wanted to go to that newspaper for some reason. I just got here, they’re not sharing a lot with me.”
“Where are you going tomorrow?”
“Nowhere as far as I know. They haven’t told me.”
God, I hope I’m as good a liar as I think I am.
“I heard someone say something about touring the countryside, but I don’t know if it was everyone or just them. It was the black guy and the older guy, Martin.”
The woman nodded, “You’re lying. But, no matter.” She held out her hand, a small silver disk, the size of a watch battery, was there. “Take this, and put it under the seat in your vehicle.”
Slowly, Miranda took the disk, it felt like aiming at Bill, finger on the trigger.
As she took it, the woman grabbed her wrist and brought her close, almost eye to eye, her voice dropped octaves, “Don’t fuck me on this. You do, and I will make sure Langford knows. I didn’t get sent out to this shithole to watch you assholes for nothing.”
The door opened, the woman’s face changed in an instant, her voice sounded like a cheerleader, “Oh sweetie, no worries, I get lashes in my eyes all the time, I can see it, hold still.” She put her finger close to her eye, put just an ounce of pressure on it, and whispered, “We’re not going to forget about you.” Instantly, her voice returned to cheerleader speak: “There you go.”
“Tha..Thanks.” Miranda said weakly.
“Cheers!” The woman waved over her shoulder as she exited. 
Miranda put her head down in her hands on the sink and started to cry, silver disk still in her hand.
At the bar, the rest of Noah’s team sipped their drinks and waited. The CIA agent left and immediately walked towards the hotel’s exit. Miranda was nowhere to be found. 
Ben looked at Sabrina, “What does that mean?”
Sabrina kept looking at the bathroom area, “Hard to say. She made contact, that’s for certain. Her leaving either means she got what she needed or she didn’t. We’ll have to see how Miranda looks and acts to tell.”
Miranda came out a few minutes later, eyes red, hair somewhat mussed up.
“Fuck.” Sabrina whispered into her hand. She looked at Noah. He wasn’t saying anything; he had that thousand-yard stare he sometimes gets. 
“It will be ok,” Noah stated, as sure as the weather outside.
Miranda walked up to them, “Hi guys, I’m sorry, I think I need to call it a night.”
“Not at all, Miranda. Get some rest, we’ll see you at breakfast in the morning.” Noah’s voice was friendly, even caring. 
“Thanks. Good night.” Miranda turned and walked to the elevators. 
Martin waited until the elevator doors closed, “So what now?”
Sabrina sat back in her chair, “The rest of the CIA observers left.”
Ben looked at her, “What does that mean?”
“That they don’t need to have eyes on us to know where we are anymore.”
Noah looked at all of them, “We knew this was likely to happen. We planned for this. You all know what to do next.”
In her room, Miranda cried again. She lay on her king bed, with a mattress better than her mattress at home, under 1000-thread-count sheets, still in her clothes. The lights were off, but the TV was on, voices quick and foreign.  The air conditioning was off, but she still felt cold. She stared at the silver disk lying on the nightstand, TV light reflecting off it. It felt like a gun in her mouth

chapters.title: The Traitor’s Dilemma
chapters.content:
Miranda’s alarm screamed at her. She didn’t remember falling asleep, but exhaustion must have taken over at some point. The TV was still on, different people, different images, still incomprehensible. Her room was thick with stale, humid air. A sheen of moisture, like sweat, was on the mirrors and glass. Her clothes were soaked with sweat, and the sheets had a prominent wet spot. She turned on the air conditioning and zombie-shuffled to the bathroom. Her reflection looked like she’d gone five rounds with a grizzly bear and lost. She turned the water scalding hot and tried to wash away the last eight hours. She wrapped herself in a towel and put her makeup and clothes on like armor.
As she got ready to walk out the door, she looked at the bedside table, willing reality to change. The silver disk was still there, daring her to defy it. She walked over to the nightstand and looked at the disk. So small, so… harmless. She moved to pick it up, recoiled, like a snake bit her. She flipped off the disk with both hands, “Fuck you.” She looked at the ceiling and television, still flipping them off, “Fuck all of you.” She put her hand on the doorknob, took a deep breath, and walked out the door. 
She made her way to the elevator. A man was there waiting, looking at his phone. The doors dinged and opened. Miranda looked at him to get in first. 
“Oh, I’m waiting for someone.”
Miranda walked into the elevator.
“Oh, miss, you dropped something!”
Miranda patted herself down and looked at her purse. 
The man held some cash and put it into her hand; she could feel the weight and density of the disk in the middle. “Try not to forget this one.”
“Wait! What?”
The doors closed.
Miranda felt tears welling in her eyes, her stomach full of acid, her heart racing. She unwrapped the disk from the bills and shoved it deep into her pocket. The cash she threw on the floor. She took calming breaths. They didn’t help much. 
A well-dressed man here on business or diplomacy, and a woman in a hotel uniform, entered on one of the floors. The woman looked at her, as if to ask if she was ok. Miranda studiously ignored her. The woman turned around and faced the door.
She exited the elevator just in time to see Ben emerging from the one next to hers. 
Oh, just fucking wonderful.
“Morning!” His voice was full of energy and cheer. This man was obviously a morning person, and right now, she hated him for it.
“Hey Ben,” she almost growled. Ben looked at her askance.
Get in control.
“Sorry,” Miranda said more normally, “I really didn’t sleep well.”
“I…can tell. You need some fuel. The breakfast buffet here is fantastic.”
“How do you know?”
“I already ate, after I worked out. But I want some more of those beignets. They’re fantastic. You’ve got to try them.”
Ben’s exuberance over pastries made her smile, which only made her stomach acid bubble over.
She followed him to the restaurant. She could see Sabrina and Martin, already at a table. “No Noah this morning?”
“He usually eats alone. I imagine he’s working right now. He’s either on the phone, typing an email, or doing whatever billionaires do in their hotel rooms alone.”
“Huh…”
“Yeah, his hanging out with us last night was rare. He does it sometimes, but he likes to keep himself separate from us most of the time.”
Ben grabbed four beignets and moved on to the table, while Miranda fixed herself a plate and sat down. After the initial greetings, the table was silent. Sabrina looking at something on her phone, Martin reading in his notebook, Ben playing a game on his phone. 
Do they know? Oh god, they know. 
Miranda picked at her food. The taste was excellent, but her stomach couldn’t handle anything. She had hardly eaten since yesterday. She should be starving, instead her pants felt a little looser. Everyone ate mostly in silence, with occasional small talk. Ben’s silence in particular was unnerving to Miranda. She hadn’t known him long, but quiet was not a word she would use to describe him. The easy camaraderie was gone from yesterday. A wall of silence had been erected in its place.
She looked around. The scene here was similar to the bar last night. A blond American woman she could hear speaking French with a local official of some kind, Men in traditional robes conferred nearby, African men speaking a language she'd never heard were laughing together. Hers was the only table that was silent.
Miranda left to throw away some leftover food and grab a coffee. As she walked to the trash can, her hand went to her pocket. The disk was there, small and cold.
They already know. Look at them, silent, distant. The camaraderie from last night is gone. They KNOW.
What's the point? Plant it, and I'm a traitor. Don't plant it, and the CIA comes after me. Either way, I lose.
But at least if I throw it away, I'm choosing MY loss. Not theirs.
She pulled the disk from her pocket, covered it with a napkin, and dropped it in the trash.
Her hand was shaking.
At the table, Sabrina was observing Miranda, she texted Noah, “She dropped the tracker.”
“Thumbs up emoji.” Then, “Leaving in 15.”
The rest of the meal was quiet until Martin got a call about 10 minutes later. 
“Time to head out. Noah will meet us at the car.”
They all gathered their things and headed out. As they were moving past the area in front of the kitchen, one of the staff slipped on the floor and fell. He hit the ground hard, dishes and glasses shattered, silverware thrown out like a grenade blast radius. The entire hotel seemed to stop for a moment. Several people closeby moved to help. Miranda and the team moved to help, picking up silverware from the floor nearby. 
No one noticed the blond woman walk by and drop a disk into her purse.

chapters.title: Afternoon Prayers
chapters.content:
They pulled out from the hotel and headed east towards Monzou. The Land Cruiser felt heavy on the road, but still more agile than it should be, like a fat antelope. North through the center of the city, and then south across the river. The bulk of the city was actually on the south side of the Niger River. Traffic pressed in on them like a weighted blanket. The horns and swearing felt accusing to Miranda.
Her hands were still shaking. She told herself it was coming down from adrenaline, but that felt disingenuous. Even though she felt… lighter after relieving herself of the last silver relic. The whole thing felt like she'd been thrown in oil.
Noah was already in the front passenger seat, reviewing something on his tablet. Ben drove, Martin took the back row, Sabrina and Miranda in the middle seats.
"Where exactly are we headed?" Miranda asked, trying to sound normal.
"Monzou," Noah said without looking up. "About two hours northeast of the city center."
Ben navigated through the chaos. Motorcycles weaving between cars, street vendors pushing carts loaded with fruit and phone cards, women in bright fabrics balancing impossible loads on their heads. Gradually, the city thinned. Buildings gave way to scrubland and occasional clusters of mud-brick homes.
Sabrina opened her tablet,. "I've got the latest imagery pulled. Commercial satellite passes from the day of the attack and the two days after."
"Anything?" Noah asked.
"Maybe. There's a debris field about fifteen kilometers outside Monzou. Could be the crash site, could be an old vehicle graveyard. Hard to tell from this resolution. Plus it’s Synthetic Aperture Radar, SAR, it’s a series of white dots and blobs in a field."
Martin leaned forward between the seats. "What's the confidence level?"
"That it’s worth looking at? One-hundred percent. That it’s our crash site? Seventy-five percent. This site wasn’t here before the attack. The scatter pattern is consistent with a crash. But I’m not an imagery analyst, and even if I were, I’m looking at it on an iPad mini"
Martin sighed, “Nothing other than that?”
“Afraid not. Unfortunately, the fields east of Bamako aren’t in high demand on the commercial imagery market.”
Noah swiped through images on his tablet. "I guess we’ll find out when we get there." He started typing in the nearest location for the navigation. Ben thanked him.
Miranda stared out the window. Traffic had thinned greatly. The landscape was changing now. Drier, more open. Baobab trees rose like ancient sentinels from the red earth.
"What do you think we’ll find at the crash site?" she asked.
"Proof, I hope." Noah said. "Moussa crashed we know this. The weather story is a fabrication. I looked up the weather in that area around the time of the crash this morning. I also looked up Moussa’s history. Ten years in the Malian Air Force flying MI-24s and MI-8s. Another ten years flying charters for everyone from the Ministry of Agriculture to the CIA. He was well-known as a competent and skilled pilot. He wasn’t the type to just crash and his maintenance record was spotless. Why did he crash? Who was with him? No one seems to have a record of who, where, and why he was flying that night."
"And if the CIA's already been there?"
"I don’t think they have been. In fact, based on some information I have, I don’t think they knew much of anything until last night. But if they have already been there, then we find out what they took."
Noah’s glance at Miranda, sent shivers down her spine. 
How does he know?
The silence settled again. Miranda forced herself to look out the window.
A thousand feet above them, a DJI Matrice drone zoomed in on their Land Rover, stark white against the cacophony of rust and faded colors of the native vehicles. A red dot blinked on the screen, marking the vehicle in case they lost visual. Noah's voice came through the feed, muffled but clear: "Then we find out what they took."
Alyssa Pompeo, the dark-haired agent, smiled at the screen, blonde wig still hanging out of her backpack on the ground behind her. She turned to Quentin, her drone operator, “Quality is better than I expected.”
He kept his eyes on the screen, knee bouncing nervously, “The cell repeater on the drone is boosting the signal off the towers nearby. Once they get out of cell tower range, we may lose it.”
“That’s fine, we’ll still know where they’re going. Show me the chase vehicles.”
The drone’s camera swiveled as it hovered in place. In the distance, two blue dots were visible on the horizon. 
He took a long swig from his water bottle. His voice came out hoarse,“They’re five kilometers behind them. They’ve got the encrypted, frequency-hopping radios for comms with each other and us like you requested.”
He lazily pointed at the radio operator to their right. Everyone called him “Bones.” Alyssa didn’t know why, didn’t really care. This wasn’t her normal team, so she made do with what she had in-country. This was the fourth time she'd reviewed everything with them, and the first time she hadn't found some critical error.
Bones wasn’t paying attention, he had a headset on and was focused on the screen in front of him. Alyssa tapped him on the shoulder. He flinched like he’d been shocked. 
“Tracker signal strength?”
Bones had some sweat on his brow, his mouth opened and closed a few times, “Um… good. strong. The spread-spectrum trackers are usually pretty reliable.”
“Good. That’s our last one. I’m gonna strangle that bitch if I see her again.” She turned around, “Quentin, how long to Monzou?”
“They’ve got at least an hour.”
“Good. I need to report in. Get me when they’re close or if anything unusual happens.”
Alyssa walked out. Quentin and Bones looked at each other, and breathed out deeply.
They pulled up to the area where the imagery led them. Stripped luxury car shells—BMW, Mercedes, Land Rover all sat in neat rows, mocking them in the sun. Barely husks of their previous glory.
Sabrina sighed, “I guess that answers that.”
Miranda looked at her, “What is this?”
“Car thief gangs. They steal a bunch of cars around the same time, get them out of the city to some place remote, strip them for parts. They’re gone within a few hours of dropping the cargo.”
“Then the timing…”
“Purely coincidental.” Martin interrupted.
“Then what’s next?”
Noah turned to Sabrina, “Pull more imagery. While you do that, we’re heading into the town of Monzou. I can’t imagine a helicopter crash out here wasn’t heard by someone.” They entered the Land Rover, and started off the dusty road, following that sign that said Monzou 25.
The actual town of Monzou was barely a speck on a map. Low cinderblock walls surround many of the buildings, large sets of solar panels ring the outskirts, a modest mosque sits in the center of the town. It seemed like half of the village was on their way inside. 
The Rover brought a cloud of dust with it as it stopped near the mosque. Noah looked at his watch, “Perfect. Right on time.”
Miranda cocked her head, “For?”
Noah gestured toward the mosque, “Afternoon prayers. The entire village, or most of it will be here.”
Sabrina nodded, “Impressive. We can get the entire village at once. We’ll make an operator out of you yet.”
The haunting sound of prayers in Arabic sounded from the top of the mosque where two men sang them. Noah watched the men sing the prayer, wonder in his eyes, “I remember the first time I ever heard that. It was as haunting and beautiful then as it is now.”
Allāhu akbar, Allāhu akbar
Lā ilāha illā Allāh
The call to prayer ended. The actual worship was silent. The town was quiet, except for the soft winds and High, thin tsee-tsee of a local bird. 
Less than ten minutes later, people started filing out of the mosque. Sabrina and Noah started speaking to them in French. Miranda couldn’t understand the words much beyond “hélicoptère”
Finally, a woman seemed to know what they were talking about. She gestured to a man, who came over and spoke with them.
“Dans les champs,” he’d told Noah. In the fields. He pointed Southwest.
“Vers l'endroit où se trouvent les grands pylônes.” Toward where the big pylons are.
The Land Rover bounded away from Monzou’s dusty village center to the Southwest.
In the back seat, Noah balanced his phone on his knee. The screen glowed faintly in the hard daylight. “Sabrina, any imagery out that way?
Sabrina squinted at her tablet, moving the image on her screen with her fingers. “There! Found the power lines.” She copied a set of coordinates and switched applications. More SAR imagery appeared on her screen. She pinched to zoom, then followed the black line of the transmission corridor as it cut past the village and out into the fields. For a while, it was just crops, rectangles of different texture and tone, irrigation channels like veins.
Then there was a scar.
A pale gouge carved into the darker green, the shape irregular but directional: a swipe of bare earth, a dark knot at one end where something had burned. The power lines stepped neatly past it, straight lines continuing their march.
“Got you,” Noah said quietly.
He tilted the phone between the front seats so Ben could see.
“Can you get us there?” he asked.
Ben studied the image, then the landscape ahead, then the angle of the pylons relative to the road.
“Yeah,” he said. “With these roads, we're about 30-60 minutes out."
Back at the CIA office, Quentin called Alyssa.
Is that even really her name? Cyber-bitch seems more appropriate.
“What is it?” She demanded, walking in. 
“They’ve stopped. We have no audio. She left her purse in the vehicle.”
Alyssa watched as they gathered outside the mosque.
“What are they doing?” Bones had come over to watch.
Alyssa sighed, “They’re pinging the locals for intel.”
“What does this mean?”
“Just follow them. Keep out of sight. I’m looking something up.” 
Alyssa went to her desk in the other office, brought up the imagery from the past couple of days for the area. Unlike the commercial imagery her prey was using, she had high-resolution electro-optical imagery. She plugged it into the new AI system they were using, gave it parameters, comparisons between the last pass before the attack and today, and asked it to show me anything 10 meters or larger. She waited as the system processed. Ten images on the screen. Only one clearly showed a helicopter wreck. She printed it and went back. 
Alyssa handed the still-warm laser print of the wreckage to Quentin. “How far is our chase team from these coordinates?”
Quentin entered the coordinates into Google Maps, and the chase team’s, “They’re an hour out.”
“Send them, now.”
Bones communicated to the team. On the screen, Alyssa watched as the dots stopped, then changed direction. She turned back to watch her prey.
Ben turned off the main road onto a narrower one, then again onto packed dirt that threaded between low fields. Women bent over seedlings looked up as they passed. A boy paused his donkey cart and watched them with wary curiosity.
The acrid stink of burned fuel clawed its way into the car before the wreck came into view.
Ben eased the car to a stop on the edge of a shallow depression, killed the engine, and let the heat rush in. For a moment, nobody spoke.
The helicopter had slammed into the earth. The main fuselage lay on its side, blackened and gutted, ribs exposed. The tail boom had snapped off and lay twisted several meters away. One rotor blade had scythed a path through the crops, leaving a fan of flattened stalks. The rest lay in jagged pieces.
No tape. No guards. Just the husk and the circle of trampled earth where officials and curious neighbors had once stood and then left.
“Looks like somebody already did the first pass,” Miranda said, stepping out into the glare. “Took what fits in pockets—wallets, watches, phones.”
“And left anything that didn’t,” Sabrina said. “Look, bodies in the back. Four of them.”
Martin looked inside, “So he wasn’t alone. Any ideas who these guys are?”
“I’ll try to do some pocket litter surveys of them and see what comes up. If the CIA was cooperating, we could run DNA samples. But since they’re not.”
“We’re out of luck.” Martin finished.
Noah stood at the lip of the depression, hands in his pockets, reading the crash site. Rotors sitting up like totems in the dirt, debris scatter radius fanning west, impact crater centered on the pylon base where fuel had blackened the evidence of impact: a gouge through crops, soil churned to reveal darker layers beneath
“MI-8” he said. “Enough cabin space for six, maybe eight, plus gear. Lost power maybe?”
“Or got helped down,” Ben said.
“Or that,” Noah allowed.
They slid down into the crater. Metal groaned under their boots. The heat coming off the blackened fuselage was the old, dry kind, not the fresh sear of an active fire.
Miranda spotted the Pelican case first.
It sat half‑wedged under a collapsed section of hull, one corner jutting out from under a strip of torn aluminum. The plastic was blistered on one side, soot‑streaked on the other. It had the stubborn, indestructible look of gear that had been designed to survive minor nuclear wars.
“Hold up,” she said, pointing, “What’s that?”
Ben helped her drag the twisted sheet metal away. The case came free with a scrape and a puff of black dust. It was about a meter and a half long, about a meter wide. 
The lid was scorched, but stenciling showed through the grime.
VANGUARD CHARTERS.
Block letters. A stylized V with wings underneath.
“Vanguard,” Ben read. “Not exactly Air Mali.”
Sabrina joined them, eyes already tracking the stenciling.
“Don’t open it yet,” she said. She raised her phone, took a series of photos—logo, latches, burns, the wreck in the background for context. Then she nodded. “Okay. Let’s see what we’re dealing with.”
The latches gave with a resistant clack. Miranda lifted the lid slowly.
Inside, the foam was custom‑cut. Two long parallel cavities ran down the middle, each with precise recesses at one end for fittings and mounts. The shapes were perfect negatives of the twin‑cylinder device they had taken from the market tower.
Miranda whistled, “Well,” she said, “that’s subtle.”
“It’s a match,” Sabrina said. “Same length, same profile. They flew the device in this.”
She took more photos, working quickly, angles neat and methodical. Each shot caught the empty cutouts like missing teeth.
Noah stepped closer. For a moment, his expression didn’t change. Then his hand arrested its motion, suspended in the crater's heat shimmer like a gesture interrupted by memory.
“Vanguard Charters,” Martin said, looking up at him. “Ring any bells?”
Miranda spoke, quiet at first, then cleared her throat. “They’ve had other names,” he said finally. “Vanguard’s one of them. Aviation, maritime shipping, and trucks. Logistics. At least on paper. I’ve dealt with them on human trafficking and trade-based money laundering cases.”
Noah shut the lid. Gently. As if not to wake something.
“This wasn’t some one‑off atrocity,” he said. “This device wasn’t cheap. You don't spend that kind of money on a single stunt. This is deliberate. Someone’s been refining this.”
He looked back at the broken helicopter, then out across the fields toward the distant smear of the city they’d left.
“Someone trusted Vanguard to move their system,” he said. “
Miranda hefted one end of the Pelican case. Ben took the other.
“Let’s get this into the car,” she said. “Before whoever lost it decides to come looking.”
“Wait!” Miranda stopped at the driver’s door.
“What is it?” Noah looked up from his phone, 
“The man at the mosque.. He said ‘J'ai entendu les hélicoptères.’”
“Yes.”
“LES hélicoptères. Plural. If this one crashed, how did he hear two? No hills or mountains out here to make echoes.”
Noah didn’t move immediately, then he pointed around, “Let’s spread out. There was another helicopter here. Maybe we can find something.”
They spread out and methodically searched the terrain. Ben found it about 80 yards from the wreckage.
“Over here. Looks like tire tracks in the field.”
Pale earth scored the darker field in a straight line, recent enough that the millet stalks still bled sap where they’d been crushed. The track ran parallel to the power lines for fifty meters, then veered sharply west. Tire treads crisp in the clay, deep, aggressive. Someone in a hurry. Someone who didn’t care about leaving tracks. Miranda looked at the long trail, “So he heard propellers and mistook the sound for a helicopter?”
Ben nodded, “If you’re not around them all the time, they would sound similar.”
Miranda looked around, “This is pretty clear from here. Why didn’t we see this on the satellite?”
“Low resolution pass. Three-meter resolution, this is well below that.”
Sabrina looked at the tracks, “Three rows of two tires, widely spaced. Rough field, but still a short takeoff. Maybe a C-130 or AN-12? I’m not an expert. But it looks about the right size.”
Miranda kneeled on the ground, picked up some dirt, grit scraping against her skin, each grain sharp as glass, the heat baking into her fingertips until the dust felt like powder from a fired weaponfeeling it run through her fingers. At the disturbed site, Miranda separated the earth with surgical precision, noting how the top inch had been raked smooth to hide deeper gouges. Someone had worked to erase what signs they could.
Miranda wiped her hands on her slacks, “Two years ago, when we were looking into them, Vanguard Air, one of their subsidiaries, bought a pair of brand new C-130Js. It raised some eyebrows. It was an over $100 million purchase for a company with less than a tenth of that in revenue. It was all legit, money borrowed and all that, but we never saw them used for anything.”
Ben pointed at the road, "We've got company coming."
Two white Land Rovers were coming quickly down the road parallel to them. They would soon be able to intercept the crossroad.
Sabrina sighed, "CIA found us again."
The sky above the fields was a flat, merciless white, the kind of glare that erased shadows and made distance impossible to judge. Miranda looked at the sky, "How? They weren't following us on the road. I don't see any drones."
Sabrina looked at Miranda knowingly, "That’s a great question, Miranda. How did they find us? We weren’t followed. Closely at least, which meant that they knew exactly where we were at all times. There’s probably a drone overhead right now, watching our every move. Which they only could have done if they had a tracker in or on the vehicle." Sabrina looked at Ben.
”Not me, I swept the car before we left.” 
Sabrina nodded, “I know.”
She turned back to Miranda, arms crossed, “Anything you’d like to tell the class, Miranda?”
Miranda looked around, and she saw no allies. Martin had his arms crossed, looking at her disapprovingly. Ben’s mouth hung open, his eyes wide. Noah just looked… bored.
“I, I.” Tears came to her eyes. She felt nauseous. 
Sabrina cocked her head, her look was almost pity, “Let me fill in the details. You were in purgatory, all of the sudden you get summoned to the CIA, and some shadowy person tells you that you need to report on what’s going on here. You agree. Then you start working with us and forget to check in. That dark-haired woman approaches you in the bathroom and tells you to plant the bug. You agree. You do a performative ‘I’ll ditch this thing’ at breakfast, to make us think you’re on our side, meanwhile, you kept one in your purse.”
Miranda put her hands up, “No, that’s not it! I mean, yes, but not all of it. It was the Director, Langford, who told me. They did approach me, but I left it in my room, then they gave me another one at the elevator. I threw it away too! It’s not me! I swear.” She started turning out her pockets, “You can check my purse.”
“I did,” Ben said as he was looking through her purse. Then, in the outside slim pocket, he pulled out a small silver disk." Ben held the disk between two fingers, frowning. “It’s warm. It wasn't just sitting in your bag, Miranda. It’s been harvesting energy from the induction coil in your phone to keep the transmitter running. As long as your phone is charged, this thing never dies. This is really advanced stuff.”
Miranda burst into tears, through hyperventilating breaths, “...not mine. I…how it…”
Sabrina observed silently. Ben had tears welling in his eyes. Noah was still examining the wreckage.
“Langford you said.”
Miranda, still sobbing, shook her head yes. 
“Interesting. Interesting that he did it himself.” Noah was pacing near them. The Land Rovers were only a half mile from the intersection that would take them here.
“Let’s assume Miranda is telling the truth for a second.”
Sabrina looked at Noah, confused.
“If she didn’t do this on purpose, when could she have been unknowingly co-opted?” 
“The fall,” Martin said, louder than he intended.
Sabrina turned to him, “What?”
“The guy who fell at breakfast. They put it on her when we were cleaning up after that guy fell.”
“What makes you say that?”
“I believe her. She’s either the greatest actress in the world, or she really didn’t know. Think about it, where was her purse when we were in town?”
Miranda’s eyes lit up, through sputtering sobs, “I left it in the car.”
Martin gestured, “You did, I remember seeing it in here. I thought it was odd because women never leave their purses, and I saw yours on your seat. If she were actively spying on us, she would have brought the purse with her. Sabrina, you’re the experienced one here. What’s your gut telling you?”
Sabrina hesitated, eyes never leaving Miranda. Her pause and look made Miranda feel that she was reliving every bad thing she’d ever done. Finally, Sabrina turned away, “Fuck.” She kicked a rock, “I believe her.”
Noah approached them. “Are we quite finished? We need to leave before our friends arrive.” He grabbed the tracker from Ben as the rest of the team went to the vehicle.
In the office, Alyssa screamed, “I told you to keep the fucking team out of sight. What the fuck kind of amateur hour are you running here?”
Quentin and Bones both flinched, their shoulders curling in as they tried to protect themselves from the verbal lashing that felt physical.
“They thought they couldn’t be seen from there.”
“They thought?” She screamed, “They thought?” She slammed a notebook on the desk, “Because of these idiots, the source is burned. They found the tracker. God-damnit”
Noah’s voice came over the speaker, “Hello, CIA. Thanks for the entertainment. We wish you nothing but the best.” A dull noise, then, static.

chapters.title: Going to Confession
chapters.content:
The drive back to the hotel was a millennium. She felt their stares like brands on her skin. Martin and Ben wouldn't meet her eyes. Sabrina and Noah spoke in low voices in the front seat, words she couldn't quite hear. Miranda just laid her head against the window and watched the world change. The voices around her melded together, and then everything was black.
A hand on her shoulder jolted her awake. Sabrina was leaning over from the front seat. They were at the hotel. Everyone else was walking inside, leaving the two of them alone in the heat that was creeping into the car. The sun was setting, but Mali didn't give up so easily. A peal of thunder rolled across the city, low and threatening.
From a third-story window, unnoticed by the team, a dark-haired woman watched through small binoculars as Noah, Ben, and Martin exited the vehicle and made their way inside. She brought her phone up, and looked at the image on her screen, and compared it to the man she was walking into the hotel. Her target had arrived. The hunt started tomorrow.
In the back of the Land Rover, Sabrina looked at Miranda with an intensity that legitimately scared her.
"Talk. All of it. Now."
Unlike earlier, Miranda didn't have tears welling in her eyes or nausea rising in her throat. This felt more like going to confession. She took a deep breath and started talking.
Bill's death. The trial. SOD. The meeting with Langford in that sterile office. The bathroom, the elevator, and throwing away the tracker at breakfast. The relief she'd felt, thinking she'd gotten away with it. The shame when Ben pulled the disk from her purse.
She held nothing back. Not even the parts that made her look weak, or stupid, or complicit.
Sabrina listened without interrupting. Her shoulders dropped. Her jaw unclenched. By the end, she looked merely like a disapproving Catholic school nun, rather than a serial killer.
"Langford's a bastard," Sabrina said finally. "He knew exactly what he was doing."
Not absolution. But not condemnation either.
Miranda slumped against the seat. She had nothing left. No secrets, no energy, no fight.
But she'd been believed. And that was enough.
Lightning crashed across the river, and the roof of the Land Rover was pelted by rain that started as just a few drops but became a machine-gun staccato of water. Sabrina said nothing. It was nighttime now. Miranda wasn’t sure how long they’d been here she was afraid to look at her watch. They were both sweating, a little breathless. They just sat there, quiet, while the rain punished the roof of the car.
The rain finally slowed. Sabrina sat back and crossed her legs. “They’re not going to stop. You’re burned on this op, but you’re still an official member of the team, which we’re not allowed to get rid of unilaterally.” 
She wiped her brow, “The only thing that matters is, are you witting or unwitting.” 
Sabrina leaned towards her, “For the time being, you’re a bit of both. You’re caught between us and them.”  
Sabrina handed Miranda her purse, it felt lighter than before. “At some point, you’ll have to choose. That choice will affect the rest of your life. It will change how well you sleep at night. You’ll wonder if you made the right one. The answer should be simple.” 
She sat back again, “The answer will determine if you are a trusted and valuable member of this team, or dead weight.” Sabrina turned around, opened the door behind her, and walked into the light rain, leaving Miranda sweating inside the car.
The choice should be simple, Sabrina had said. But nothing about this felt simple.

chapters.title: Shopping and Murder
chapters.content:
Everyone met again for breakfast at 0700. Miranda silently thanked God that the CIA had not bothered contacting her again. It also worried her. 
What does this mean? Did they give up on me? Are they going to do things different now?
Miranda checked her pockets, the lining of her suit jacket, her shoes, everything that was on her body; she was as sure as she could be that there were no trackers. She left her purse in the room, just bringing her passport, weapon, cash, and a credit card. Her badge sat on the bedside table, impotent. It bought her no currency here, only liabilities. She still stared at it for some time, picked it up, lay back on the dresser, closed it, and walked to the elevator. 
The man from yesterday was gone. She almost thought he would be there again. Her ride to the main floor was in an empty elevator. Her head was on a constant swivel the entire walk to the restaurant. She looked around, but didn’t see anyone from the team. It was early still, 0648. They would be here soon. 
As she sat to eat, she realized she was starving. They had eaten a little on their jaunt yesterday, but not much. She had gone straight to her room and to sleep yesterday. She mentally counted. She hadn’t had a real, full meal in three days, just bits here and there. Her body was screaming for calories and water. She piled her plate with porridge, eggs, bread, fruit, anything within reach, and drank two glasses of water before she even started eating. 
As she gorged, she tried to look around at the other patrons. All the faces were unfamiliar yet familiar. Was that the man from the elevator? No, wrong nose. The woman from the bathroom, she would never forget. But she didn’t see her anywhere. The only one she recognized was the man who fell yesterday. He seemed to be just doing his job and ignored her completely.
Maybe that was really an accident? When did they plant it then?
Sabrina was the first to arrive.
Fuck. Of course, it's her.
"Good morning, Miranda." Sabrina's tone was cheerful, friendly. Miranda's blood pressure rose.
Why is she acting as if nothing happened?
"Hi, Sabrina." Miranda's tone was careful, unoffending.
Sabrina sat down with a small plate of food and started eating. "I see our friends are back."
"Huh?" Miranda looked around, trying to spot what Sabrina was talking about.
"You didn't see them? Not surprised. Back corner of your right shoulder, and another far corner of my right shoulder. The one behind me is alone, female, dark-haired. Coffee and a plate she hasn't touched. The ones behind you, two men, polo shirts, one Black, one white. Meant to look like NGO workers, but they're both in special-forces shape, armed, and wearing tactical pants. We're meant to notice those guys. The woman is the actual watcher."
Miranda tried to spot the woman in her peripheral vision. There. Back corner, dark hair, coffee cup. She still wasn’t seeing what Sabrina was.
The rest of the team showed up soon after. Sabrina dutifully informed them of their surveillance.
Martin asked quietly, “What should be our plan?”
Sabrina waved her hand, “Too noisy and obvious in here for a microphone, don’t be obvious, but you can talk normally.” Miranda relaxed slightly. She'd been holding her breath, afraid to say anything. 
Sabrina noticed and gave her a small nod, ”We’ll lose these guys. I worked with Damon back home and got some good intel on where we should be going next. The market is back open today, we’ll go do some shopping.”
Martin looked at his phone, “Noah just texted. Leaving in 15.”
Ben shot up, “I’m getting some beignets for the road.”
The dark-haired woman watched from the corner. The first woman was oblivious; obviously a neophyte. The second had made her. Not obviously, but the way the first woman was trying to make it look like she wasn’t looking at her and the other team watching them, might as well have been screaming to both of them that they were made. She had no idea who the first team was. No matter, she would either evade or remove them. 
They all got up to leave. 
Spectre waited. 
The other team moved first, the black man shooting her a passing glance, as if to question if she was coming along. She kept waiting. 
Three minutes later, she got up, went to the bathroom, and put on a different pair of pants and a green T-shirt from the bag she carried. She quickly went to her motorcycle in the parking lot. She pulled out a black jacket from the case on the back and put on her Feher air-conditioned helmet. The helmet made her stand out; no one here would be wearing one. It was also her insurance against being identified. The air-conditioned helmet was a necessity here; she would quickly pass out from the heat otehrwise.  A worthy tradeoff. 
Her silenced Ruger was still in the motorcycle’s case, under another change of clothes. She wouldn’t pull that out until it was needed. No sense in drawing unnecessary scrutiny.
Spectre could see the target getting into the front passenger seat of the Land Rover across the parking lot. It was an armored version. She could tell from how low it was riding and the way it lumbered, even with a V8 engine. Drive-by was out of the question. She could wait; he’d eventually have to get out. 
She waited for them to leave; she wasn’t worried about losing them. She had painted a polarized paint on several spots of the vehicle. Her helmet’s visor was tuned to the specific polarization of the paint to make it obvious to her, but invisible to anyone else. She could watch them from afar. 
Her target pulled out into the traffic and headed into town. She waited and followed when they were almost out of sight. She had an idea where they were going since they turned towards the center of town. 
The two men from earlier pulled out in front of her about a minute before her. They didn’t seem to even notice her after the change of clothes.
She maintained a speed that kept her from disrupting the flow of traffic, but also kept her quarry in sight. The roads were congested, but somehow the traffic still flowed, until they got close to the market.  She noticed several drones overhead, but only one of which was chasing her target. 
CIA probably. Interesting.
The vehicle pulled off to the side of the road, about half a kilometer from the edge of the market. Spectre stopped, and surreptitiously removed her gun, hidden inside her clothing, and placed it on the front of the motorcycle. She started moving again, they had all finally just exited the vehicle. 
There was her target. Completely unaware. Sofia stood in front of her, Spectre shook her head to ward her off, but she was still there. 
“Favor, nao te mata este homen.”
Please don’t kill this man.
Spectre spoke into her helmet, “It is God’s will. Leave me alone, you’re dead.”
Sofia disappeared.
She focused. The traffic moved at a glacial pace, but motorcycles weaved in and out without difficulty. She started her approach. 
Miranda was immediately assaulted by the revived market. Loud, hot, crowded. The smell of diesel, exhaust, spices, and sweat mingled into a miasma.  
Beggars and sellers shouted for attention. Motorcycles weaved in and out of the people and vehicles in the street. It was chaos brought to life.
Something tingled at her senses; she turned back around. There. A woman on a motorcycle was shaking her head violently. What was wrong with her? She was dressed simply, Green T-shirt, light jacket for a modicum of protection, helmet, clothes on the front of her cycle. She was what was bothering her. 
Why?
The motorcycle started in her direction, helmet looking directly at her. That was it, the helmet. No one wore helmets here, especially one that expensive! She had a case on the back of her bike. Why were there clothes there? She pulled her gun and was shouting a warning when she saw the gun.
Spectre pulled up next to the target. There was a gap in the street, and someone shouted when she pulled her pistol. three cracks split the air, almost inaudible over the market’s din. Two chest, one head. Mozambique drill. She saw the blood and brain spray on his female team member behind him. She had been the one shouting; her gun was drawn, but she was too late. She sped away, leaving screaming and cries behind her. 
Blood and brain sprayed Miranda in the face. 
NO! NOT AGAIN! NOT AGAIN!
Noah fell to the ground. She watched the motorcycle’s tires speed away as the crowd erupted into bedlam. She felt for a pulse. 
Nothing. 
She checked his breathing.
Nothing.
“Fuck Noah’s down.”
Sabrina was the first to move, “Get him into the car, NOW!”
Miranda, Martin, and Ben picked him up Sabrina was shouting into the crowd in front of the Land Rover, “écartez-vous du chemin!!”
