They got him into the vehicle, Ben jumped into the driver’s seat, and they tried to speed away. Traffic had stopped, but somehow Ben made it at a crawl.
“Where’s the nearest hospital?” Miranda shouted. 
Martin calmly asserted, “We have a plan for this. Ben?”
Ben was focused, “Kilo-Alpha”
Martin nodded, “That works.” He placed a hand on Miranda’s shoulder, “This will be ok. It’s not as bad as it seems.”
Miranda’s mouth dropped, “Not as bad? He’s dead! His fucking brains are in my hair. He’s fucking dead.”
Sabrina handed her some tissues, “Clean up what you can. This isn’t going to be easy.”
Ben had exited the market and was now proceeding through the town normally, like nothing was wrong. 
Miranda was hyperventilating, “What the fuck? Who just shot him? Why is no one else concerned here.”
Silence. 
Ben spoke, “Kilo-Alpha coming up on the right.”
Miranda looked out the window, “A cemetery?”
Sabrina just nodded.
Ben proceeded through the gates and found an out-of-the-way spot. 
Martin put a reassuring hand on her shoulder, “Miranda, this wasn’t how things were supposed to happen. You’re about to find out why you are here.”
Miranda just sputtered, “What do you mean?”
Martin gestured at Noah.
Am I imagining things, or did his eye twitch? Nervous system misfire? Wait… His lips, his cheeks, the color… What… the…fuck…
Miranda watched, silent, heart thumping, as Noah’s head wounds closed themselves, bullets and fragments appearing on his forehead. His eyes fluttered, then opened. His hand wiped the blood, gore, and bullet fragments from his head. 
Miranda jumped back in her seat, “What. The. Fuck. What are you?” She went to grab her gun, but fumbled and dropped it. Noah placed his hand over the gun gently, “Please, Miranda. I might be immortal, but no one else here is.”

chapters.title: 600 Seconds
chapters.content:
00:00 Noah saw the assassin, just not in time. Her dark hair and complexion, he had just enough time to think, Brazilian maybe? Before five cracks split the air. He saw the shots hit him, three chest, two head. Noah didn’t even feel them; all he could do for a split second was admire the marksmanship. The world went black.

00:06 Miranda checked Noah’s breathing. Nothing. His pulse, none. NO! NOT AGAIN! NOT AGAIN! Her tears streaming down her face were selfish. She couldn’t believe she had gotten someone else killed. Why me? Why the fuck is it always my fault?

00:38 Sabrina watched as they placed Noah’s corpse into the back of the Land Rover. No way we’re getting this out of the upholstery. She gave Ben a curt nod as she entered the vehicle last. 

00:45 Miranda's hands found Noah's chest. She started compressions. One. Two. Three.
Pointless. This is pointless.
She kept going anyway. Blood slicked her palms. His ribs didn't move. His chest didn't rise.
"Miranda." Ben's voice, sharp. "We have to move."
She didn't stop. Couldn't stop.
Ben's hand closed around her wrist. "Miranda. Now."

01:15 Ben swerved. A motorcycle clipped the passenger side, tipped, baskets spilled produce across the street. The driver threw a squash at the rear window hard enough to rattle the frame. The bulletproof glass held.
Thank God.
His map said another hundred yards to the edge of the market. Ben threw the driver's side tires onto the sidewalk, threading through the crowd.
Just like Bangkok.
Three green dots on the screen, the recovery sites they'd coordinated on the flight over. Kilo-Alpha, primary, was closest.
A woman darted into the street, child on her hip. Ben slammed the brakes. 30-0 in less than a heartbeat. The Land Rover lurched. Miranda's hand shot out, bracing against the seat.
"Hey now!" Sabrina scolded him.
The woman froze, eyes wide. Then she ran.
Ben exhaled and hit the gas.
Secret Service agents are trained to run over anyone who gets in front of the motorcade. He'd read that somewhere.
He didn't know if he had that in him.
"Kilo-Alpha," he said, hoping someone was listening.
Martin's voice: "That works."
God help me if this ever becomes mundane like it is to Martin.
Fourteen times. He'd seen Noah die fourteen times.
Still freaked him the fuck out.

02:33 Martin sat in the middle row, hands folded in his lap. Steady. Calm. The eye of the storm.
That's what Noah called him once. The eye of the storm.
Inside, his heart was hammering.
Miranda was in the back, beside Noah's body, staring at nothing. Tears streaked through the dust on her face. She hadn't blinked in over a minute. He counted.
Forty-eight times. He'd watched Noah die forty-eight times.
And come back forty-eight times.
Please let this be forty-nine.
It never got easier. It still scared him, thirty-nine years later.
Every time, the same fear clawed at him: What if this is the last? What if something scrambled whatever magic or biology makes him come back, and this time he's gone for real?
His hands were still folded. Steady. He made sure of it.
They were headed to a cemetery. Of all the recovery sites they'd coordinated—an abandoned warehouse, a construction site, a fucking cemetery—Kilo-Alpha had to be the closest.
The irony would've been funny if his stomach wasn't in knots.
He glanced at Miranda again. That vacant stare. He'd seen it before. On his own face, in a mirror, decades ago. The first time he'd watched Noah die.
This wasn't the plan.
She was supposed to see the photos first. Videos. Controlled. Gradual. A briefing in a secure room with time to process, time to ask questions, time to breathe. Grace, rather than trauma.
Not this. Not Noah's brain matter on her face. Not her hands slick with his blood, checking for a pulse that wasn't there.
Martin's jaw tightened. He forced it to relax.
Steady. Calm. Eye of the storm.
Ben glanced at him in the rearview mirror. Martin nodded once. I'm fine. Keep driving.
Ben looked away.
Martin wasn't fine.
This might break her.
Hell, it might break him.
Martin's hands were still folded in his lap. Steady. Calm.
She may never forgive us for this. But she'd never believe it. Not until she sees it. What would I say, "Don't worry, he'll come back"?
What if he doesn't?
He didn't unfold them. If he did, they might shake.

04:18 Miranda stared at Noah’s corpse. Then at her bloody hands. She’d tried CPR, but she knew it was pointless. She couldn’t cry, couldn’t speak. She just looked at the empty shell in front of her. She felt the vehicle moving and even got thrown into the sides and the seats. She barely felt it. 
Why wasn’t anyone else upset? She looked at the others in the cabin, Ben driving, weaving through the traffic like it was a dance. Sabrina focused on the road behind from the front seat. Martin sat in the row in front of her. His eyes were soft. Sad.
He blames me. He should. I got him killed. Just like Bill.
It was so quiet. No one was talking except for a few words here and there. She thought she heard a voice, far away. She stopped to listen, it was Martin.
“Miranda, everything is going to be ok. We’re going somewhere to regroup. It’s going to be ok.”
Yeah right. It’ll be ok. Like Noah’s going to miraculously be reborn with five bullets in him. Don’t patronize me, Martin. I’m not a child.

06:46 Miranda was slumped on the floorboards. She could see the view outside the window changing to more of the shanties and slums they had, to this point, been avoiding. She saw Noah’s foot twitch. It scared the shit out of her at first. 
Death twitches. Scary. Disgusting. Normal. I guess. I haven’t spent much time around dead bodies.

07:13 The Land Rover stopped. Ben killed the engine.
Silence.
Miranda looked up. Through the window: crumbling headstones, red dirt, weeds pushing through cracked concrete.
A cemetery. Of course, it's a cemetery. They could dump him here, and no one would ever find him.
Ben and Martin lifted Noah's body. His head lolled. Blood dripped onto the dirt.
They laid him between two graves. The names had worn away decades ago.
Miranda sat five feet away, staring. The exit wound in his skull was still open. She could see bone.

07:55 The twitches hadn't slowed. They were rhythmic now. Steady. Like a heartbeat. Miranda tried looking anywhere else. Then his fingers twitched.
What was that?
“Martin. Is it normal for bodies to twitch like this?”
“Sometimes yes. I think you should drink some water.”
Miranda took the water. Drinking it felt like she hadn’t had any in a month. She did feel better.

08:55 Now that’s not normal for sure. 
Noah’s eyes were fluttering. “Martin, what the hell is going on?” 
“I’m sorry Miranda, it will be ok, I promise.”
“I’m not sure I want to believe you.”

09:30 Miranda was staring at Noah's chest.
It was moving.
Not twitching. Not spasming. Rising and falling.
No. That's not…
She leaned closer. His chest rose. Fell. Rose again.
Reflex. Post-mortem reflex. That's a thing. Right?
But reflexes don't have rhythm. Reflexes don't look like breathing.
Her hand moved toward his neck. She stopped herself.
What if I'm wrong? What if he's…
No. Dead is dead. I checked. No pulse. No breath. Dead.
But his chest kept rising.
Miranda's vision tunneled. Her ears rang.
This isn't real. I'm in shock. I'm hallucinating.
Noah's fingers twitched. Then curled. Then flexed.
Oh God.


09:59 Noah could hear what was going on. He was too tired to open his eyes. Too tired to talk. Too tired to move.
This wasn't the plan. Why hadn't I seen this?
Tomas. That had to be it. If Tomas was involved, Noah's visions went dark. Just like Bamako.
His heart was racing. This hadn't happened in over half a century.

10:00 He opened his eyes.
Miranda's face. Pale, wide-eyed, streaked with tears and his blood, stared back at him. Mouth open in a silent scream.
She looked terrified. He couldn't blame her.
Miranda fumbled for her gun. 
They knew.
The thought cut through the shock like a blade.
Martin knew. Ben knew. Sabrina knew.
Miranda's gaze snapped to Martin. He was watching her. Steady. Calm.
Like he'd been waiting for this.
They weren't scared. Not once. Because they knew.
They knew he'd come back.
Her hands were shaking. She couldn't stop them.
How many times?
How many times have they watched him do this?
She looked back at Noah. His eyes were open. Wet. Apologetic.
Dead men don't come back.
But he did.
So what else is real?
Miranda screamed and dropped her gun.
Noah’s eyes looked heavy; he looked down.
This isn't the way things were supposed to go. I'm so sorry, Miranda.

chapters.title: Cousins
chapters.content:
The cemetery smelled like sun-baked earth and diesel fumes from the road. No grass, just red dirt, cracked headstones leaning at angles like broken teeth, and weeds pushing through the clay. 
The door of the armored Land Rover opened, and Miranda spilled out from the vehicle. The heat pressed down like a hand on Miranda's chest. Her shirt stuck to her back. Sweat pooled at her temples.
Inside, Noah groaned and sat up.
Miranda's hand went to her holster. Empty.
Her gun. Where was her…
It was still inside the Land Rover. On the floor. Where she'd dropped it.
She pointed at Noah anyway. Her hand shook. "Stay down. Stay the fuck down."
Ben stepped between them, hands up. "Miranda…"
"I felt his pulse stop." Her hand shook. "I saw… There were brains. On my face."
Noah wiped blood from his forehead with the back of his hand. Bullets and bone fragments slid off his skin like rain. He didn't flinch. Just... cleaned himself off.
"Miranda." His voice was calm. Too calm. "Breathe."
“What?”
“Breathe Miranda.”
“The fuck I will.” Still pointing at Noah she yelled, "What are you?"
Ben handed Noah a package of baby wipes. Noah took them without looking, then started scrubbing gore from his hairline, as if washing off mud after a hike.
Miranda's legs gave out. She sat hard on the ground, hands planted in the dirt. A stray dog ran across the cemetery behin the car. A cicada screamed from a nearby acacia tree. Somewhere, a car horn blared. A gentle breeze came from behind her. She could smell diesel exhaust, trash, and something cooking. The city kept moving. The world kept turning.
And Noah Hammond kept breathing.
"You were dead," she said. Quieter now. "I checked. No pulse. No breath. You were gone."
Noah cracked his neck. The sound made her flinch.
"I was," he said, looking at his watch, "For about ten minutes."
Martin handed him a water bottle. Noah drank half of it in one pull, then poured the rest over his head. Pink water ran down his neck, soaking his collar.
Miranda laughed. It came out wrong. Sharp, jagged, the sound of something breaking.
"This is insane. You're insane. I'm…" She pressed her palms to her eyes. The world spun. "I need to sit down."
"You are sitting," Ben said gently.
"Then I need to stand up." She tried. Her knees buckled. Ben caught her elbow.
Noah stood, testing his legs like a newborn colt. He flexed his fingers, rolled his shoulders. Everything worked.
“How is this possible? What are you?” Miranda heard herself ask.
“I’m still me. I’m not fully human, like you. I like to say, we’re cousins. I’ve been alive for a very long time. God willing, I will have many more years in front of me.”
"How old are you?"
Noah looked at her for a long moment. "You're accepting this awfully quickly."
Miranda laughed. It sounded wrong. Brittle. "Accepting? I'm not accepting shit." Her voice rose. "I'm sitting in a cemetery in Mali watching a dead man drink water. I don't know what the fuck I'm doing. I'm just..."
Her voice broke.
Noah hesitated. "You're in shock. We should…"
"Answer the fucking question." Her voice was raw. "You owe me that much."
Noah's jaw tightened. He looked at Martin. Martin nodded once.
"Not to be evasive, but—" Noah rubbed his forehead. "I'm not sure. Calendars lost meaning a long time ago." He paused. Dust swirled around his boots. "I remember Egypt without Pharaohs, if that gives you an idea."
The bile rose fast. Miranda turned and vomited into the red dirt. Once. Twice. Her stomach clenched until there was nothing left.
Ben handed her a bottle of water. She rinsed, spat, drank.
"Cousins," she rasped. "What the fuck does that mean?"
Noah smiled faintly. "It means we're close. Genetically, as close as Neanderthals and Denisovans. I’m Homo extremis."
"But not Homo sapien."
"No."
Miranda stood. Wobbled. Wiped her mouth.
The cemetery stretched out around them—crumbling walls, faded names, graves no one visited anymore. A century of history, maybe two.
Noah remembered Egypt without Pharaohs.
The irony wasn't lost on her.
"Your brother," she said. "Tomas."
"Is immortal, yes."
"We can't kill him?"
Noah's hand twitched. Just once. "We can die. It's just not easy." His voice went flat. "He and I have killed each other countless times over the centuries."
"Why?"
Noah sat on a low stone wall. The ancient rock didn't crack under his weight, but it looked like it wanted to.
"Much like humans," he said, "immortals are not a homogeneous group."
He gestured for her to sit. She didn't. Couldn't. Her legs were shaking too hard.
“To make this easy to understand, there are three main factions," Noah said. "The Watchers. They withdraw completely, tend to their own, don't engage with humanity." He paused. "Then there are those of us who try to help or at least engage with humanity. The Shepherds."
His hand twitched. His jaw clenched.
"And then there are those who see humans as livestock. Who believe you exist to serve us, or not at all. The Dominatus.
"Tomas."
"Has been Dominatus since I can remember."
Noah paused. "The factions are informal. It's not like the Freemasons or Illuminati. No rites, no ceremonies. People move between them as they age. I consider myself a Shepherd, but..." He looked away. "I'm moving toward being a Watcher."
Miranda's mind raced. "How have immortals not taken over?"
"Have you ever heard of the Bronze Age Collapse? The Sea People?"
She nodded slowly. "Entire civilizations wiped out. No one knows why."
"That was the Dominatus."
Miranda stared at him. "You're saying immortals destroyed the Bronze Age Civilizations?"
"Yes."
"How many died?"
Noah's jaw tightened. "Millions. Maybe tens of millions."
The heat pressed down. A bead of sweat ran down Miranda's spine. She couldn't breathe.
"Millions." Her voice was flat. "Your people killed millions."
"Not my people." Noah's voice went hard. "The Dominatus. The ones we've been fighting for thousands of years."
"How did they lose?"
"Because the other immortals banded with the humans." Noah's fists clenched. "Also, you outnumber us by about a million to one. Even then, we were outnumbered ten thousand to one."
His eyes were wet. His gaze unfocused, staring at something a thousand miles, or a thousand years, away.
"The Great Breaking—the Šĕbārā Rabbā—killed ninety percent of us." His voice cracked. "Entire bloodlines erased. It was genocide."
The silence hung over them, thick as the heat.
“Why would immortals work with humans against other immortals? That makes no sense.”
“Their reasons are as varied as any. Mine was that we had our chance; evolution chose differently. There is no sense in fighting reality. You can make the best of it, or get swept away in the tide.”
Miranda's legs finally gave out for real. She sat on the wall beside him. Close enough to feel the warmth radiating off his skin. Close enough to see the faint tremor in his hands.
"I need." Her voice broke. "I need a minute."
Martin stepped forward. "We'd expect nothing less. This wasn’t how we intended for things to happen. You weren’t meant to find out in such a…traumatic manner."
Miranda’s laugh was mirthless, “You mean I wasn’t supposed to watch Noah die and get his brains on my face? That was nice of you to consider an alternate method.”
Noah and Martin looked at each other. Faces downcast.
“Who was that? The assassin.”
Noah looked up, “I don’t know. She looked like she was Brazilian, or maybe Argentinian.”
“I’d ask how you know that so definitively, but I honestly don’t give a shit right now.”
Miranda looked around, “I’m going to need a minute.”
She stood. Walked toward the cemetery gate. Her hand trailed along the rusted iron, steadying herself.
Behind her, Noah's voice, quiet and ancient: "Take all the time you need."
She stopped. Turned back. "Who knows?"
Martin answered. "Myself, Ben, Sabrina, a few senior staff you haven't been introduced to yet—I'll tell you ahead of time if they know—and the senior members of the National Security Council."
She stopped. Turned back. "Who knows?"
Martin answered. "Myself, Ben, Sabrina, a few senior staff you haven't been introduced to yet. I'll tell you ahead of time if they know, and the senior members of the National Security Council."
Miranda shook her head. "Okay. Thanks."
"What else?"
Martin shifted uncomfortably. "What do you mean?"
"What else is there about Noah and this place that I need to know?"
Noah moved to speak, but Martin interrupted. "A lot. But all things in time. At this point, we're not hiding anything from you, just trying not to overwhelm you."
Miranda put her hand up to object, then lowered it. "Thank you."
She walked twenty paces away, leaned against a crumbling headstone, and tried to remember how to breathe.
The stone was warm under her palm. The name had worn away decades ago. Whoever they were, they were forgotten now. Overhead, a hawk flapped its wings in the warm updrafts of the city, watching for prey. 
And Miranda Cruz, who'd spent eighteen months drowning in guilt over one death, realized she was now working for a man who couldn't die.
She pressed her forehead to the stone and let the tears come.
The hawk broke from its circle, gliding past the cemetery toward the edge of the city. Watching. Hungry. It passed over the slums, towards a former industrial area, past a group of abandoned warehouses. 
From the top of one of these warehouses, 500 meters away, Tomas lowered his binoculars. He spoke in Ancient Hebrew, from biblical times, “Shalom, akhí” Hello Brother. “Erʾakh be-karóv” I’ll see you soon.

chapters.title: Rodney’s Speech
chapters.content:
## Oran, Algeria 8 November 1942 09:30 AM
Noah Hammond, then known as Lt. William Danforth hit the dirt a heartbeat before the next burst stitched the scrubline where his head had been.
The air above the Oran approaches was a sheet of sound HMS Rodney’s big guns hammering somewhere out in the haze, Vichy batteries on Djebel Santon answering back, the dry, ugly chatter of machine guns from the whitewashed village ahead. Dust hung in the morning light. Spent casings glittered in the rocks like brass seeds.
“Hold it down!” he snapped.
The two BARs on his flank answered, short controlled bursts back toward the stone houses. The enemy guns didn’t flinch. They were firing from elevation, from good cover, and they had the range.
Danforth crawled forward, belly scraping red earth, until the ground broke away beneath him.
The ridge fell off toward the sea in a steep, stony slope, terraced fields and scrub dropping to the pale ribbon of road that curled around the bay. Beyond that, the harbor at Oran was a shallow arc of concrete and breakwater and smoke. The sea wall stood out in hard angles, taking the pounding from the ships offshore. To the right, the little Capaya fishing camp clung to the shoreline, flat roofs, beached boats pulled high, nets hanging in ragged curtains. The fishermen tending to their nets and lines onshore as if it was just another day, indifferent to the fury and death going on just a few miles away.
​And higher than all of it, crowning Djebel Murdjadjo, the old fort watched the battle. Fort Santa Cruz, Fort Santiago, it had worn too many names over too many centuries, clung to the summit like a stone barnacle, its bastions etched sharp against the sky. It stood as a silent witness, judging this new era of warfare with detachment. Danforth could see the flashes of Vichy guns near Santon and the heavier puffs from the fort’s supporting batteries, throwing shells out toward the invasion fleet.
Rodney answered, somewhere beyond the horizon. Her reports came in a slow, abdominal rhythm you felt more than heard, a deeper concussion under the smaller guns. Each time she fired, the sea wall and harbor installations jumped in a jitter of dust and smoke.
Closer in, men were dying.
OSS had sent them here months ago, to prepare the battlefield for the landings, recruit allies, gather intelligence. They’d done their job well. The assault craft were not far behind them. 
The village, little more than a handful of stone houses and a tiled customs building at the inland edge, dominated the shallow valley between his position and the bay. They’d hit the coastal gun they’d come for, charges placed, breech wrecked, crew scattered, but the exfil had fallen apart the second they broke cover. Machine guns on the upper floors had cut the platoon in half. Now they were in the open, pinned against scrub and rock that might as well have been a flat parade ground.
A rifle crack snapped past his ear. One of the BAR men, Pfc. Harlan, rolled onto his back, clutching his throat. The sound he made was wet and surprised.
“Doc!” someone screamed.
“Keep your heads down,” Danforth said, without looking back. “We’re not getting out of this by trading rounds.”
He pulled the binoculars up, ignoring the way his hands shook. The glass brought the village in close enough to touch.
There he was.
Third floor of the customs house at the far end, the one with the red tile roof and the chipped plaster Saints over the door. A man in a field-gray coat at the window, not firing, just pointing. He had a field telephone in one hand, headset crooked against his jaw, the other arm extended, directing fire like a conductor. Each time he jabbed, a machine gun or sniper barked from the balcony, and another American went down.
The man turned slightly, shouting to someone inside the room. Danforth saw the scar line along the jaw, he would know that face anywhere. His brother. It seemed like whenever there was a right side or a wrong side, he’d always manage to end up on the wrong side. 
“Lieutenant!” It was Corporal Hensley, down-slope, voice flat with panic. “We’re getting chewed up here”
“I see them,” Danforth said.
He dropped the binoculars against his chest and keyed the radio handset clipped to his webbing.
“Firefly, this is Raven Two,” he said, voice steady by force of habit. “Adjust fire, over.”
Static, then a clipped English voice, calm against the chaos. “Raven Two, Firefly. Send position. Over.”
He glanced down at the map case pinned against the rock, the grease pencil marks they’d made in the pre-dawn briefing, tied to the naval fire grid.
“Target: stone customs house at eastern edge of Oran approaches, overlooking Capaya fishing camp,” he said. “Grid Eight-Nine, Three-Four. Marked by red tile roof and adjacent minaret. Range approximately ten miles from your current station. Request one round, high explosive, over.”
“Raven Two, Firefly copies customs house at Eight-Nine Three-Four, one round HE. Stand by. Out.”
Danforth raised the binoculars again. In the window, his brother was laughing at something the machine-gun team said, head thrown back in the same careless arc as he had when he would push the neighbor boy’s face in a mud puddle, right up until William had almost beaten him to death for it. Willam had left home shortly after. He hadn’t seen his brother in decades.
A low, distant rumble rolled across the water. Rodney was speaking again. A heartbeat later, Firefly’s voice: “Shot, over.”
“Splash,” Danforth said automatically. “Out.”
He scanned beyond the village, uphill. The shell came in far, he saw it as a flicker and then a geyser of earth and stone well short and right of the customs house, plowing a furrow in a terraced field. The shock wave hit them as a hard slap of air and dust.
Hensley flinched, ducking as if the round were theirs. “Jesus Christ, sir, you trying to kill us?”
“Not yet,” Danforth said.
He squeezed the transmit key again. “Firefly, Raven Two. Drop three hundred, left two hundred. One round, over.”
“Raven Two, Firefly copies—drop three-zero-zero, left two-zero-zero, one round. Out.”
He could taste grit. The sea breeze coming up from the bay brought smoke and a faint tang of diesel and salt. Below, Capaya’s beach was a smear of chaos, beached fishing boats pushed aside for landing craft, Algerian fishermen’s nets trampled under American boots. The seawall took another hit and coughed white dust into the blue air.
​“Shot, over.”
“Splash. Out.”
Second shell. He saw this one all the way in, a dark speck descending with lazy inevitability. It hit just beyond the customs house, in the road, close enough to lift the building’s far wall on a bulge of pressure and rip the doors off their hinges. The machine gun went silent for a second, then snapped back to life. The customs house still stood, wounded but intact.
“Little more…” Danforth muttered.
“Sir?” Hensley’s face was streaked with mud and blood. “We’re in the short grass here. They don’t move, we’re all…”
“You want to stand up and run for it, Corporal, be my guest,” Danforth said, eyes never leaving the glass.
He saw his brother again at the window, staggering back from the near miss, then lunging for the phone, shouting new corrections to his own gun crews. Stubborn as ever. Unkillable as ever.
Except he wasn’t.
“Firefly, Raven Two,” Danforth said. The words tasted like iron. “Add one hundred. Fire for effect, one round. Same target. Over.”
There was the tiniest pause on the line. The British FO on the other end understood what “add one hundred” meant at that range.
“Raven Two, Firefly copies. Add one-zero-zero, fire for effect, one round. Shot, over.”
Danforth lowered the binoculars for a moment. His men were pressed into the earth, trying to make themselves as small as stones. Harlan was still, eyes open to the sky. Someone had closed his throat with a bandage anyway, a useless, reflexive act of defiance.
He brought the glass back up.

He was at the window again. No telephone now, just his hands, framing the killing ground, pointing.
“Splash,” Firefly said.
The shell arrived with no drama this time, just a sudden white flash on the street in front of the building. The customs house seemed to stand defiant, for maybe half a second, walls bowing inward, then exploded in a roar as stone and tile and timber flew outward in a dirty blossom. The roof lifted and folded. The third floor vanished. The second followed, then the whole thing collapsed into a 30-foot crater in the street.
The shock wave rolled over the ridge like a physical thing, slamming dust and hot air into Danforth’s face. Pebbles rattled down the slope. For a moment, there was no sound but the distant thunder of other guns and the ringing in his ears.
“Target destroyed,” Danforth said softly, not keying the mic.
Danforth vomited. Everything in his stomach came up in the dust. He heaved until there was nothing left but bile and spit, burning his throat.
He had probably just killed his brother. His akhi.
But he’d saved his men. His people. Once again, he had chosen humans over blood—because in the end, he was the one who gave the order.
Tomas had made his choices.
Danforth made his.
Hensley risked a look. The customs house was a jagged stump, one wall half-standing, the rest a heap of rubble spilling into the street. The machine guns were silent. What defenders were left were either dead or too busy digging themselves out to fire. The rest of the buildings within a 50-yard radius of the building were all flattened, collapsed, or damaged beyond repair.
“Jesus,” Hensley whispered. “You got it.”
Danforth swallowed. The taste of dust and cordite sat heavy in his throat. He couldn’t see the window anymore. Couldn’t see the face.
“Raven Two, Firefly,” the radio crackled. “Report effects, over.”
He keyed the handset. His voice came out level. “Firefly, Raven Two. Customs house collapsed. Enemy fire suppressed. Good effect on target. Over.”
“Raven Two, Firefly. Roger. Out.”
He lowered the radio, fingers white around the handset.
“Get them moving,” he said. “While they’re still blind. We’re off this hill in ninety seconds.”
“Yes, sir.”
Men began to rise, one by one, stumbling into a crouched run down the slope toward the gap where the customs house had been, skirting the Capaya shacks and the nets and the beached boats. Behind them, Fort Santa Cruz watched in silence from the heights, as it had watched every other empire use the harbor and the bay and the men between them as pieces on a board.
Danforth stole one last glance at the ruin.
“I kill you to save them.” He shook his head.
“Fair trade.”
Then he turned and followed his men down toward the smoke.

chapters.title: Akhí
chapters.content:
Five hundred meters away, on the roof of an abandoned warehouse, Tomas lowered his binoculars.
The cemetery stretched out below—red dirt, crumbling headstones, weeds pushing through clay. Noah stood near the Land Rover, speaking to his team. Martin, steady as always. Ben, still jumpy after fourteen resurrections. Sabrina, scanning the perimeter.
And the woman. Miranda. Crying against a headstone like the world had ended.
Pathetic. 
But Noah.
Noah looked tired.
Tomas's fist flexed. Unflexed. Flexed again.
He spoke, archaic Hebrew, closer to the tongue of slaves in Egypt than any modern form. The words felt heavy on his tongue, older than the city below, older than the nations that had risen and fallen around them.
"Shalom, akhí."
Hello, brother.
"Erʾakh be-karóv."
I'll see you soon.
The wind shifted. Dust swirled across the rooftop. Somewhere below, a car horn blared. The city kept moving, oblivious.
Tomas turned away from the cemetery. His tactical vest, dark with sweat and grime, shifted with the movement. Shoulder-length hair, unkempt and streaked with dust, fell across his face. He pushed it back with one hand. He took a water bottle from his vest and took a long pull.
The scar on his right cheek caught the light. A thin line, healed centuries ago. A souvenir from one of the times he had fought Noah, his name was Belem then.
He walked to the roof access door. His boots made no sound on the concrete.
Behind him, the hawk circled one more time, then flew toward the outskirts.
Tomas descended into the warehouse.
The air inside was cooler. Dim. The warehouse had been abandoned for years, rusted machinery, broken pallets, and graffiti on the walls. Something was dead in the far corner, its stench heavy in the air. But in the back corner, behind a false wall, his team had set up a temporary operations center.
Eight people. Humans, all of them. Dominatus-aligned, or paid well enough not to care.
Tomas stepped into the room.
The shift was immediate. Subtle. The low hum of conversation faltered. One of the men, a South African mercenary named Kobus, rubbed his face and looked away, suddenly restless. A woman near the radio, Camila, Spanish, ex-intelligence, blinked and frowned, as if she'd forgotten what she was doing.
Tomas noticed. He was leaking. He exhaled slowly, pulling his emotions back inside him. Resolute. Controlled. Nothing.
The restlessness faded. The team refocused.
He didn't apologize. They wouldn't understand anyway.
"Status," Tomas said.
Kobus looked up from a laptop. "The hit went clean. Local police are still securing the scene. No pursuit."
"And the message?"
Kobus hesitated. “Went out 60 seconds after the hit. Message was confirmed received. We received a movement confirmation five minutes later.”
Tomas' fist flexed, “Good. That’s well within the window. If he’s dead, he can’t see,” Tomas said. “That’s the point.
“And the message?” Kobus asked.
“Two messages.” Tomas corrected. “Both sent and received.”
“One audience acted. The other will learn.”
Kobus nodded slowly, not understanding, but smart enough not to ask.
Tomas turned to the rest of the team. "We're leaving Bamako tonight. Kobus, coordinate transport. Camila, The site is prepared? 
She nodded, “Explosives are rigged.”
"Anything else?" Tomas's tone made it clear there shouldn't be.
Silence.
"Then move."
The team dispersed. Efficient. Obedient. Tomas watched them go, his expression unreadable.
They were tools. Useful, for now. If one of them died tonight, he'd replace them by morning.
The warehouse emptied. Footsteps echoed, then faded. Engines started outside. Vehicles pulled away.
Tomas stood in the center of the room, alone.
The light from a broken window cut across the floor in sharp angles. Dust hung in the air, suspended, golden.
He stared at it.
For a moment, he wasn't in Bamako. He wasn't in a warehouse. He wasn't ancient beyond time.
The steppe stretched endlessly in every direction. Grass, sky, wind. Nothing else.
Tomas rode beside his brother, both of them laughing, racing their horses toward the horizon. In the distance, a herd of mammoths moved slowly across the plain. Closer, a woolly rhino—shaggy, horned, long extinct—snorted and lumbered out of their path. Noah—Orion, he'd called himself then—was younger, barely sixteen summers, but already faster, already sharper.
"You'll never catch me!" he shouted, grinning over his shoulder.
Tomas leaned forward, urging his horse faster. "I don't need to catch you, akhí. I just need to outlast you!"
Noah laughed. The sound carried across the steppe, bright and unguarded.
They rode until the sun set. Until the stars came out. Until their horses were too tired to run.
They sat together on the grass, breathing hard, staring up at the sky.
"Do you think we'll always be like this?" Noah asked quietly.
Tomas didn't answer right away. Then: "Yes."
Noah smiled. "Good."
Tomas blinked.
The warehouse came back into focus. The dust. The broken window. The silence. Outside the engines of their trucks were running; they were ready to leave.
His fist flexed. Unflexed.
His jaw tightened.
Come on, brother. Follow the trail. You want to know what I'm doing. You want to stop me. You can't help yourself.
He walked to the door. His hand rested on the handle for a moment.
Then he opened it.
Stepped through.
And closed it behind him.
The warehouse went dark.

chapters.title: Algebra at Scale
chapters.content:
Silvana Carolina da Silva parked her motorcycle on a curb outside the market and walked away like she belonged there. Spectre was done for now.
Loose cotton shirt. Dark jeans. Sunglasses. A canvas bag over her shoulder with her pistol buried inside loose clothes.
No one looked twice.
She turned down a side street, away from the sirens. Police would be swarming the market by now. Securing the scene. Interviewing witnesses. Looking for a shooter who was already gone. Honestly, maybe not. Everything happened so fast, no one may even have called the police.
Spectre pulled out her phone—encrypted, routed through three VPN layers—and opened WetWrk.
The job was still listed as IN PROGRESS.
She tapped MARK COMPLETE, typed her confirmation code, and hit submit.
The screen refreshed.
JOB COMPLETED. AWAITING CLIENT CONFIRMATION. PAYMENT PENDING.
Silvana exhaled. Done.
She pocketed the phone and kept walking.
A few hours later, Silvana was sitting in a café a kilometer from her hotel. Outdoor seating. View of the street. Espresso in front of her, untouched. 
Her phone buzzed. She pulled it out.
NEW MESSAGE FROM CLIENT.
She opened it.
CLIENT HAS MARKED JOB AS COMPLETE.
RATING: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5 stars)
The $500,000 was transferred to her account. Seconds later, $50,000 was transferred anonymously to Igreja Nossa Senhora da Conceição in Paranatinga, Brazil. Padre João would set it aside for scholarships and pray for the benefactor’s soul. He didn’t know it was too late.
Another $50,000 was transferred to her family’s account. They would spit on her if they saw her, but they always spent the money.
She walked away from the café, leaving the motorcycle where it was. In a place like this, an unattended bike didn’t stay unattended for long. By the time anyone noticed, it would be somebody else’s problem.
Heading toward the hotel would be risky for most people at this hour. A woman alone drew attention. She didn’t. Something about her made attention slide off. Predators knew when they could become prey, and they chose easier meals.
The hotel came into view three blocks later. Azalai Grand. Mid-tier international chain. Anonymous enough to blend, expensive enough to have security cameras that actually worked.
Silvana slowed as she approached the entrance. Her training kicked in automatically. Scan the perimeter. Note the exits. Identify threats.
A Land Rover was parked near the entrance. New. Clean. Not the rental she'd seen earlier.
And standing beside it, talking to another man, was her target.
Noah Hammond.
Alive.
Silvana stopped walking. Her hand found the strap of her canvas bag by reflex. The world narrowed to a single point. The man. His profile. The way he gestured with his left hand. The same gestures she'd watched through her red dot sight.
Her pulse stayed steady. Her breathing didn't change. But something inside her chest went cold and tight.
She was fifteen meters away. Close enough to see clearly. Far enough to be just another pedestrian.
Noah turned slightly. For half a second, she thought he might look at her. She kept walking. Steady pace. Natural. A woman heading home after a long day.
He didn't look.
She went into the lobby, down the hall near the bathrooms. She laned against a wall, and pulled out her phone.
Her hands were shaking.
The hands bothered her. They never shook.
She opened the helmet cam footage. Pulled up the timestamp. 14:23:17.
There. Market entrance. Target in the white shirt. Clean sight picture.
First shot. Center mass. Impact visible. Target stumbles.
Second shot. Center mass. Impact visible. Target falls.
Third shot. Head. Clean entry. Mist.
Textbook Mozambique drill. She'd done it a thousand times. Two to the chest, one to the head. Guaranteed kill. At the very least, the target is in a hospital, slowly dying.
Except he wasn't even wounded.
She replayed it. Watched the impacts. Watched him fall. Watched the crowd surge. Watched him disappear under bodies and chaos.
And then nothing. The feed went dark when she'd extracted.
Silvana closed the footage. Opened her browser. Searched for news from the market.
CHEMICAL ATTACK IN BAMAKO. CASUALTIES UNKNOWN.
Chemical attack. Almost two weeks ago. Not a shooting. The articles didn't mention gunfire at all. Just the yellow mist. The panic. The bodies.
She scrolled through images. Hazmat teams. Covered bodies. Survivors being evacuated.
No mention of a man shot three times who got back up.
Silvana looked up from her phone. The alley was dark. A cat moved through garbage near a dumpster. Somewhere a generator hummed.
She replayed the footage again. And again. And again.
Same result. Perfect shots. Perfect placement. He should be dead.
She pressed her palm flat against the wall. The concrete was rough. Real. Solid. The world still made sense in small ways.
But Noah Hammond was alive.
And that didn't make sense at all.
Her room was on the fourth floor. Corner unit. View of the street and the hotel entrance. She'd paid extra for it.
Silvana dropped the canvas bag on the bed. Her hands were still shaking.
She sat down hard. Pressed her palms flat against her thighs until the tremor stopped.
Then she pulled out the encrypted phone and opened her secure messaging app. The client's rating glowed on the screen. Five stars. Payment confirmed—full amount, clean transfer.
But something was wrong.
She'd killed him. She knew she'd killed him. Three rounds. Perfect placement. The helmet cam didn't lie.
But Noah Hammond was alive.
Silvana stood and walked to the window. Looked down at the hotel entrance. The Land Rover was still there. Noah was gone now—probably inside with his contact.
She should leave. Now. Tonight. Take the payment and disappear into another city, another name, another life.
But she couldn't.
Because if she'd killed him and he came back, she needed to know how.
And if she hadn't killed him—if something had gone wrong with her shots, her weapon, her technique—she needed to know that too.
It’s not that he’s… It can’t be. God wouldn’t have let me kill a messenger of his. Unless…
She was losing her grip on reality. Had she even done the hit? Everything said so, but her 
Silvana pulled the external drive from her bag. The one she'd pulled from her employer's systems when the job closed too cleanly, too quickly. Insurance. Answers.
She powered up her air-gapped laptop and plugged it in. 
The drive mounted. Clean. No alarms. No viruses. She’d run software to break the encryption on the way here. 
She opened the directory.
PHASE-ONE BMK.
Bamako. She already knew that much.
She clicked through folders. Weather models. Crowd density projections. Aerosol persistence calculations. Casualty estimates.
Her stomach turned. To whoever planned it, it was just math homework. Almost a thousand dead, collapsed into a few neat equations and variables.
Spectre shivered.
She kept digging.
PHASE-TWO VTC.
Vatican City.
Silvana stopped breathing.
Photographs. Architectural layouts. Papal schedules. Crowd projections for a papal mass.
Bile rose in her throat. Her hand closed into a fist. She forced it open. Kept reading.
A separate directory. Set apart from the phases.
AQUILEIA.
She clicked it. Found a scanned document. Ancient. Roman. An account of a chemical weapon attack that sounded like myth.
Inside, a subfolder.
ORION.
She opened it.
No photograph. No biography. Just models. Equations. Math too complicated to understand. The graphs showed frameworks that collapsed when a single variable was introduced.
ORION.
The summary at the end: ORION CONTAINMENT REQUIRED PRIOR TO PHASE TWO.
Silvana leaned back. Stared at the screen.
If ORION was a person—and her gut said it was—then it was someone whose existence threatened their plan. Someone they needed gone before the Vatican.
They’d hired her to kill the person who would stop the attack on her world’s only chance at salvation and redemption.
She thought about Noah Hammond. About the way he'd fallen. About the way he'd stood outside the hotel six hours later like nothing had happened.
"What are you?" she whispered.
The room didn't answer.
She scrolled through more files. Threat assessments. Contingency plans. All of them treating ORION like a problem to be solved. An obstacle to be removed.
But none of them explained what it was.
Or how he'd survived three gunshots.
Silvana closed the laptop. Sat in the dark. The city hummed beyond her window. Sirens. Generators. The ambient noise of a place that never truly slept.
She reached for her rosary. The beads were worn smooth. She'd carried them since childhood. Since before everything went wrong.
Ave Maria, cheia de graça...
The prayer came automatically. She didn't think about the words anymore. Didn't know if they meant anything. But her hands knew the rhythm. Her lips formed the shapes.
Hail Mary, full of grace...
She stopped mid-prayer.
Grace.
She'd shot a man three times. Perfect placement. Perfect technique. And he'd gotten up.
Her hands started shaking again.
"Sophia?" she whispered.
The name came out before she could stop it. Her sister's name. The name she never said out loud anymore.
Silence.
Silvana closed her eyes. Pressed her forehead against her clasped hands. The rosary beads dug into her skin.
"Sophia, if you're there..." Her voice cracked. "Tell me what he is."
More silence.
Then, so quiet she almost didn't hear it—
"Why does it matter?"
Silvana's eyes snapped open.
The room was empty. The voice had come from inside her head. Or beside her. Or nowhere at all.
She didn't turn to look. She knew what she would see. Or wouldn't see.
"Because I need to know," Silvana said. Her voice was steadier now. "I need to know what I'm dealing with."
"You already know."
"No. I don't."
"You shot him three times. He died. He came back." The voice was gentle. Patient. The way Sophia had always been. "He’s the one that evil men say can stop an attack on his grace. What does that make him?"
"I don't know. A demon? An angel? Something sent by—" She stopped. Couldn't finish.
"By who?" The voice pressed. "By God? By the Devil? By something else?"
Silvana's jaw clenched. "I don't know."
"Then maybe you're asking the wrong question."
"What's the right question?"
Silence stretched. Silvana waited. The rosary beads bit into her palm.
"The right question," Sophia's voice came back softer, "is how will you redeem yourself for this?"
Silvana opened her eyes. The room was still empty. Just her. Just the laptop. Just the darkness.
"I don't know," she said to the empty room.
"You're Catholic, querida. You know what an attack on the Vatican means."
Silvana's throat tightened. "I'm damned. What I think doesn't matter."
"Then why are you praying?"
She sat in the dark for a long time. The rosary beads pressed into her palm. The laptop hummed softly. Outside, Bamako continued being Bamako. Dangerous, chaotic, indifferent, alive.
Finally, she stood. Walked to the window. Looked down at the street.
The Land Rover was still there. And standing beside it, talking to a woman, was Noah Hammond.
Alive.
Silvana watched him. Studied him. Looking for something—anything—that would explain what he was.
He looked normal. Human. Vulnerable.
But she'd put three rounds in him. And he was standing there like it had never happened.
"Does it scare you?" Sophia asked.
"Yes," Silvana admitted.
"Good." A pause. "Fear means you're still human."
"I stopped being human a long time ago."
"No." Firm now. The way Sophia used to sound when she was alive. When she was real. "You stopped being innocent. That's not the same thing."
Silvana turned away from the window. Sat down at the laptop. Opened the ORION file again.
She stared at the screen. At the threat assessments. "What are you?" she asked again.
"Does it matter?" Sophia's voice. Quiet. Sad. "You already know what they're planning. You know what Phase Two means."
Silvana's breath caught. "That's not my problem."
"Then why did God choose you to show this to?"
"I'm an demon. Not a hero."
"God doesn’t think so." She could almost feel Sophia’s hand on her shoulder.
Silvana closed her eyes. "You're not real."
"Maybe. Maybe not." Sophia's voice was fading now. "But that doesn't make me wrong."
Silence.
Silvana opened her eyes. The room was empty. Her heart ached.
Then she looked at the window. At the street below. At the man who wouldn't stay dead. He was walking into the hotel with the woman that called out right before he was shot.
Silvana Carolina da Silva had choices to make. Choices that Spectre couldn’t.

chapters.title: Nachall
chapters.content:
## Bamako, Mali
The Land Rover's engine ticked as it cooled. Noah stood beside it for a moment, one hand on the roof, steadying himself.
His legs felt like lead. His chest ached, not from the bullets, those wounds were gone, healed without a trace, but from the memory of them. The phantom pain of dying. Again.
How many times had he died? He'd lost count somewhere around the eighth century. He’s died of violence, pestilence, and misfortune. He slipped in the bathroom in 1922 and hit his head on the bathtub. He’s survived eight car accidents, machine guns five times, three plane crashes, and, as of last week, two chemical attacks. 
He sometimes wondered if there was a limit to his lives. If somewhere, in some cosmic ledger, there was a number. A final count.
His fear, the one that woke him at 3 AM, the one he never spoke aloud, was that he wouldn't know when this life was his last life.
What if the next time, the pull didn't come?
What if the void kept him? Would he know beforehand? Would his last life feel different than this one? Would he rush towards death if this were his last life, or make something wonderful with it?
He was often jealous of humans with their minuscule lives. They accomplish so much in so little time. His people had grown complacent with endless time. Procrastination wasn’t a flaw; it was design. Why work on something today, when you have eons to do it?
Noah exhaled. Let go of the seat.
Not today. Today, he'd come back.
Tomorrow was another question.
"You good?" Martin's voice, low and steady.
Noah nodded. Didn't trust his voice yet.
Martin studied him for a moment, then clapped him on the shoulder. "Get some rest. We'll talk in the morning."
Noah nodded again. Watched as Martin, Ben, and Sabrina headed toward the hotel entrance. Their movements were slow and exhausted. They’d only have to experience the roller coaster from the ground, and their reserves were depleted.
Miranda lingered by the Land Rover. She hadn't said a word since they left the cemetery.
Noah looked at her. She was staring at the ground, arms crossed, jaw tight.
"Miranda…"
"I'm fine," she said.
In all of his centuries of life, he had never met a woman who was fine when she said she was fine. Miranda was not the first. She wasn't fine. He could see it in the way she held herself, rigid, controlled, like she was afraid she'd shatter if she let go.
He'd done this to her. Put her in the field too soon. Let her watch him die. Let her do CPR on a corpse that wouldn't stay dead.
This wasn't the plan.
He should say something. Apologize. Explain.
But what was there to say?
I'm sorry you had to watch me die. I wish I could say differently, but if you stay, it won't be the last time.
Miranda turned. She opened her mouth as if to say something.
Noah straightened his back to give her his attention.
Miranda turned and walked toward the hotel without another word.
Noah exhaled. Rubbed his face.
He needed food. Sleep. A shower.
He needed to think.
Noah's suite was on the top floor. Corner unit. Large for the location, but smaller than most luxury suites. Functional.
Bed, desk, chair, bathroom. A window overlooking the parking lot. A separate bedroom, living room, and office. Marble floors, handcrafted teak furniture, art from locally famous artisans.
He dropped his duffel on the floor, locked the door, and stood in the center of the room for a moment, just breathing.
His body was screaming at him.
Hungry. Tired. Thirsty.
Resurrection always did this. Burned through his reserves like a wildfire. His body had to rebuild itself—cells, tissue, blood, bone—and it needed fuel.
He picked up the menu, got on the phone, and ordered room service: pitchers of water and juice, a bottle of wine, Grilled Tiger Prawns, Avocado & Tomato Salad, Beef Brochettes, Poulet Yassa, Grilled Captain Fish, Domoda, Fried Plantains, Jasmine Rice, Roasted Sweet Potatoes, and a tub of vanilla ice cream.
Then he stripped off his shirt—the clean one he'd changed into after the market—and walked to the bathroom.
The mirror showed him what he already knew: no wounds. No scars. Just smooth skin where the bullets had torn through him hours ago.
He touched his chest. Right where the first shot had hit.
Nothing.
He remembered the impact. The shock. The pain. The way his knees had buckled.
He remembered the moment the bullet entered his brain—the instant blackness. He often felt like if anyone should know what happens after death, it should be immortals. Instead, it was usually a fade to black, and then waking up in the movie's next scene. This black was more of a door slamming, and a gradual reopening.
He remembered the void. The cold. The silence.
And then—
Breath.
Pain.
Life.
He gripped the edge of the sink.
Forty-eight times since he'd met Martin. Wait… Forty-nine now?
He'd lost count.

Room service arrived twenty minutes later. Noah tipped the group of staff that had dropped all of it off, locked the door, and ate standing at the desk.
He ate the prawns first, then the brochettes. Poulett Yassa wasn’t far behind. His body was still screaming for more, but the edge was off.
He sat on the bed. Stared at the wall.
Miranda.
She'd been rattled. Badly. He'd seen it in her eyes, the shock, the confusion, the fear.
Martin told him she'd done CPR, then given up when it was obvious he was gone. 
Then he'd sat up. Gasped. Lived.
Noah rubbed his face.
He needed her. That was the truth of it.
Not just because she was good, she was everything advertised, and then some. Sharp instincts, quick thinking, steady under pressure.
Immortals cannot affect other immortals with their gifts. Noah was blind.
Tomas was out there. Somewhere. Planning something.
And Noah couldn't see it. His brother was a strategic thinker. Devious. Patient. He played the long game, set traps within traps, left breadcrumbs that led nowhere. Or worse, led exactly where he wanted you to go.
Noah had been chasing him for centuries. And every time he thought he was close, Tomas slipped away. Every time he’d killed him, Tomas had come back like a cockroach. He’d known about Tomas and Vanguard for some time. He’d kept them in his peripheral vision. He hadn’t been watching close enough; now over 800 humans were dead.
Tomas had been three steps ahead the whole time, and Noah had walked right into his trap.
The Bamako site. The Vanguard presence. The attack in the market.
Was it a breadcrumb? A distraction? A message?
Noah didn't know.
And that terrified him.
Because if Tomas was manipulating him—if this whole thing was a setup—then Noah needed someone who could see what he couldn't.
Someone with fresh eyes. Unclouded by millennia of history and hatred.
Miranda.
But he'd just traumatized her. Again.
Noah stood. Paced to the window. Looked out at the parking lot.
Empty. Quiet.
Somewhere out there, Tomas was watching. Waiting.
Noah could feel it.
Not literally—he didn't have heightened senses, no supernatural awareness. Just experience. Instinct.
His brother was close.
And Noah had no idea what he was planning.
A knock at the door.
Noah turned. Checked his watch. 21:47.
He walked to the door, looked through the peephole.
Miranda.
He opened the door.
She stood in the hallway, arms crossed, jaw set. She'd changed clothes—jeans, a loose shirt, hair pulled back. She looked exhausted.
"Can I come in?" she asked.
Noah stepped aside.
She walked in, looked around the room, then turned to face him.
"I need to know something," she said.
Noah waited.
"Are you going to die again?"
Noah exhaled. "Probably."
"Soon?"
"I don't know."
Miranda's jaw tightened. "That's not good enough."
"It's the truth."
"Then give me a better truth." Her voice was sharp. Angry. "Because I just spent twelve hours thinking I killed you. Thinking I didn't do CPR right, didn't compress hard enough, didn't—" She stopped. Took a breath. "And then you sat up. And you were fine. And I don't know what to do with that."
Noah looked at her. Really looked.
She wasn't scared of him. She was scared for him.
And she was angry. At him. At the situation. At herself.
"I'm sorry," Noah said quietly.
"For what?"
"For putting you in the field too soon. For letting you watch me die. For—"
"Stop." Miranda held up a hand. "I didn't come here for an apology."
"Then what do you want?"
She met his eyes. "I want to know if you trust me."
Noah blinked. "What?"
"Do you trust me?" she repeated. "Because if you don't, I'm gone. I'm not staying on a team where the leader doesn't trust me."
"I trust you."
"Then prove it."
"How?"
"Tell me what's really going on." Miranda stepped closer. "Not the mission brief. Not the sanitized version. The truth. Who's after you? Why? And what are we really doing here?"
Noah was silent for a long moment.
He could deflect. Change the subject. Tell her it was classified.
But she was right. If he didn't trust her, she'd leave. And he needed her.
"My brother," Noah said finally.
Miranda frowned. "Your brother?"
"Tomas. He's... like me. Immortal. We've been fighting for a long time."
"How long?"
"Thousands of years."
Miranda stared at him. "You're serious."
"Yes."
"And you think he's behind this? The Vanguard? The attack in the market?"
"I don't know," Noah admitted. "That's the problem. I can't see him. He's too good. Too strategic. He leaves breadcrumbs, sets traps, manipulates situations so I'm always three steps behind."
"So what do you need me for?"
"Fresh eyes," Noah said. "You're not clouded by history. You can see patterns I miss. Instincts I've lost."
Miranda was quiet for a moment. Then: "You think I can outsmart your brother?"
"I think you can see things I can't."
She studied him. "And if I can't?"
"Then we're both blind."
Miranda exhaled. Looked away. "That's a lot of pressure."
"I know."
"And you're asking me to stay anyway."
"Yes."
She was silent for a long time.
Then she nodded. "Okay."
"Okay?"
"I'll stay. But you have to promise me something."
"What?"
"No more secrets. If you think your brother is involved, you tell me. If you think we're walking into a trap, you tell me. If you're about to die again—" She stopped. Took a breath. "You tell me."
Noah nodded. "Deal."
Miranda held out her hand.
Noah shook it.
She turned to leave, then paused at the door. "One more thing."
"Yeah?"
"Next time you die, I'm not doing CPR. You're on your own."
Noah almost smiled. "Fair enough."
She left.
Noah locked the door behind her. Stood in the center of the room.
He felt lighter. Not much. But enough.
He walked back to the window. Looked out at the parking lot.
Somewhere out there, Tomas was watching.
And Noah still had no idea what he was planning.
But now he had Miranda. And that was something.
He pulled the curtains closed. Turned off the lights.
Lay down on the bed, fully clothed, and stared at the ceiling.
His body was still exhausted. The hunger had dulled, but the bone-deep weariness remained. Resurrection always left him like this—hollowed out, running on fumes.
He closed his eyes.
Sleep didn't come. 
Every time he died, there was a moment.
Not long. A few seconds. Maybe a minute. Time didn't work the same way there.
The void.
Cold. Silent. Empty.
No light. No sound. No sensation.
Just... nothing.
And then—pull. Like a hook in his chest, dragging him back.
Breath.
Pain.
Life.
He'd tried to stay there once. In the void. Tried to let go, to stop fighting the pull.
It hadn't worked.
Whatever force brought him back didn't care what he wanted.
Noah opened his eyes. Stared at the ceiling.
He sat up. Walked to the desk. Pulled out his laptop.
Opened the encrypted folder. Mission reports. Intelligence briefs. Maps.
Bamako. The helicopter crash. The attack in the market.
He pulled up the map. Marked the locations.
Then he pulled up the records he had kept on his brother and his sightings. Kiev, Damascus, Gaza, Rwanda, Bangalore. Anywhere there was suffering en masse, he was nearby. He limited it to just the last 6 months.
The market. The helicopter crash.
He zoomed the map out.
What was this? Batouma?
Zooming in on the village, he saw it immediately. The unmistakable shape of a C-130 south of the village. Nearby was a fenced compound, with maybe half a dozen buildings, one of them substantially larger than the others. All prefabricated metal buildings.
He went to the commercial imagery archives. There were images of the area taken a year ago. He brought them up.
Construction vehicles, cement pads, well drilling equipment.
There was another from 2 years ago. Empty. No activity.
The drive would be over 12 hours. A helicopter would be faster, but the only helicopter and pilot for hire were both dead in the fields to the east.
What are you doing all the way out there, Tomas?
He looked at the region itself. Flatlands, some scattered forest, then the village itself, with a blue-green lake.
No.
It can't be.
The blue-green water, the same color as the Niger River site. The same algae?
Is there another location?
Noah sat back. Stared at the screen.
In the 80s, he had finally found partners and government support to dam the Niger River and destroy the only location where they had found the specific algae needed to produce the neurotoxin. Until that point, he had to be content with just bulldozing the area every few years.
But if there was another location where this algae grew...
That would be very, very bad.

He looked at the clock. 12:43 AM. He called the 24-hour staff back home.
"Evan? Yes, I need you to get a cleaning crew for the current Land Rover—blood, bullet holes, the usual. And I need a replacement armored Defender 90 V8 sourced and delivered by morning. You have my location?" A short pause. "Good. Thank you."
Noah hung up and walked back to the window. Pulled the curtain aside just enough to look out.
The parking lot was empty. Streetlights cast long shadows. A few cars. No movement.
But the feeling was still there.
The unease.
Not supernatural. Not a sixth sense. Just... instinct. The kind you developed after living long enough to know when something was wrong.
Someone was watching.
Or maybe he was just paranoid.
He let the curtain fall. Turned away from the window.
He thought about Miranda.
She'd come to his room. Demanded the truth. Demanded trust.
And he'd given it to her.
Noah sat on the edge of the bed.
Thought about Tomas.
Akhí.
Brother.
They'd been close once. Racing horses across the steppe. Laughing under the stars. Promising each other they'd always be like that.
And then—
Everything had changed.
After thousands of years, time had not dimmed the memory of when the split between them started.
His family had human servants. At that point, humans were barely forming tribes—sentient, but no more advanced than bonobos or chimpanzees today. But Noah had seen the future, and they were it. His people were nowhere to be seen.
He tried telling Tomas and his parents. They didn't believe him. He couldn't blame them; without the gift of his visions, he wouldn't have either. But still, he knew. Even without his visions, he loved the humans who lived in their house. At the time, they were looked at as beloved dogs are today. Spoiled with food, given attention and affection, but not allowed on the furniture.
He kept trying to convince Tomas. But Tomas always argued with him.
Finally, Tomas decided to prove his point.
He took one of the humans by the arm and punched her in the face. She fell to the ground.
"See?" he said. "They're nothing. No better than big dogs. They won't even fight back."
He grabbed a dagger from the wall and slit her throat.
"Their lives are meaningless. They will never rule us; to think otherwise is blasphemy."
"No!" Noah had cried. "Why are you so cruel? Stop it!"
Tomas caught another as the man tried to run away and stabbed him through the skull. He dropped.
Then he killed another, and another.
Twelve.
Twelve humans dead.
Because Tomas was afraid of a future in which their people would be irrelevant.
Noah left home shortly after. Then the cataclysm came—just as he'd foreseen. His people were nearly wiped out. His visions came true in the worst possible way.
Homo sapiens thrived.
Homo extremis became fiction.
Tomas hadn't changed. But Noah had adapted.
He'd had so many names and stories he could hardly remember them all. Marcus, Orion, Theodore, Noah—all different, all him. Different faces, different lives, different centuries. He'd learned to bend, to shift, to become whoever he needed to be to survive.
Tomas, as far as he knew, had never changed his name. He'd adopted different last names, but only because they'd never had last names to begin with. Tomas was Tomas. Always. Unchanging. Unyielding.
Noah is the ocean. Tomas is Gibraltar.
Millennia of hatred. Of hunting each other. Of trying to kill each other, even though neither of them could die. A wound that still festered after all of this time.
Tomas had killed people Noah had cared about. Not just when they were young, but throughout history. Roman legions, soldiers he served with in World War II, friends, lovers. Tomas treated them like weeds in a field.
Noah could never bring himself to hurt Tomas, not like that. Not that Tomas had anyone. No friends. No lovers. No one he cared about enough to make him vulnerable. Tomas had made himself invulnerable by caring about nothing and no one.
Noah only ever went after Tomas. Never his allies. Never bystanders. The fight was between them, and Noah kept it that way. He tried to contain the destruction, to keep the rage focused.
Tomas was like bricks falling from the sky. Never a thought about where they landed, what they crushed, or who they killed, as long as one of them hit Noah.
Over and over. For thousands of years.
Tomas tried to prove a point that he'd already lost. Noah protected what he could and tried to stop the madness at its source.
It wasn't even about the original wound anymore. It was just... momentum. Inertia. The weight of history.
Noah rubbed his face.
He was tired.
Not just physically.
But tired of this. Of chasing his brother. Of being three steps behind. Of dying and coming back and dying again.
Tired of the void.
Tired of the pull.
Tired of living.
But he couldn't stop.
Because if he stopped, Tomas would win.
And Noah couldn't let that happen.
Noah stood. Walked to the bathroom. Splashed cold water on his face.
His phone rang. He looked at the clock. 1:19 AM. He picked up.
"We already had a backup vehicle on standby," Evan said. "We can have it there by 8 AM. Looking at the logistics and cost, it would be cheaper to just buy the rental vehicle you have."
Noah thought for a moment. "Do it. We’ll have a 2-day drive, make sure it’s stocked with provisions and extra fuel. Thank you, Evan. Good work."

Noah looked at himself in the mirror.
Same face he'd had for ten thousand years. A few lines around the eyes—cosmetic, not real aging. His body reset every time he died, but the stress left its mark in other ways.
He looked tired.
He was tired.
But he couldn't rest. Not yet.
In a few hours, he would lay out what he had found to the team. In the meantime, he would let them sleep; they were going to need it. And if Tomas was laying a trap, Noah would walk into it.
Because that's what he always did.
He turned off the bathroom light. Walked back to the bed.
Lay down. Closed his eyes.
This time, sleep came.
He was on the steppe again.
Grass stretched endlessly in every direction. Sky so blue it hurt to look at.
Tomas was there. Younger. Sixteen summers. Grinning.
"You'll never catch me!" he shouted.
Noah urged his horse faster. "I don't need to catch you, akhí. I just need to outlast you."
Tomas laughed.
They rode until the sun set. Until the stars came out.
They sat together on the grass, breathing hard, staring up at the sky.
"Do you think we'll always be like this?" Tomas asked quietly.
Noah didn't answer right away. Then: "Yes."
Tomas smiled. "Good."
Noah woke.
The room was dark. The clock on the nightstand read 03:12.
He lay there for a moment, staring at the ceiling.
The dream lingered. The sound of Tomas's laugh. The warmth of the sun. The promise they'd made.
Do you think we'll always be like this?
Yes.
Noah closed his eyes.
They were still like that. In a way. Still chasing. Still taunting. Still brothers. Still bound together.
Just not the way they'd hoped.

Noah woke at 07:00. Showered. Dressed. Ate the leftover fries from last night's room service (cold, but he didn't care).
His body still ached, but the worst of it had passed. He could function now.
He grabbed his phone. Checked messages.
Martin: We’re at breakfast. 
That was 15 minutes ago. 
Noah replied, "I'll be down shortly."
He walked to the window. Pulled the curtain aside.
The parking lot was waking up. A few people loading cars. The sun just starting to rise.
He scanned the area. Habit. Looking for threats.
Nothing.
But the feeling was still there.
The unease.
Someone was watching.
Noah let the curtain fall.
He grabbed his duffel. Opened the door.
Stepped into the hallway.
And closed the door behind him.

chapters.title: Lendemain
chapters.content:
## Azalai Hotel Bamako, 0800
Miranda approached the table on autopilot. Hunger had reduced her world to a single imperative: FOOD. Four days of bad sleep and worse nutrition had left her mentally threadbare. If she’d been a computer, the fans would be screaming while the screen was locked.
Everyone else was already eating. They weren’t talking. They were waiting.
She didn’t have the energy to finesse it.
“Good morning,” she said, reaching for a plate. “I know you’re all wondering. I’m fine. Not great, but functional. I’m not going anywhere, and I’m not reporting this up the chain. I need time to process, but I will. So—yes. I’m here.”
The tension around the table eased in a way she could feel more than see.
Sabrina smiled slightly. “I won’t pretend we weren’t curious. Thank you for addressing it directly.” She lifted her fork. “I’m glad you’re here.”
Martin set his coffee down. “For what it’s worth, I’m sorry about yesterday. That was not the sequence we had planned. You were supposed to be vetted before being exposed to the… broader reality.”
Miranda glanced at him. “So the intent wasn’t to traumatize me?”
“No—what? Absolutely not.”
She smiled, just enough.
Martin stopped, looked around, then exhaled.
Ben laughed despite himself, earning a few confused looks from nearby tables.
“I couldn’t resist,” Miranda said. “Look—what happened happened. Decisions were made under pressure. I get why.” She paused, then leaned forward. “But we’re past that now. There’s a chemical weapon loose. It’s already been used once. Whatever comes next won’t be theoretical.”
Sabrina swallowed and set her fork down. “I found something last night. I was working with Simon back home. We followed the money trail. There’s another facility in Mali.”
Noah’s voice came from behind her. “Batouma.”
Sabrina flinched. “Jesus, Noah, how long have you been standing there?”
“Long enough.” He sat, setting down a plate piled high with food. 
She frowned. “How did you…”
“I track Tomas indirectly. Nothing formal, just open reporting, regional chatter, Vanguard sightings. There was a hit in Batouma two weeks ago.” He slid a set of printouts onto the table. “I pulled commercial imagery after that.”
The images showed a cluster of low structures where scrubland had been a year earlier. On the next page, farther out, a familiar silhouette: a C-130 parked on an improvised strip. To the northeast, a shallow blue-green pond.
“I believe that’s our aircraft,” Noah said. “And that pond is the problem.”
Miranda’s head came up. “A pond is the problem?”
He nodded. “The original source came from algae formed under extremely specific microclimatic conditions. We only ever found one location. I had a dam constructed upstream decades ago to eliminate it permanently. Flooded the basin, buried the site, destroyed the conditions.”
Miranda stared at him. “The Roman account. That was you?”
“Yes.”
“Oh.” She sat back. “Okay.”
Sabrina leaned in. “We found the same location in a different way. Heavy equipment rentals. Well-drilling contracts. Short-term leases. All paid through layers of shell companies, but sloppily enough to reconstruct.”
Ben frowned. “That doesn’t track.”
“Exactly,” Sabrina said. “These people executed a WMD attack in a public market without leaving a usable forensic trail. But they let two analysts and a few hours of OSINT walk us to their doorstep? I don’t buy it.”
Noah shook his head. “Neither do I.”
She hesitated, then added, “There were two anomalies.”
The table went still.
“First,” she said, “a civil engineering firm out of Abidjan is contracted to bulldoze the site tomorrow afternoon. Short window. Paid in advance.”
Martin’s eyebrows lifted. “Expansion?”
“Maybe,” Sabrina said. “But the second anomaly makes that unlikely.”
She slid another page forward. "Shipping references. Oblique ones. Containers flagged K-class. References to 'Klub' with a K. No declared destination."
Martin went still, staring at the page.
"That's not a facility," he said finally.
Ben looked between them. "Then what is it?"
"A missile," Martin said. "A Russian cruise missile. They make a version that fits in a standard shipping container."
The implication settled slowly, heavily.
"You don't fire a cruise missile to protect something," Martin said.
"No," Noah agreed. "You fire one to erase it."
Miranda leaned forward. "How do you acquire Russian cruise missiles if you're not a state actor?"
"With enough money, it's easier than you'd think," Martin said.
Sabrina's voice was tight. "They think someone is close enough to justify burning the site."
Noah nodded once. "Tomas wants me to find it."
Ben swallowed. "So it's a trap."
"It's absolutely a trap," Noah said. "And they're prepared to delete the evidence when we arrive."
Miranda looked at the images again. The pond. The strip. The temporary buildings.
"They're not cleaning up," she said. "They're making sure no one ever proves it existed." She sat back. "How are we going to get there?"
Noah went quiet mid-sentence.
Miranda waited.
“Noah?”
He was staring past her now. Not at the cemetery. Not at the team. Somewhere further away. Somewhere she couldn’t see.
“That wasn’t about killing me,” he said slowly.
Miranda frowned. “You were shot three times.”
“Yes.”
He shook his head once. Small. Controlled.
“But Tomas knows that doesn’t last.”
Let the silence breathe.
“He needed me gone,” Noah continued. “Not dead. Absent.”
Miranda feels it before she understands it.
“Gone from what?” she asked.
“From seeing,” he said.
Miranda’s stomach tightened.
“I don’t see my own death,” Noah went on. “I never have. I see consequences. Ripples. Decisions that hurt other people.”
His voice dropped.
“But… When I’m dead, anything that would have caused me to have a vision, goes unseen.”
Miranda swallowed. “All of that to just..:”
“Kill the messenger.”
“Ben looked around and whispered, “The CIA spooks are back. My left shoulder, by the wall.”
Miranda tried to covertly see who was there, but couldn’t see without making it obvious. 
That scared her more than anything else he’d said so far.
“But something moved,” Noah said. “Something big enough that he was willing to burn you, burn Bamako, burn whatever came next—”
His jaw tightened.
“—just to make sure I couldn’t see it happen.”
“We need to get up there. Now.” Noah sighed. "The fastest way would be a helicopter. Unfortunately, the only one available for charter anywhere near here crashed a week ago. So we'll have to drive. With stops, it will be about 13 hours one-way."
Ben protested. "That car's a disaster. The blood on the seats, the floor—"
Noah interrupted, nodding toward the hotel entrance, where a new Land Rover was pulling up to the curb. "I've already arranged transportation. We leave in 15. Dress comfortably. "

chapters.title: Signals and Noise
chapters.content:
## Hotel — Bamako
Noah had been back in the room for hours.
That was the problem.
The suite’s sitting area wasn’t meant for pacing. Too small, too carefully arranged—an armchair that had never held anyone, a low table staged with a bowl of green fruit no one would eat. He still wore the same clothes from downstairs, minus the shoes. The tile under his feet felt cold even through the rug’s thin edge.
Miranda sat in the corner where she could see the door and the balcony without looking like she was trying. Her phone lay face down on her thigh. Every few minutes, she shifted it a centimeter, as if the weight bothered her.
The blackout curtain wasn’t fully closed. A strip of Bamako’s night leaked in—streetlight, passing headlights, the pulse of a generator somewhere nearby.
Sabrina had claimed the writing desk. The hotel’s lamp cast a hard oval of light over three phones, a tablet, and a small fold-out keyboard. She’d turned the room’s television into a second monitor; muted, it showed a map with colored overlays and scrolling timestamps.
Noah stopped at the coffee table and stared at the hotel clock on the wall. Cheap plastic. Second hand ticking with confidence.
On the tablet, Sabrina had pinned a timer anyway.
00:10:07.
Ten minutes and seven seconds.
He rubbed his thumb across his fingertips, then opened and closed his hand, like he could work stiffness out of something deeper than muscle. His eyes kept returning to the number, the way a tongue worries a sore tooth.
“Run it again,” he said.
Sabrina didn’t glance up. “Same inputs, same outputs.”
“Run it again.”
Her pause was brief—an exhale more than a sigh—before her fingers moved. The tablet chimed softly in the quiet suite.
“OPIR first,” she said. “Thermal bloom. Low confidence. Right on the edge. Two systems logged it as noise. One flagged it. The timestamp falls inside your window.”
Noah’s mouth tightened.
“Radar?” he asked.
“Nothing that holds.” She tapped the screen and a small line graph replaced the map. “One airborne track lasted eleven seconds before it got swallowed by terrain return. Analyst note says ‘clutter.’”
Noah leaned in, close enough that Miranda could see the thin line of sweat drying at his hairline.
“Signals,” he said.
Sabrina’s eyes flicked to a different pane. “Transient spike. Could be telemetry. Could be an atmospheric artifact. Could be any one of a dozen things you don’t get to prove from a hotel desk.”
His hand came down on the coffee table. Not a slam, but hard enough to rattle a spoon in a saucer left untouched since check-in.
“Something happened,” he said. “Three systems don’t all fail politely in the same ten minutes.”
Sabrina looked at him now, expression flat in the lamplight. “I’m not arguing with you.”
“Then why can’t anyone say what it was?”
“Because nobody wants their name attached to ‘unexplained hostile action during a predictive gap.’” She kept her voice low, as if the walls might carry. “Not in writing. Not on a record that survives a change in administration.”
Noah turned away and dragged his hand through his hair until it stood up in uneven ridges. His gaze went past the desk, past the curtain gap, to the smeared reflection of the room in the window: three figures boxed inside a lit aquarium, city darkness pressing in.
“I need someone senior,” he said. The words came out rougher than he meant. “Someone who can move assets without a memo.”
Sabrina’s hands stilled over the keyboard. “Who?”
He stared at the kitchenette—mini-fridge, kettle, a row of identical water bottles—like the answer might be there if he looked long enough.
His mouth opened. Closed.
