Miranda saw the hesitation catch in him the way it always did right before anger—a brief, vulnerable misfire.
“I don’t know who to call,” Noah said.
Silence settled into the suite. Even the street noise seemed to thin, as if the city was holding its breath.
Miranda pushed herself out of the chair. “Then stop calling.”
He shot her a look sharp enough to cut. “That’s not helpful.”
“It is,” she said, and crossed the room without hurrying. “You’re asking the wrong question.”
Sabrina didn’t interrupt. She watched, steady, from behind the desk.
Noah held Miranda’s gaze as if he could will her into giving him something concrete.
“Something moved while I was gone,” he said.
Miranda nodded once. “Yes.”
“And you’re telling me not to find out what?”
“I’m telling you to find out what changed.” She spoke like she was placing pieces down on a table. “You keep treating this like a strike. A blast. A weapon. That’s why you’re getting fog.”
Noah’s eyes narrowed. He didn’t disagree. He just didn’t like the shape of it.
Miranda’s attention dropped to the tablet timer, still pinned at the top of the screen.
“Ten minutes,” she said. “That’s not enough time to wage war. It is enough time to choose.”
Sabrina’s posture shifted. A small signal: yes, that. She reached to swipe, then stopped herself, letting Miranda finish.
Noah’s breathing changed—slower, like a runner who’d hit a wall and was trying not to show it.
“A choice by who?” he asked.
Miranda didn’t flinch. “By people who were waiting for you to blink.”
Noah went still. The energy that had been pacing him around the room seemed to pull inward, condense.
Sabrina spoke carefully, as if stepping onto ice. “We did see movement. Not physical. Financial. Legal. Logistics.”
She turned the laptop so Noah could see. A list of entities, time-stamped, each line brief and maddeningly clean.
“Dormant accounts got touched,” she continued. “Shells woke up just long enough to sign something and go dark. Instruments executed with preloaded authorization. Everything looks… normal.” Her mouth tightened. “Normal in the way a well-planned theft is normal.”
Noah stared at the screen. His eyes tracked the timestamps, the pattern under the pattern.
“Where?” he asked.
“Everywhere and nowhere,” Sabrina said. “Nothing you can litigate. Nothing you can raid. Just commitments. Options exercised. People told to stand up and then sit back down.”
Noah sank into the chair nearest the coffee table like his legs had decided without him. For a second his hands hung between his knees, empty, unsure what to do.
Miranda watched his face change—less fury, more calculation, and underneath it a thin, bright edge of something she didn’t want to name.
“He used my absence,” Noah said. “Not to kill me.” His gaze stayed on the list. “To lock them in.”
Miranda didn’t soften. “That’s the part that scares you.”
Noah looked up. The hotel lamp caught the red in his eyes; not tears, just strain. “You don’t understand—”
“I do.” She cut him off without raising her voice. “You can see the consequences of choices while they’re still choices. You can’t see past the ones made when you weren’t here.”
Sabrina finished it, practical as a ledger. “Because they’re already in motion.”
Noah’s eyelids lowered. For two breaths he didn’t move. Then he let his head tip back against the chair, not in defeat, but in the way someone sets down a weight and realizes their hands are shaking.
The suite smelled faintly of detergent and ozone from an overworked air conditioner. A place meant to erase evidence of whoever had slept here last.
Miranda stepped closer until she was in his line of sight.
“Listen,” she said.
He opened his eyes.
“You’re not useless without it,” she said. “You’re just not above the rest of us. That’s all.”
A breath escaped him—short, almost a laugh, but without humor.
“I hate it,” he said.
“I know.” Miranda’s voice stayed even. “But circling this room won’t tell you what happened.”
Noah’s gaze flicked to the tablet again. 00:10:07. Like a bruise you keep pressing.
“Then what will?” he asked.
Miranda didn’t look away. “People.”
Sabrina nodded, once. “The anomaly isn’t the event. It’s the coordination. Tomas did something knowing you’d come back looking for fire.”
Noah’s jaw flexed. A muscle jumped near his temple.
“And instead,” Miranda said, “you look for paper.”
A beat. The city hummed through the curtain gap. Somewhere down the hall, an elevator bell chimed and stopped.
Noah straightened, the chair creaking softly under him. He reached for the tablet Sabrina had angled toward him and pulled it closer.
“Yes,” he said, quieter now. Not calm, but aimed. “That’s exactly what he wouldn’t expect.”
Sabrina slid another file across the screen—names, dates, the faint outline of benefit.
“Then we start with who profited from your absence,” she said.
Noah’s fingers closed around the tablet like it was a handle.
The timer remained at the top of the display.
00:10:07.
Ten minutes that had moved the board.
And in a hotel room in Bamako, with the air conditioner whining and the city pretending not to notice them, Noah Hammond started looking for signatures instead of smoke.

chapters.title: The Trip to Batouma
chapters.content:
The city let go of them slowly.
Bamako at five-thirty in the morning was already awake. Vendors rolling carts into position, motorcycle taxis threading between buses, the smell of charcoal and exhaust layering over everything.
Miranda watched it from the back seat as Ben pulled onto the main artery heading east, and she told herself that was why she kept looking out the window. To watch. To cover their six. Not because she couldn't look at the road ahead. The Land Rover was new; smelling of plastic wrap and air freshener. She had no idea how it had gotten here so quickly. She assumed billionaires just made things happen, the same way Santa just made presents appear on Christmas morning. 
Noah rode up front. Martin was beside her, door-side, his arm propped on the window ledge, already half-asleep or doing a convincing impression of it. His eyes were closed. His breathing was even. She did not understand how anyone's breathing could be even right now. Sabrina stayed behind to work on getting them more information, she would be checking in via satphone as they got closer. 
She checked her phone. No signal. She stowed it. Then checked it again four minutes later.
The highway east was nothing as she'd expected. Two lanes widened into four as the city sprawled outward, and then the traffic — already aggressive inside Bamako — became something else entirely. Trucks ran loaded past their rated capacity, suspension groaning, listing slightly to one side. Minibuses overtook on blind curves with absolute conviction. A cattle truck had shed part of its load somewhere north of the city; they passed three men in the median attempting to corral a goat while traffic split around them without slowing. 
Ben watched the men and the goat as he navigated around them, "That wasn’t in my bingo card for this trip." he said, to no one in particular. He made a slight correction around a pothole-sized patch of missing asphalt. 
Five hours outside of Bamako, Miranda's knee started doing the thing. She pressed her hand down on it. She'd been fine in the hotel. She'd been fine at breakfast — she'd eaten, or moved food around, which was close enough. She'd been fine loading the vehicle in the grey pre-dawn, checking her kit, confirming comms. She'd been trained for fine. Fine was the ground state she operated from. At some point between Bamako and wherever they were now, fine had stopped working. She knew what was out there. 
That was the problem. Noah had laid it out at breakfast, the imagery, the C-130 sitting south of Batouma like a period at the end of a sentence, the compound that hadn't existed two years ago and now had six buildings and a pond that was the wrong color. She'd looked at the images herself. She'd understood what the algae meant before he finished the sentence. She understood what they were driving toward. 
Her knee resumed.
Somewhere around the seventh hour, a lorry cut across two lanes without signaling. Ben absorbed the correction without comment, the vehicle barely moving. Martin didn't look up from the map. Noah resettled his arm on the window ledge. Miranda exhaled."We’re making good time, right?" she said. Her voice came out slightly too bright. "Are we going back today?"Ben opened one eye. Looked at her sideways.A beat."Depends on what we find." He closed his eyes again. Then, "But yeah. Good time." Miranda faced forward. Something in her chest unknotted slightly. Not much. Enough.
The highway life moved around them in waves. A convoy of NGO vehicles running south in the opposite lane, a school bus painted orange and purple with a sound system audible through closed windows, a man on a motorcycle carrying an entire wooden headboard strapped vertically to his back, leaning forward into the wind like a sail. The world out here was enormous and indifferent and operating on its own logic that had nothing to do with neurotoxins or immortal men or C-130s parked next to blue-green ponds in the middle of nowhere.She kept waiting for that to make her feel better.
Around the ninth hour they stopped for gas, and she got one bar. She watched it. Watched it drop. It came back. She looked out the window. Noah had gone inside to pay. Ben was stretching his legs. Martin was asleep now, head against the window. Ben was eating something from a foil packet, watching the road with the patient, unfocused attention of someone who'd done a lot of long drives. She took out her other phone, the one Langford gave her. It felt hot and heavy in her hand. She typed.
Going northeast. Don't have a destination yet. Team intact. Will report when I have more. 
She read it back. Every word true. She sent it. Put the phone back into her pocket. Nobody said anything. Ben kept eating. Noah started pumping gas. He glanced at her and for just a second, she caught his gaze. He gave her a short nod. She looked away.
The Rover came to an abrupt halt. Miranda looked around to see what the issue was. She didn’t see anything, and turned back to the front. Noah held a gun to her face. She felt the heat on her face as the flash erupted.
Miranda started herself awake. Martin looked at her questioningly. She gave a weak smile and looked at her watch. Two hours to go, plus or minus. The air was cool inside the Rover, but Miranda could feel sweat pooling on her back. 
The afternoon heat pressed in through the glass despite the AC. The landscape had simplified itself — flatter, drier, scattered trees throwing sharp shadows at low angles. They occasionally saw parts of the Niger River, shallow here. HIppos wallowed in the river, while antelope watered themselves. 
The highway was quieter now. The trucks were still there, but spaced further apart. The minibuses had mostly turned off somewhere south. It felt like the world was narrowing.
"Can I ask you something?" she said to Martin."Probably.""How long have you worked with him?"
Martin smiled. "almost forty years."
"How many times—" She stopped. Started again. "
"I stopped counting at thirty," he said. "Makes it easier."
Miranda looked out the window. "Does it get easier?"
Martin’s face took on a look of pity. He thought about it with what seemed like genuine consideration. She appreciated that he didn't just say yes.
"The first time is the worst," he said finally. "Because you think it's your fault. You run the whole thing back looking for where you went wrong, what you missed, what you should have done." He paused. "Took me a while to understand it wasn't a failure. It's just..." He shrugged. "Tuesday."
"That's insane."
"You’re not wrong."
"You're very calm about it."
"I've had over thirty Tuesdays," Martin said. "You've had one. Give it time."
Noah’s head turned, he could obviously hear, but he said nothing. He just watched the road.
Other phone buzzed. Langford. Miranda sighed befire she could control it. 
Northeast of what. Who authorized the route change? Call me.
She turned the screen off.
Martin glanced down at her hand. She put the phone in her bag.
The sun had moved behind them by the time the highway degraded to two lanes and the two lanes began to feel like a suggestion. The trees got taller. The road curved through scattered low forest and then opened again into flat scrubland. 
Ben pointed out a herd of cattle maybe half a kilometer off the road, walking in a loose line toward something only they could see.
"Fulani," Martin said, awake again. "Moving to water."
Miranda watched them until they were gone. The phone had been in her bag for forty minutes. Her knee was still. Ben had said something ten miles back and she'd answered, one word, she couldn't remember which one, and that had been enough. She watched a hawk ride a thermal above the scrubland, tilting without effort, and she let herself watch it until it was gone.
She closed her eyes, imagined herself walking through the compound. She ran through every scenario, ambushes, attacks, chemical weapons. She focused on all of the possibilities, in her mind, they all became real. She opened her eyes. 
Ben slowed. Ahead, where the road curved slightly south, a concrete wall rose out of the scrubland. Three meters high. The top edge ran with razor wire that caught the last of the sun and threw it back in flat, hard glints. Behind it, just visible above the roofline was the peak of a prefabricated metal building.
“You have arrived at your destination.”
Ben pulled off the road. Stopped. Cut the engine.
They sat. The gate was open. Not ajar, open. Almost… inviting. 
It looked as if someone had left in a hurry, or wanted them to think so, or wanted them to think about what it meant that they were thinking about it.
The sun was low and golden in the trees next to the road a bord called loudly, “cow-cow-cow.” Other than that, nothing stirred, not even the wind. 
"Well," Ben said. Nobody answered that.
Miranda looked at the gate. She flexed and unflexed her hands. 
Noah looked at all of them, “It’s time.”

chapters.title: Cinnamon, Cloves, Death
chapters.content:
## Aquileia, Roman Frontier, 220 BCE
Noah—then known as Marcus Terentius—surveyed the field about to be drenched with blood and sacrifice. He had worn a dozen names across a dozen lives, but the man across the clearing had only ever worn one.
Tomas.
His detachment of 1,000 Roman legionnaires—one vexillation, with 200 horse and 50 Eastern archers—spread across the open ground in practiced lines. A hundred paces ahead, his brother opposed him with less than half the force: rebels and mercenaries, ragged standards snapping in the breeze. This should have been a slaughter. Noah took no joy in what was to come.
Artentus, his runner, returned to his side, breathless.
“My Lord, General Tomas has indicated that he will not surrender, and advises against attacking him unless you wish for most or all of your Legion to be killed.”
“He’s gone and promoted himself to General, has he?” Noah muttered.
“I’m sorry, my Lord?”
“Nothing. What do the scouts say?”
“There are no cavalry or infantry hidden in the forests flanking us or on his side.”
“Very well.” Noah’s jaw tightened. “Order our men forward. Let’s get this over with.”
Flags rose. A horn bellowed. His legion began to move. Cavalry trotted to the flanks. His archers nocked arrows.
A scout came running, spoke quick to Artentus, eyes wide.
“Scouts report he has a strange machine just out of sight behind the ridge. It doesn’t appear to be a threat to us.”
“A machine?” Artentus turned sharply. “Describe it.”
He motioned the scout forward, voice hard.
“Describe this machine, now!”
The scout flinched.
“It’s flat, with wheels on the sides. There’s a log with a bucket on the end. Ropes around the bucket with a large clay pot inside it. There are several more buckets near it. A crew of eight men.”
“How far?” Noah asked, already feeling something cold gather behind his ribs.
The scout cocked his head.
“How far?”
The scout swallowed, shaking. “Roughly two hundred paces. Maybe a little more.”
Noah’s stomach rolled. Nausea hit him so fast it felt like a hand clamped around his throat.
“Artentus,” he said, voice suddenly tight. “Signal retreat.”
“Sir?”
“Signal retreat—now.”
Artentus’ face wrinkled in confusion. How could something so far away matter? He passed along the order anyway, lips pressed thin, silently cursing his General’s cowardice.
“General,” he said, unable to keep it in, “they’re so far away. How could this scout’s report mean anything?”
Noah looked at him—really looked. There was loyalty there. Trust.
And Noah was about to get those men killed.
“You think me a coward?” Noah’s voice dropped. “If we survive, you’ll hang for that.”
Artentus went pale.
“I know what that is,” Noah said. “We’re all dead unless we scatter.”
Artentus’ mouth opened. He lifted a hand, as if a single question could pull them back from the edge.
It was too late.
Noah saw the pot sailing up over the ridge, turning end over end in the sun. For a heartbeat he could not move. Then he spurred his horse—hard—already knowing it wouldn’t matter.
The clay pot crashed into the ground and burst.
A sickly yellow cloud rolled out low and heavy, hugging the earth, crawling toward them on a vomitous breeze. And with it came the smell—cloves and cinnamon, rare spices, wrong here, wrong now—sweetness poured over death.
Then the coughing started.
Noah turned his head and saw men dropping like sacks, hands clawing at their throats, faces swelling, eyes wide with disbelief. His horse stumbled, screamed, collapsed, throwing him hard into the dirt. The ground shook with bodies hitting it. Screams broke into wet choking. Lines dissolved into panic.
Another crash.
Another pot.
More yellow cloud, rolling and spreading, crawling over boots and shields and lungs. Men ran—and fell. Men tried to pray—and couldn’t draw breath to finish the words. Archers loosed arrows into nothing, then folded over, convulsing, hands at their mouths as if they could pull air back in.
Noah dragged himself, nails digging into the soil, and lifted his head.
On the ridge, Tomas stood visible now—still, calm, watching.
Not frantic. Not desperate.
As if the outcome had never been in doubt. As if Noah’s men had been dead the moment the horn sounded.
Shame hit harder than the gas.
Shame that he had led them here. Shame that he had underestimated his brother. Shame that he had believed numbers and steel could answer whatever Tomas had become.
His brother’s rebellion might succeed. And that would spell ruin for Rome. The Senate would never understand what happened. They would only count bodies and call them nameless deaths.
Noah tried to breathe. His lungs refused him.
The cinnamon stayed sweet as it filled his mouth.
And then there was only darkness.

chapters.title: Ten Minutes
chapters.content:
Miranda walked to the gate first.
It was standing open. Not ajar, not broken off its hinges — just open, like someone had left for a grocery run and forgotten to latch it behind them. Chain-link frame, eight feet tall, razor wire coiled along the top. Open. She felt the wrongness of it before she could articulate it, the way you feel a car about to slide on ice a half-second before it does.
"Trick or Treat" she called out.
Ben snickered. Martin was unamused. Noah ignored her.
Noah and Ben stopped on either side of her. The three of them stood at the edge of the gravel approach looking at the compound. It was four low concrete buildings arranged in a loose square, a comms tower on the far end, dry scrub grass pushing through cracked pavement. Nothing moved. No guard. No dog. No sound except wind off the hills and something loose rattling against metal somewhere deep in the yard.
Ben's hand went to the H&K at his hip, didn't draw. Miranda drew her Glock. MArtin and Noah stayed together. Neither armed. 
Noah said nothing. He was observing the buildings like they were telling him, and him only a story. Flat, systematic, left to right. Whatever he found, he kept to himself. His jaw was tight.
Miranda scanned the fence line. No cuts in the wire. No sign of forced entry or hasty exit. Just the gate, open, like an invitation.
"Could be a trap," Ben offered.
"Could be," Noah said.
Neither of them moved.
Miranda pushed through first.
The gravel crunched loud under her boots. She counted her steps without meaning to — eight, nine, ten — before she was fully inside, the gate behind her. The air inside the compound was different. Stale. Hot in a way the outside heat wasn't, like the concrete had been holding it. Something chemical underneath, not sharp, just present. Solvent, maybe. Or plastic baking in the sun.
Ben came through. Then Noah and Martin.
Nobody spoke. They spread without discussing it, three points of a loose triangle, moving toward the nearest building. Miranda kept her eyes moving. Roof edges, windows, doorways. The windows were dark. The doors were closed. The comms tower had a camera mount at the top that she clocked and filed — it looked dead, the housing cracked, the lens filmed over.
Except.
She looked again.
The housing on the nearest camera, mounted above the door to the first building, was newer. Cleaner. Different from the tower mount. Recent install, or recently wiped down.
She opened her mouth.
Every speaker on every building came alive at once.
Static first, a breath of it, like a throat clearing, and then a voice, confident, warm, precise, and unhurried. The voice of someone winning, without it even being a contest. "Noah."
Noah flinched like he’d been shot.
"You look tired. Did you sleep on the drive? You never sleep in cars."
The voice was coming from everywhere, bouncing off concrete, the compound suddenly acoustically strange. Tomas. She'd never heard him speak before. The voice didn't match the photos she'd been shown. The photos had suggested something brutal. The voice was almost gentle.
Noah had stopped walking. His face had done something and then stopped doing it.
Ben's hand was on the H&K now, drawn and held low, safety off. He was turning in a slow circle. There was no one to aim at.
"Benjamin. Always the prepared one. Put it away. No one's going to shoot at you."
"Keep moving," Noah said quietly. "Both of you."
"Noah." A pause that lasted precisely long enough. "The gate at Niamey."
Noah's stride didn't break. But his right hand, hanging at his side, curled closed.
"Four men. Your intelligence, your call. You were so sure."
"Miranda," Noah said, still walking. "Check the second building. Far door."
She went. Didn't argue.
The door to the second building was unlocked. She pushed it open with her shoulder, one hand on the Glock, and cleared the frame before entering. Inside: metal shelving, mostly empty. A folding table. A server rack with the guts ripped out — wires hanging like stripped copper vines. No windows. A second door at the far end.
"Seventeen months in Ramstein. Three reconstructive surgeries. And what did you tell the family? What exactly did you tell them, Noah?"
The voice was piped into the building too. She found the speaker box mounted in the corner near the ceiling. New. Same clean housing as the camera outside.
She scanned the shelves. Zip ties. An empty Pelican case. A laminated site map under the table leg, water-damaged, useless. She kept moving.
"You told them he died serving something larger than himself. That's a beautiful phrase. I've always admired how easily it comes to you."
Ben's voice crackled in her earpiece. "Nothing in building one. Cleared."
"Copy," she said. "Building two is empty. Moving through."
The second door opened into a narrow corridor linking to building three. She went through fast, low.
"And Cairo," the voice said.
Noah came through the outside entrance to building three from the opposite end. They nearly walked into each other. He looked at her and whatever she saw in his face made her look away first.
"Anything?" he said.
"Rack's stripped. Nothing useful."
A crackle. Then Ben: "Building four. There's equipment in here. Recent."
They both moved.
"Seventeen minutes," Tomas said, cheerful, "from the moment you received the intelligence on Cairo to the moment you made the call. Seventeen minutes, Noah. For something you'd spent three years building. Didn't even call Langley. Just… decided."
"He's trying to slow us down," Miranda said, half to herself.
"I know what he's doing," Noah said tightly.
They came through the connecting corridor into building four. Ben was in the center of the room, standing over a workstation — two monitors, both dark, a keyboard still plugged in. Wiring ran along the baseboards. Another server rack, this one intact but wiped, the drives pulled. Stacks of paper on the floor, shredded. Badly shredded — strips instead of cross-cut. The kind of job you do when you're in a hurry.
"You have ten minutes," Tomas said. "Before this facility ceases to exist. I'd move quickly if I were you. Although… honestly, I'm not sure what you'd be looking for at this point."
A pause.
"Ten minutes, Noah. I wonder if you'll do better this time."
Ben started on the shredded paper. Miranda went for the workstation, tried the monitors — nothing — then dropped to a crouch and checked the underside of the desk.
Zip-tied to the frame: a laptop. A thin one, consumer grade, matte black casing. She pulled the zip tie until it bit into her palm, kept pulling, felt it give and the laptop drop into her hands. She opened it. Dark screen. No indicator light.
"Battery's dead," she said.
"Take it anyway," Noah said from somewhere behind her.
She shoved it under her arm.
"Eight minutes," Tomas said. "I set the charges myself, by the way. I find it's the kind of work you don't want to delegate."
"Miranda." Ben was holding a strip of paper up to the light through one of the high windows. "This fragment. Arabic and — something else."
She crossed to him, looked at the strip. Partial text, two lines. Arabic script on top, below it what looked like a column of numbers. A serial or an account string. She photographed it with her phone, then photographed the pile.
"Seven."
"We need to go," Noah said.
"One more—"
"Now." Not a request.
She took two more photos. Noah had the door open. She was last through it, laptop tucked under one arm, running.
The compound looked exactly the same outside. Nothing had changed. No visible charges, no smoke, nothing ticking or blinking that she could see. Which meant they were in the walls or under the floors and she needed to stop thinking about it and run.
She ran.
Ben was ahead of her, already at the gate. Noah was behind her — she could hear his footsteps on the gravel, deliberate, not panicking, which irritated her, which meant she was scared and using the irritation as a place to put it.
"Six."
"Oh, Jesus fucking Christ," she said, not to anyone.
She hit the gate at speed, caught the frame with her free hand to redirect, came through into the gravel approach outside. Ben was thirty yards ahead moving toward the car. She ran after him.
"It's interesting," Tomas said, the speakers still reaching this far, "how much hope you've invested in a dead laptop and a photograph of shredded paper. That's… Noah, that's not you at your best."
Noah came through the gate.
"Five."
They ran.
The car was a rented Citroën, beige, unremarkable, parked fifty meters down the road behind a scrub berm. Ben had the key out. Miranda was watching her feet on the loose gravel, watching the road, watching the compound over her shoulder.
"Four."
The voice cut out. Static, then nothing — just wind and their own breathing and the sound of Ben getting the Citroën unlocked, the alarm chirping once.
Miranda reached the car. She yanked the rear door, threw herself in. Noah hit the passenger seat. Ben was already turning the ignition.
"Three."
"Drive," Noah said.
Ben drove.
The road was unpaved for the first hundred meters, the Land Rover’s suspension banging over ruts. Miranda braced herself against the door, laptop pressed flat to her chest, and watched the compound through the rear window.
"Two."
Then the sound.
It came before the light — a deep, compressive crack that she felt in her back teeth and the base of her sternum, less like hearing and more like being struck. The first building went and the pressure wave rolled over the car and the windows flexed and Ben jerked the wheel and the Rover fishtailed on the loose surface.
Then the second building.
Then the third and fourth almost simultaneously, the sound merging into something continuous and rolling, black smoke punching straight up, and the shockwave came a second time and hit the car like a flat hand and knocked it sideways and Miranda's head hit the door frame and her vision went white at the edges.
The Land Rover’s rear end went off the road. Stray rocks pelted the rear window, making a mosaic of spiderweb cracks.
Gravel, then scrub, then the car stopped hard against something she couldn't see, the hood dipping, and the impact threw her forward and she caught herself on the back of Noah's seat.
Silence.
Not real silence. Her ears were ringing too high to hear properly. She could feel the heat from the compound even through the car. The column of smoke visible through the rear glass was black and growing.
Then, cutting through the ring in her ears, through the creak of the settling car, through the distant roar of the burning compound, she heard him.
Laughing.
Not from the speakers this time. From Ben's phone, from Noah's earpiece, from whatever channel Tomas had been using. The laugh was unhurried. Satisfied. The laugh of someone watching something they'd made work exactly the way they planned.
Miranda sat up straight.
She was bleeding from somewhere above her left ear — she could feel the wet, pressed two fingers to it, looked at them. Not deep. She'd had worse.
Ben was checking his own hands. Noah was already turning to scan the road, the tree line, the compound. 
The silence filled the cabin.
The laugh continued.
Miranda looked down at the laptop she'd kept hold of through all of it, through the blast and the fishtail and the impact. Matte black casing, crack running across one corner now. Dead battery. An educated guess at best.
She turned it over in her hands.
On the back, beneath a sticker that had been mostly peeled away, four characters of handwriting in ballpoint pen. Small, careful. Someone's note to themselves, or to whoever came looking.
She tilted it toward the light.
Four characters. Not a serial. Not random.
She stared at them.
The laughter cut off.

chapters.title: Breadcrumbs
chapters.content:
The feed was four windows, tiled across the screen. Two ground-level cameras, one disposable drone, one thermal. Tomas watched the Land Rover pull clear of the scrub berm and straighten onto the road before the second shockwave reached it. The rear end went. The car fishtailed. He watched it come to rest at an angle off the shoulder, the thermal camera reading the engine block as a bright smear against the cold ground.
All four windows. All four exits. He'd watched them come in and he watched them leave, and the whole time he felt nothing he could properly name.
He closed the thermal feed first. Then the drone. The two ground-level feeds he let run a moment longer, long enough to watch Noah climb out of the passenger seat and scan the road, the tree line, the burning compound. The smoke column was thick and perfectly vertical in the still air, rising until it bent east. Tomas recognized Noah's posture the way you recognize a song from three notes. Shoulders set. Head still. Processing.
He closed the laptop.
The aircraft cabin was dark except for the ambient wash of the cabin lighting along the floor strips, the kind of light that suggested sleep without encouraging it. Outside the oval window, nothing but black water and the occasional wink of shipping traffic forty thousand feet below. The Mediterranean at night looked the same as it had when they were boys, from the roof of their grandfather's house in Limassol. Flat. Indifferent. Vast enough to make everything on land feel temporary.
He set the laptop on the seat beside him and looked at the leather seat back across the aisle.
The laptop was the right model. Consumer grade, matte casing, the kind that gets bought in bulk for field offices or distributed to staff who'll mostly use it for email. The right weight. The right kind of dead — battery pulled and replaced with a depleted one, BIOS wiped clean, nothing recoverable. Except the four characters on the back, under the half-peeled sticker, written in his own hand. Small. Careful. The kind of mark someone makes in a hurry, not wanting to be obvious about it.
Miranda had found it. He'd expected it to be Miranda.
He'd written the characters knowing it would be Miranda.
He turned the cabin glass and looked at his own reflection a moment, then looked through it at the dark.
The work Dominatus was doing wasn't the kind that got written down, which meant the only record of it lived in the people who knew it, and the people who knew it were careful about who else they told. Careful about paper. Careful about servers. Careful about exactly the kind of stripped rack and shredded document that he'd left in that building for Noah's team to find. You shred documents because they matter. You leave a stripped rack because you were protecting something on it. He'd given them the shape of an operation without the content of one, and a human mind doing what human minds did would fill that shape with meaning. With urgency.
The document strips. The Arabic script and the account string — partial, just enough to be interesting, not enough to be complete. The numbers were real. The account was real. Let them run that down and they'd find themselves in a corridor that led somewhere he needed them to go, and they'd believe they'd found it themselves because they had, in the way that mattered, which was that they'd worked for it, and people trusted what cost them something.
He'd thought about this for a long time. Years, in the way that planning sometimes works — not as a fixed project but as a consideration that runs in the background, patient, that you return to and refine the way you refine anything that has to be exactly right.
The mistake people made with Noah was thinking that he responded to pressure. Noah didn't respond to pressure. Noah processed pressure and routed around it, the way water routes around stone. You didn't apply pressure to Noah. You gave him a problem. You made the problem personal enough that he couldn't leave it alone, and then you put it in front of him and stepped back.
Niamey was the right choice. Niamey had been a weight that Noah carried in a place he didn't show anyone, which was exactly how Tomas had known it would still be sharp after all this time. Not because he wanted to wound him. Because he needed Noah acting, not processing. Noah managing a situation was Noah keeping it contained, keeping it quiet, keeping it from becoming something larger than the institution that employed him could comfortably explain.
This needed to become something larger.
The account string would take them to a shell company registered in Ljubljana. The shell company would take them, if they were thorough — and they would be thorough — to a logistics node that Dominatus used to move personnel. Not assets. Not materials. Personnel. The kind of people who had specific skills that certain clients required, skills that weren't listed on any CV, that weren't legal in any jurisdiction that had signed any treaty that anyone enforced.
That was the part that mattered. That was the part that had to surface.
Not because Tomas wanted to stop it. He'd facilitated enough of it that wanting to stop it now would require a reckoning he wasn't ready to have with himself. But because there were people inside Dominatus who were moving the operation past its original mandate, past what had been agreed, and what they were doing now was the kind of thing that, if it ran to completion, would make the original mandate look like a parking violation.
He needed someone outside to see it. He needed someone outside to have found it themselves.
Noah would do that. Noah would take the account string and the Arabic fragment and he'd pull that thread without knowing who left it for him, and he'd go where it led, and when he got there, he'd be looking in the right direction at the right time.
The cost of this was not something Tomas could calculate cleanly.
He'd sat across from Noah at their mother's table. He'd watched Noah become what he became, watched the institution take someone who had been — not innocent, no, neither of them had ever been innocent, not after their father — but whole, and disassemble him into something efficient. Watched him learn to make calls in seventeen minutes that left men dead in other countries, and learn to live with those calls the way you learn to live with a joint that never healed quite right. You protect it. You don't think about it. You move.
He had no room to judge that. He'd made his own calls.
But the people who'd been displaced, who'd had what little they had converted into a resource for someone else's balance sheet — those were not abstractions to him. Those were not acceptable losses on the way to a stable operating environment. He'd watched that happen up close and he'd let it happen and at some point the weight of having let it happen had become heavier than the weight of what stopping it would cost him.
This was not stopping it.
This was putting a thread in Noah's hand and walking away.
He didn't have a clean name for what that made him.
The aircraft shifted slightly, a gentle banking correction. The floor angle changed maybe two degrees. Tomas felt it in his inner ear and looked toward the cockpit. The cockpit door was closed. The Bombardier's cabin was quiet, just the low continuous sound of pressurized air through the vents and, further forward, the soft movement of one of his men settling in a seat.
There were three of them. Covic nearest the cockpit, reading something on a tablet with the brightness turned down. Brandt asleep across the back row with his jacket folded under his head. The third — Salter — across the aisle from Tomas, awake, watching the window.
Salter didn't ask questions, which was why Tomas had brought him.
He reached into the seat pocket and found the satellite phone, set it on the tray table, left his hand over it. The tray table had a hairline crack along one edge from some earlier flight, some earlier turbulence. He traced it with his thumbnail without thinking about it.
The laptop was the most dangerous thing he'd done. Not the compound — the compound was theater. Expensive, effective, final, but ultimately theater, a way of being serious and untraceable simultaneously. The laptop was different because it was deliberate communication disguised as accident, and deliberate communication could be traced back to intent if someone looked at it the right way.
Miranda would look at it the right way. Eventually.
He'd counted on that too. Miranda first, Noah second, and by the time it got to Noah it would already be framed in whatever terms Miranda had given it, and Miranda's terms would be different from his, and that difference would be useful.
He needed them to be just skeptical enough to keep working.
He set the phone down again.
Below, through the oval window, the lights of the Sicilian coast had appeared at the edge of the frame, scattered yellow against the black, and then fallen behind. He watched them go. A half-hour, maybe forty minutes. The altimeter was dropping in small increments, the angle of descent almost imperceptible, the kind that wouldn't wake a light sleeper. Brandt slept through it.
He thought about what came after Rome.
Rome was the fuse. Once lit, Dominiatus would emerge from the ashes as saviors. That was their plan. 188 years in the making, and this was the best they could come up with. Tomas’ frustration with his “peers” had been noted and ignored. 
Tomas knew where this went in reality. Nowhere good. What use was ruling if there was no one to rule? 
The descent continued. The cabin pressure changed in that small, ear-register way and Covic swallowed and went back to his tablet. Salter had turned from the window and was watching Tomas with the focused patience of someone who'd learned to read the room.
"Twenty minutes," Salter said.
Tomas picked up the phone.
He didn't dial. He held it in his hand and thought about the contact in the phone's memory no name, just a string of digits that would route to a forwarding number that would route again before it landed, that first call itself made from a burner SIM. He thought about what he would say when someone answered, and whether what he planned to say was the right thing or only the most efficient one.
Noah's face, scanning the tree line. Shoulders set.
He dialed.
Four rings. Then an answer, in Bambara Rapid Intervention Batallion.
Tomas smiled as he spoke in Bamabara, “Yes. Thank you. I just saw some people running from a building that was blown up in Batouma. They’re foreigners, in a Land Rover. They killed a lot of people from the Village. They were speaking Russian. They’re terrorists. They’re heading south on RN16. Black Land Rover. “
The gasp was audible even on the cheap phone Can you repeat that? 
He pressed the screen and hung up. He set the phone down on the armrest and looked at the dark oval of the window. The lights of the coast had reappeared, closer, the sprawl of something larger than what had gone before, a city taking up space against the water.
The city below had resolved into something recognizable in the geometry of it, the curve of a river, bridges lit in gold. The aircraft banked a few degrees, aligning for approach. Rome opened below them, ancient and flat and extending to every horizon, its lights continuous and unhurried, unbothered by whatever was about to land in it.
Tomas closed his eyes.
He thought about the four characters in ballpoint pen on the back of a dead laptop. Small, careful. Written in his own hand, by someone who knew exactly what he was doing.
He thought about what Miranda would do with them, once she understood what they were. He thought about what she'd do when she understood they weren't an accident.
The landing gear came down with a mechanical thunk that he felt in his feet, and he opened his eyes, and Roma greeted him again.

chapters.title: Frogfoot
chapters.content:
The compound was still going up behind them when Ben hit the road. Smoke in the mirrors, thick and black, the kind that climbs so fast you can't tell what's already gone. The new Land Rover had smelled like factory plastic and air freshener. Now, underneath it, the acrid bite of plastic explosives, burning rubber, and melted plastic.

Nobody said anything for the first kilometer.

Miranda was watching through the rear window. The column had spread wide at the top, bending east on whatever wind existed up there. The buildings were gone — she knew it even without seeing them directly. That specific kind of roar, felt in the chest before it hit the ears, didn't leave anything standing.

She turned forward.

Her hands were still in her lap. She breathed deeply. Held it. Exhaled. She repeated is quietly multiple times. 

No blood. Everyone is alive. We made it. 

Inhale… Exhale…

What the fuck am I doing here?

Noah rode with his elbow on the window ledge, watching the tree line on his side. Martin had his eyes closed, but she could tell by his breathing he wasn't asleep. Too deliberate, too slow. 

Ben drove like he was escaping a rolling boulder in ancient ruins. 

The road south from Batouma was still busy with vehicles of all sorts. Some had pulled over within the first kilometer or so to watch the explosion.

Miranda looked at the laptop wedged under her thigh. The four characters on the back looked back at her. She'd photographed them already. Three times. She wasn't sure why she kept thinking about them.

"We're about twelve minutes from the next village," Ben said.

Noah nodded once.

Martin opened his eyes.

They approached the village a short time later, passing through like any other vehicle. Another village was thirty minutes further. No-one seemed to car the least about them. Miranda felt her shoulders relax. 

An hour later, the road curved south and widened slightly, which was when Miranda first heard it. The rhythmic staccato of a helicopter. She tilted her head. Noah looked out the window. 

His satphone rang, a singsong melody that was almost a nursery rhyme. Noah answered after a few seconds.

“Sabrina, tell me what’s going on.”

Miranda could hear Sabrina’s voice, muffled, but the stress was obvious. The only words she could make out were “Batouma” and “terrorists.”

“They think we what!?”

Again, muffled voice, higher pitched, rushed.

Noah’s eyes widened. He pulled the phone away from his ear, Miranda heard Sabrina clearly, “Get off the highway, they’re looking for you and I need time to unfuck this.”

Noah looked at Ben gravely, "Ben. We need to get off this road."

"That’s for us isn’t it?"

“I’m afraid so.”

“Fuck”

The checkpoint appeared around the next bend.

Two armored personnel carriers parked with their noses facing outward. .50 caliber machine guns manned at the top, and nearly a dozen soldiers checking cars. The line was already backing up on the busy road.  

And the helicopter. A long, sleek, European style military helicopter, not the bulbous Russian ones you were used to seeing in Africa. It was circling the area ahead of them. 

Noah turned from the front seat to face them, “Someone tipped off the Malian Rapid Reaction Force to a terror attack in Batouma, and that the perpetrators left in a black Land Rover.”

“Your brother’s an asshole Noah.” Miranda blurted it out before she even realized what she was saying. 

Then a deep, almost guttaral jet exhaust came from the west. Over the hills and trees the squat, angry form of a jet screamed overhead and continued to the east. 

Miranda’s heart was racing like it was trying to keep up with the jet. “What the hell was that?” 

Martin watched it pass by, “SU-25. Russian anti-tank jet.”

Miranda turned. A brown smear of smoke at altitude, a swept silhouette banking into a wide orbit. The aircraft was a stubby, ugly thing,  a flying tank. She'd seen photos. The photos didn't communicate the weight of being on the angry end of it.

The car in the next lane, a battered Peugeot 504 with a cracked rear window, had pulled to a stop in the checkpoint queue. In the back seat, a girl of maybe five was watching the jet through the glass. Round face. Eyes that hadn't learned to be fearful yet.

The girl's mouth went wide. She pointed at the plane. Delighted.

"Oh fuck," Ben said.

Not the checkpoint. He was looking at the sky.

Miranda had time to register the aircraft's nose dropping — the angle of it, sudden and purposeful, the engine note changing in a way she felt before she heard it — and then Ben yanked the wheel hard right and the Land Rover went off the asphalt at speed, suspension hammering as it hit the dirt shoulder, and the 30mm cannon opened up.

The sound was nothing like a gun. It was more like the sky unzipping. A continuous, percussive ripping that she felt in her back teeth and her sternum and the base of her skull simultaneously.

The Peugeot disintegrated.

Not dramatically. Not in a fireball. The cannon shells walked across it at the rate of dozens per second and the car simply came apart, the door panels opening outward and then gone, the roof lifting, the driver's side turning into something she looked away from.

The girl.

She couldn't see the girl.

There was no explosion, no fire. Just dust and shrapnel where there was once a family.

The Land Rover was already forty meters into the scrubland and accelerating, Ben finding the gaps between the low acacia scrub by instinct or luck or whatever made him good at this. A second pass — she could hear it before she saw it, the aircraft banking hard, that tearing sound tracking south of them across the field, throwing up a line of earth and stone that moved faster than anything should move.

"Go left!" Noah said.

Ben went left. The Su-25's second pass chewed through the ground to their right, close enough that dirt and small rocks clattered against the bodywork.

Miranda gripped the door handle. Not for comfort. Because she needed something that wasn't moving.

The third pass was coming. She could see the aircraft pulling up and around, that heavy-shouldered bank, the nose starting to come back toward them.

"Village," Martin said.

She looked ahead. Maybe eight hundred meters, the first buildings of something small — cinderblock, painted white, solar panels, a water tank on a rusted frame. A minaret.

Ben drove toward it without being told.

The cannon fired again, far left, the aircraft correcting for their movement. Close enough. She heard glass flex behind her. A crack ran up the rear window, neat as a ruler line, and she understood the round hadn't hit them directly but the shockwave had been enough.

The rover started pulling left.

"Tire," Ben said.

"Keep going."

"I am."

They came into the village at a limp, the left rear wheel making a sound she felt through the seat more than heard. A goat moved off the road with the dignified urgency of an animal that had seen worse. A woman carrying a load of firewood stepped into a doorway without looking. The Su-25 was pulling up — she tracked it through the cracked rear window, the aircraft climbing steeply.

Turning back.

Or running out of time.

The engine note changed again, flattening, the aircraft climbing without banking. She watched it. Kept watching.

It didn't turn.

"He's RTB," Ben said.

She didn't know the abbreviation.

"Going home," Noah said, reading her face.

The rover managed two more blocks before the left rear gave up entirely, the rim grinding on dirt, the whole vehicle listing like a boat in a swell. Ben turned off the main road without discussion, threading between a cinder-block wall and a stack of plastic chairs someone had left out, and pulled into the shadow of a narrow alley between two buildings.

He killed the engine.

Outside: voices, somewhere distant. A door. Then the whump of rotor blades, the Super Puma coming over the village low enough to rattle the water tank above them.

Nobody moved.

The helicopter passed. The sound moved south, then north, then south again. Searching. Patient.

Miranda pressed her back against the seat and listened to her own breathing and tried to make it quieter.

That poor girl…
 She pushed it down. Filed it somewhere dark and locked it. Later.

"How many troops from the checkpoint?" Noah asked.

"I counted a dozen, plus whoever was in the carriers," Martin said.

"They'll have called it in. More coming." Ben looked at the alley walls on either side, calculating.

They had the laptop. The phones. Three sidearms.

No cover identities that would survive a military checkpoint. No embassy contact who'd pick up fast enough to matter. No vehicle.

The alley was maybe four meters wide, the buildings on both sides cinderblock with corrugated metal roofing. She could smell something cooking — oil, onion, something unfamiliar. Somewhere close.

Occupied.

The helicopter came back over, lower this time.

Ben looked at Noah.

"We need to get inside," Noah said.

The nearest building's rear door was padlocked, a fat brass lock that looked older than she was. The next one had a shutter pulled down over what had been a shop front — she could see the ghost of painted lettering on the corrugated metal, faded, some of the characters gone.

She moved to it. Crouched. The shutter had a gap at the bottom. No light inside. No sound.

She looked at Ben, who brought a piece of scap metal from the alley.

Twenty seconds. The shutter went up eight inches. She went under first, rolling to her feet in the dark, hand on the Glock. Nothing. Shelves. Dust. The smell of old grain and motor oil and something dry. A defunct hardware shop, or the Malian equivalent. She scanned the corners.

Clear.

She lifted the shutter from the inside. Ben came through. Then Martin. Then Noah.

Ben lowered it behind him and locked it in place. 

Dark. Not complete. There was a seam of light under the front shutter and a small, dirty louver above the door that let in a stripe of afternoon. Enough to see shapes.

Shelves of old stock. A wooden counter. A chair behind it, the cane seat rotted through. On the floor, a dried rat and two plastic containers of something unlabeled.

Nobody sat. Nobody spoke.

Outside, the helicopter continued its grid. North pass. South pass. The pitch varying as it turned, patient and methodical. Beneath it, she could hear boots on packed dirt — the specific sound of people moving in formation, checking doors, working outward from the road.

Miranda stood at the far wall and looked at a seam of light that ran between the shutter and the frame. She could see a sliver of the street. Empty. A shadow crossed it, then another.

She stopped breathing.

The second shadow stopped.

Sharp banging hit the door, Miranda finched before she could stop herself. Praying her gasp hadn’t been heard. She slowly drew her Glock. Martin watched. He nodded. 

The shadow moved on.

She released the breath.

From somewhere down the street, a knock. Official. Flat-knuckled. The sound of someone with authority checking boxes.

Then another knock. Closer.

Then a third.

Sɔrɔdasi Polisiw. Aw ye aw da wuli sisan!

A pause.  The door shook up and down.

Da wuli, sisan!

A loud bang made Miranda flicnh again as the soldier kicked the gate. 
Da tugulen don. Mɔgɔ si tɛ yen.

Then another voice. 

Aw bɛ taa a nɔfɛta la.

Noah’s whisper was barely audible. “They’re moving on.”

Martin moved to the wall beside her without making a sound. He looked at the seam of light. She watched his face — still, organized, the expression he wore when he was adding numbers he didn't like.

Ben was by the rear door, the flat bar still in his hand, watching the floor grate that covered a drain. Whether he was calculating options or just needed somewhere to look, she couldn't tell.

Noah stood in the center of the room with the dried rat and the rotted chair and the unlabeled containers and the grain smell and the dim, inadequate light, and he looked at nothing in particular, which meant he was thinking about everything.

Another knock. Two buildings away now. Maybe one.

Miranda put her back against the wall and felt the cinderblock through her shirt, still warm from the afternoon sun. She thought about the Su-25 pulling up, level, climbing. Leaving. She thought about whoever had made the call that sent it — who picked up a telephone somewhere and decided, with whatever facts they had, that a black Land Rover on RN16 was worth a 30mm cannon.

She thought about Tomas watching the compound feed, watching them run, watching the shutter close.

They all froze in place as they heard a door open from the back of the store.

chapters.title: Twelve by Eight
chapters.content:
The door swung inward without drama. No alarm, no voices, just the groan of old hinges and the flat sound of boots on concrete. A thin parallelogram of late afternoon light stretched across the floor and then disappeared as the door swung most of the way closed behind them.
Two of them. Miranda tracked the sound. Two sets of footfalls, different weights, different gaits. One heavy-heeled, the other deliberate and soft.
No one breathed.
She had her back pressed to the shelving unit along the far wall, a rusted thing bolted to the cinderblock that held nothing now but a few empty motor oil containers and a ceramic mug someone had left and forgotten. The Glock was at her side, finger alongside the guard. She didn't raise it.
Not yet.
Ben had moved behind the counter. She couldn't see him, only the faint outline of his shoulder against the lesser dark. Martin was somewhere near the rear left corner, which she knew because she'd watched him go there, and now she couldn't find him, which meant he was good at this.
Noah.
She turned her head a fraction. Noah was six feet to her left, flat against the wall, between a shelving unit and a wooden post. He was looking at the floor. Not cowering. Thinking. His hands were at his sides.
The flashlight beams came first. Two of them, overlapping, sweeping the space in practiced arcs. Military issue, the kind that throws a flat, even disc of white light and doesn't bleed at the edges. They'd done this before. The beams tracked high, then low, then settled into a slow, methodical cross-pattern.
Miranda watched the light come toward her. She made herself small against the shelf and breathed through her mouth, barely.
The taller one — she could see him now in the peripheral glow — had his rifle up.
Wood furniture, which she registered in the absent, automatic way she catalogued things she'd learned to notice since this whole thing started. He wasn't careless. He cleared the near corner, moved his muzzle to the counter.
Don't look behind it. Don't look behind it.
The second soldier was shorter and walked like he had something to prove, sweeping his torch side to side with a little more urgency than the situation called for. He moved between the shelving units in the center of the room, checking between them with the beam.
Heading her way.
The shelf at her hip. She pressed back harder, felt the cold metal bar bite into her kidney. The ceramic mug shifted a millimeter. She felt it before she heard it, the faint scrape of ceramic against rust, and she put one finger out and stopped it and didn't exhale until she was sure the sound had died before it reached him.
He stopped.
Two meters. Maybe less.
The beam went up the wall above her head. Tracked left. Stopped on the mug. Sat on it for a full second, which was long enough for her to decide what she'd do if it kept moving toward her face.
It didn't.
The beam moved off the shelf and swept the rear of the room.
She'd been holding tension in her jaw without realizing it. The muscles in her face ached.
The taller one spoke, low, a few words in Bambara. The shorter one answered. Neither voice carried alarm. Procedural. Bored, even. They were checking boxes. Filing this one so they could move to the next.
The taller soldier stepped around the counter.
She felt her grip change on the Glock.
His flashlight beam went over the space behind the counter, paused, then lifted. He said something. A single syllable. She didn't know the word. The beam came back to the center of the room.
Ben was still there. Had to be. He hadn't made a sound.
The radio crackled.
It was so loud in the dry quiet that Miranda's hand twitched. Not the Glock. Her other hand. A spasm, involuntary, that knocked her knuckle against the shelf bar with a sound like a finger snap.
She went absolutely rigid.
Neither soldier reacted. The radio was already swallowing the room with noise, a fast, metallic burst of Bambara with the warped quality of military compression, someone reporting something, someone asking about positions, and behind it the low thrum that was already growing into a rotor signature.
Another helicopter.
The shorter soldier had his radio off his vest and was pressing transmit before the call finished, speaking over whoever was reporting, his voice suddenly sharp, his whole posture changed. Something had happened. Somewhere else. The longer he talked the more he moved toward the door.
The taller one followed him.
They were out in eight seconds. The side door banged against its frame and didn't quite latch, and through the gap Miranda could see a strip of orange-lit sky and the silhouettes of them moving away at a jog.
The helicopter grew louder. A different pitch to it than the Super Puma. Lower. More irregular.
Then it was overhead, low enough that the rotors pushed air through the gap in the door shutter, the dried rat on the floor rocking slightly in the draught.
Nobody moved until the sound had tracked north and begun to fade.
Ben came up from behind the counter. She heard him before she saw him, his boot on the concrete floor.
"Don't ask," he said, which she took to mean he'd found somewhere to put himself back there that didn't bear describing.
"Another bird," Martin said. He'd materialized near the rear of the room, and she still didn't know where exactly he'd been. "Sounded like an Mi-8."
"Russians?" Noah said.
"Malians fly them. Could be either."
Noah exhaled slowly. He moved to the gap at the bottom of the shutter and crouched, watching the street through the three-inch crack of light. The strip of orange was turning pink at the edges now. Sun moving.
"The radio traffic spooked them," Ben said.
"Something happened." Martin moved toward the side door. He didn't open it further, just looked at the gap and listened.
"I couldn’t hear what they were saying on the radio. But, we've got maybe twenty minutes before they finish with whatever that was and come back," Noah said.
Miranda's hands were shaking. She noticed it when she re-holstered the Glock, the slight tremor in her fingers. She pressed them together and held them.
The ceramic mug.
She looked at it on the shelf. An inch from where it had been.
She thought about the sound it had made. That tiny scrape. She thought about the flashlight beam stopping on it. One second. Maybe one and a half.
She pulled her hands apart.
"They didn't see the side door either," Ben said. "Not until they were on top of it."
"It opens inward," Martin said. "The alley angle hides it from the road."
"Terrific. Our entrance strategy was dumb luck."
"It worked."
"This time."
Miranda crossed to where Noah was crouching and looked at the street over his shoulder. Empty. A single goat near the far wall, not the same one they'd passed coming in, or maybe it was, navigating the village with more confidence than she felt. A plastic sandal in the dirt, no foot in it, no one nearby.
The radio traffic. The second helicopter. The soldiers leaving at a jog.
"Something drew them off," she said.
Noah looked up at her.
"That's not a comfort," she added. "That means there's more going on than just us."
"There's always more going on than just us."
"You know what I mean."
Noah stood. He had the satphone in his hand, the screen dark. He looked at it the way you look at a bill you know is going to be bad before you open it.
"Sabrina needs time to unfuck this," he said.
"How much time?"
"She didn't specify."
"Great."
The pink at the shutter edge had gone to amber. The temperature in the store had dropped maybe two degrees as the cinderblock started giving back the heat it had spent all day absorbing. Her shirt was damp against her back and beginning to cool, and the smell of old grain and motor oil and dust had settled around her like something familiar, which was a disturbing thought.
Ben was doing a slow circuit of the room, not random, checking the structural integrity of things in the dark. He pressed on the shelves, tested the counter, crouched at the floor grate over the drain and looked through it.
"There a basement?" Miranda asked.
"No." He stood. "Just trying to understand the space."
She nodded.
Martin was at the side door, his ear to the gap. He stood like he was listening to something specific, not sweeping for any sound, focused.
"What?" she said.
He held up one finger.
She listened. She could hear the village settling into evening, which had its own sounds layered one over another — a generator somewhere north, someone's radio playing something with brass in it, a child calling out in that particular pitch that preceded crying, then didn't. And below that, below everything, the distant rhythmic pulse of the Super Puma still working its grid.
Patient.
"Radio," Martin said finally.
"Where?"
"Southeast. Two, maybe three streets." He paused. "They haven't gone far."
Ben didn't say anything. He moved to stand near the wall below the high, dirty louver and tilted his head at a specific angle that let him see a sliver of the sky above.
"What's left us?" Miranda asked.
"Vehicle. Most of our kit." He said it without emphasis, which was its own kind of emphasis.
"Sabrina."
"Sabrina's in Bamako."
"I know where she is."
Noah hadn't moved. He was looking at the satphone. His thumb moved over the screen without unlocking it.
She watched him do it. Once. Twice. The same motion, deliberate, almost like he was wiping something off the glass.
"You can't call her again already," Miranda said.
"I know."
"So what are you doing?"
He looked up. Put the phone in his pocket.
"Thinking about what I'm going to tell her when I can."
Miranda looked at the rotted chair behind the counter. The cane seat was long gone, just the frame, the wooden rail worn to a dark shine where someone had gripped it. A man had sat in that chair and sold things for years and then stopped. She didn't know why she was thinking about that.
Martin spoke from the door without turning.
"When they come back through, they'll be more thorough. They were quick that time."
"I know," Ben said.
"We need to be elsewhere."
"I know."
"So. Options."
Ben's mouth flattened. He looked at the shutter, the side door, the ceiling grate, the floor.
The store was maybe twelve meters by eight. Two exits they knew about, one of which was on a street that had held armed soldiers twenty minutes ago. The rover was a wreck two alleys over, listing on its rim. The sky still had a helicopter in it. And wherever those soldiers had gone, they were coming back.
Miranda's Glock had eleven rounds. She didn't know what the others were carrying. She hadn't asked. She hadn't thought it would matter today.
It was starting to matter.
"There's a building attached," she said. She'd registered it when they came in, a shared wall on the north side with a void between the shutter and the roofline too wide to be a doorway but wrong for a window. "I saw the seam."
Ben looked at the north wall.
She crossed to it and ran her hand along the cinderblock, finding the vertical seam with her fingertips where the texture changed. A different pour. The gap was filled but not well, the older mortar slightly recessed. She rapped two knuckles against it, dull, meaning something hollow behind it, or at least something other than solid block.
"A door," Martin said. "Bricked up."
"Or boarded and plastered," Ben said.
"Same difference."
"Not exactly." Ben came to the wall. He pressed his palm flat against it and leaned, testing. Then he put his shoulder to it. The wall didn't give but something at floor level shifted, a scrape of material against the base, and he crouched and ran his hand along the junction.
A piece of hardboard, painted to match the wall, wedged into the lower section of the old doorframe. Old enough to feel like part of the room if you weren't looking. New enough that when he pulled the corner it came without much resistance.
The gap was low. Two feet, maybe. Black behind it.
She could smell what was on the other side. Cooler air. Dry in a different way. Fabric, old cloth, something stored.
Ben looked up. Said nothing.
"I'll go first," Miranda said.
Noah started to say something.
"I'll go first," she repeated.
She got down on her hands and knees, Glock in her right hand, and went through.
The space on the other side was bigger than the store. She couldn't see it but she could feel it, the way the air moved and returned differently. She stood. The ceiling was higher. She put her hand out and found a wall, then a shelf, then fabric, folded, stacked. Bolts of cloth, she realized, running her fingers along the edges. A fabric merchant's storage. The floor had something woven on it, a mat, and her foot found the edge of it and she moved carefully around.
No voices. No movement. No one.
Clear. Or close enough.
She went back to the gap.
"Storage room. Fabric. Empty. There's more space."
Ben came through. Then Noah, then Martin, who replaced the panel behind him.
The four of them stood in someone's back room and let their eyes adjust.
There was a window, high on the north wall, covered by a wooden shutter, but the shutter had warped and the evening light came through in faint, oblique bars. Enough. After the store, it seemed almost bright.
Bolts of cloth on shelves. Plastic crates. A sewing machine on a table, the old manual kind, black with a gold stripe. Someone's livelihood, locked up and waiting.
The helicopter to the south had shifted, the sound coming and going on some air current she couldn't feel inside.
Noah checked his watch. His expression didn't change, but she'd started learning what no expression meant on him.
"Sabrina," he said quietly. Not a question. Not quite a statement.
"Clock's running," Ben said.
Martin was at the north wall of this room now, running the same check she'd done on the other. Looking for another option, another door, another way to keep moving rather than waiting for the options to narrow to one.
Miranda watched him work.
The helicopter came back over.
Closer this time. She felt it in the thin air of the room, the bolts of cloth trembling faintly on their shelves, a loose thread on the nearest one lifting and falling.
She put her back against the shelving and waited, and watched the thread, and said nothing, and did not let herself think about where they were going to go when the dark came fully down and the searchers stopped being patient.
The thread settled.
They heard the footsteps at the same time they heard the door open. 
Everyone froze, Miranda and Ben drew their pistols toward the noise. 
They heard a throat clear.
A single voice, female, familiar, “I've made sure that the soldiers are looking on the wrong side of the village for the next five minutes. We've been watching you with a drone since you left Bamako this morning. Give me the laptop you took from Batouma, and I get you out of here. If not, I make a call and the Rapid Reaction force has an MI-24 level this building. ”
Miranda cocked her head. Ben’s mouth was agape. Even Noah seemed startled. 
A woman stepped into the doorway, with short reddish hair, dressed in tactical pants and a loose black T-shirt. 
Miranda gasped, “You! You’re the one from the hotel.”
“Yeah. That was me. Do we have a deal?”


## The Line (Spectre)
Silvana Carolina da Silva had lived under so many names that the syllables of any one of them felt like borrowed fabric—useful when it covered her, dangerous when it snagged.
This apartment wasn’t hers. The city wasn’t hers. The life she wore today—quiet, forgettable, clean lines, no photographs—wasn’t hers either. It was an arrangement. A camouflage. A lie told so consistently that it became air.
Spectre sat at a cheap table under a single naked bulb and watched the Vatican burn on a screen.
Not literally. Not yet.
But the file did.
It was the same kind of planning she’d seen in PHASE-ONE/BKM: weather models, crowd density maps, plume dispersion estimates, hospital throughput calculations. People rendered as numbers. Death rendered as output.
Her cursor hovered over the folder label:
PHASE-TWO / VTC
VTC. Vatican City.
She clicked. The directory tree unfolded like a ribcage.
A logistics spreadsheet populated with times and routes. A set of high-resolution satellite images of rooftops and service alleys. Floor plans for access points that were not meant to be public. A chemical schematic that made her tongue taste metal even through a screen.
And then a single page of narrative, written in a voice that pretended it was neutral:
OBJECTIVE: trigger maximum multi-nation emotional volatility by introducing uncertainty of attribution at a sacred locus; compound effect via concurrent narrative deployment.
Spectre didn’t need the rest of it.
She’d killed men in alleyways for less. She’d ended lives for money, for survival, for revenge that wasn’t even hers.
This wasn’t revenge.
This was architecture.
She scrolled further and saw a line item that made her stomach go hollow:
DEVICE: dual-stage dispersal; low-rolling heavy gas; signature mask: sweet-spice odor.
Cinnamon. Clove.
Bamako, rewritten for marble.
Her hand tightened around the mouse until her knuckles whitened. She forced herself to breathe through her nose, slow, even, the way she’d been trained to breathe when a room filled with gunfire and you still needed to aim.
She clicked into the attachments.
A cross-section diagram. A mounting plan. A placement overlay of a courtyard that millions recognized without ever stepping foot in it. The symbol wasn’t just religious. It was geopolitical.
Phase Two didn’t mean “bigger.”
Phase Two meant irreversible.
Spectre leaned back and closed her eyes for two seconds. In the darkness behind her lids, she saw the only honest math: if she did nothing, people died. If she did something, she died.
Not metaphorical death. Not “career death.”
Actual.
She knew the rules of the game she was in. She knew the shape of Dominatus. She knew that Tomas was not the only blade, only the visible one. Somewhere behind him were hands that didn’t shake, eyes that didn’t blink, systems that didn’t forgive.
She had been useful because she was invisible.
The moment she acted, she would become visible.
The moment she became visible, she would become hunted.
Spectre opened her eyes and stared at the folder structure again.
PHASE-TWO / VTC.
AQUILEIA / ORION.
The ORION models were the ones that broke when the variable was introduced. The ones that demanded containment. She still didn’t know if ORION was a person or a protocol, but her instincts had already chosen: it was Noah Hammond. The man who saw consequences like ghosts.
And if the machine was built to contain him, then any signal she sent toward him would be read by the same eyes she feared.
Her options narrowed down to sin.
She could save strangers by condemning someone else later.
She rubbed her thumb against the edge of her phone case—a habit, a grounding gesture. Her hands were steady. They had to be. Shaking was for people who expected mercy.
She opened a secure messaging app. Not one tied to any government. Not one tied to any nation. A private channel layered through dead drops and ephemeral keys and a thousand small lies that made her hard to find.
She stared at the blank message field.
If she wrote too much, she would be traced. If she wrote too little, it would be ignored. If she sent the wrong thing, it would be used against the wrong person.
She needed one breadcrumb. Something that could be acted on by the only people positioned to stop Phase Two—without giving them a confession they could hang her with, without giving Dominatus a clean fingerprint to follow back to her.
A coordinate set would be obvious.
A crucifix would be poetic.
A controlled encryption fracture—something that looked like a glitch rather than a hand—would be safest.
Spectre’s mouth tightened.
Safest for her wasn’t the point.
She pulled a small object from the drawer beside her: a crucifix, tarnished, cheap metal, the kind sold in tourist shops and held in trembling hands in hospital rooms. She’d taken it off a man she’d killed years ago because he’d been praying when she walked in. He’d looked up at her with wet eyes and said her mother’s name, though she’d never told him.
She didn’t believe in God.
But she believed in symbols. Symbols moved crowds. Symbols moved governments. Symbols moved men like Noah.
She set the crucifix on the table beside her keyboard and stared at it.
Then she opened a separate terminal window and began to type.
Not a message, not yet. A misdirection.
She created an encryption packet designed to look like a data spill from an intermediate node—something that would point any hunter toward a false origin. She injected time stamps that didn’t match her local clock. She layered routing paths through two countries she’d never stepped foot in. She left a trace that would make a decent analyst feelćnd fall in love with it.
A breadcrumb for the people chasing her, too.
Because if she was going to commit a sin, she was going to buy herself seconds.
Minutes, if she was lucky.
She finished the packet, attached it to the secure channel as if it had been an accidental artifact, and then—only then—she composed the real message.
Six lines. No name. No confession. No explanation.
Just coordinates.
And a photograph of the crucifix on her table, taken from above, the tarnished metal casting a thin shadow like a blade.
She hesitated with her thumb over send.
Her pulse was slow, controlled. Her mind was fast.
She thought of Bamako. Of bodies on broken pavement. Of the way the world had been told it was pesticide, as if calling it something else could make it less true.
She thought of her own life—of all the times she had chosen herself, chosen survival, chosen to remain unseen.
No one had ever thanked her for that. No one ever would.
She pressed send.
The message disappeared into the channel.
For a moment, the room was quiet.
The bulb buzzed. A car passed outside, tires hissing on wet pavement. Somewhere in another apartment, someone laughed—light, careless, unearned.
Spectre stared at the terminal, waiting for nothing and expecting everything.
Her phone vibrated.
One short pulse.
A new message, no sender tag, no avatar, no name—just a single word.
CONFIRM.
The timing was immediate enough to feel like a hand closing around her wrist.
Spectre didn’t move. She didn’t blink.
Her throat went tight, not with fear but with the old familiar sensation of being seen without being touched—surveillance, attention, the predator’s gaze.
Someone was watching her hands.
Not just the breadcrumb.
The act of sending.
She let her breath out slowly, a controlled release, and her fingers hovered over the phone again.
Utilitarian sin purchased strangers’ lives.
Now it demanded payment.
And the bill had arrived faster than she’d imagined.

## Tomas — The Breadcrumb Sin
Tomas didn’t like improvisation.
Improvisation was what frightened men did when plans broke. It was what amateurs called “instinct” to avoid admitting they had no structure. Tomas preferred architecture—layers that held even when the weather changed.
But Noah had always been weather.
The Land Cruiser’s deviation off the road had been small in the feed—just a sharp left, a blur through scrub, a return to the track like a bead sliding off a knife edge. Small, and yet it had changed the outcome.
Not dead.
Tomas stared at the crater image until it stopped being an insult and became data.
Then he turned to the thing that mattered more than a missed kill: the story.
If he couldn’t kill Noah’s orbit cleanly on the road, he could still shape what Noah learned next. He could still steer him—make him chase, make him doubt, make him choose wrong.
And now he had a new constraint: Dominatus was watching.
Not just watching the target. Watching him. Tracking pattern. Measuring deviation.
The message from above still sat in his secure line like a blade laid on a table:
YOU HAVE DEVIATED FROM PATTERN. EXPLAIN.
Tomas had not answered yet. He didn’t answer questions when the questioner didn’t deserve the satisfaction.
But he did respond—with motion.
He crossed the room to a separate workstation, one that wasn’t tied to the drone feed, and slid a small drive into its port. The screen woke, pale light on his face. Rows of text and directory trees populated as the system negotiated access.
This was the part he enjoyed. Not the killing. The shaping.
He opened the file package that had been staged in the Batouma facility—mirrored, redundantly stored, and designed for one purpose: to be found.
