Noah’s jaw flexed. “Can I—” He stopped himself, then swallowed. “Yes.”
He reached in, slow. He touched the cylinder with two fingers, testing for resistance, for tethering, for any movement that would trigger a hidden switch.
The device didn’t move.
That was the most terrifying kind of compliance.
Noah adjusted his grip, preparing to lift it free.
A sound cracked the air.
Not loud—not like a rifle at a range. A suppressed report, small and sharp, like someone snapping a thick branch close to your ear.
Stone chipped inches from Miranda’s face.
Dust puffed outward.
And the world, for one suspended heartbeat, held perfectly still.

chapters.title: Ambush / Martin Hit
chapters.content:
The second shot came before Miranda finished inhaling.
It wasn’t loud. It wasn’t cinematic. It was a tiny, mean crack in the air—like a nail snapped close to her ear—and it hit stone again, higher this time, spitting dust into Noah’s hair.
Noah flinched.
Not a full-body panic. A micro-recoil that Miranda felt more than saw, because Noah didn’t flinch. Not like that. Not when the world behaved according to physics. But this wasn’t physics. This was a message delivered by someone who understood where to place punctuation.
“Down,” Martin hissed, and his hand yanked Ben backward by the strap of his bag, dragging him out of the corridor line.
Miranda dropped, ribs screaming, the taste of old dust flooding her mouth. Her shoulder slammed the stone. She clamped her jaw to keep from making a sound.
The third shot struck lower—stone fractured near the cavity where the device sat half-revealed. A chunk chipped away and clattered to the ground, skittering into the corridor like a thrown coin.
Noah’s hand—still near the device—snatched back as if burned.
“Sniper,” Ben rasped, voice too loud with terror.
Martin’s eyes flicked up the corridor, then to the archway, then to the open slice of daylight beyond. He was mapping angles. He was calculating where a suppressor could hide its signature. He was doing what he’d always done—turning chaos into geometry.
“Move,” Martin said, barely a breath. “We break line. Now.”
Miranda’s brain tried to hold two realities at once: a chemical device nested in a wall behind sacred stone, and a shooter who could put a round through someone’s eye without raising his voice above the murmur of tourists outside.
And then there were civilians.
Tourists. Guards. Priests. People who didn’t know they were standing near a seam in a plan built to weaponize meaning.
If they fired back—if they drew a weapon, if they ran hard, if they screamed—what happened next would ripple outward fast. Panic in a holy place didn’t move like panic on a road. It moved like a wave in a crowded pool.
Miranda’s hand went to her sidearm out of instinct.
Her fingers closed on the grip.
Then she froze.
Because she didn’t know where to point it.
Because she didn’t know if a shot would punch through stone and into a passerby, or if firing would trigger security response that would turn this corridor into a choke point full of bodies.
The hesitation was a fraction of a second.
It was enough.
Martin surged forward to cover the withdrawal—his body angling between Miranda and the corridor’s mouth, between Ben and the open sightlines. He moved with the cold clarity of a man who’d already made his choice: protect them first, sort the moral math later.
A suppressed crack.
Martin jerked as if someone had hooked a chain into his shoulder and yanked.
For a beat he stayed upright. His eyes widened—not with fear, but with the shock of a body discovering its own betrayal.
Then blood started.
It wasn’t a clean bloom. It was a sudden, obscene wetness that darkened his shirt and splattered the stone behind him. Miranda saw it with sick clarity: arterial spray, too bright, too fast.
“Martin!” Ben choked, reaching.
Martin’s mouth opened as if to speak, but no sound came out. His knees buckled.
Miranda’s world narrowed to the color of that blood against pale stone.
She moved. She didn’t remember deciding to.
Her ribs screamed as she lunged, grabbing Martin under the arms, dragging him backward into cover. Martin was heavier than he should have been—dead weight arriving too quickly. The stone floor scraped his boots. Blood slicked her hands instantly, hot and unreal.
Another suppressed shot struck above them, carving a fresh gouge in the wall. Dust rained down, gritty on her tongue.
Noah’s control slipped.
Miranda felt it like a pressure drop, like a room going suddenly silent before a scream.
“No,” Noah said—not loud, but raw, the word stripped of performance. He surged forward, eyes wild in a way Miranda had never seen. His hand went to Martin’s wound, and for one terrifying moment it looked like he might do something impossible—something out of bounds.
“Don’t,” Miranda snapped, gripping Noah’s wrist hard. “Noah—stay with me.”
Noah’s eyes flicked to hers. There was nothing calm in them now. Only a bright, furious focus that had nothing to do with strategy.
Martin sagged in Miranda’s arms. His head lolled. Blood kept pumping, soaking through fabric, pooling under his shoulder, spreading across the stone in a widening stain that looked like a spill of dark wine.
Ben’s hands hovered uselessly, shaking. “Oh God—oh God—”
Miranda’s mind split—one half screaming at her to return fire, to locate the shooter, to end the immediate threat; the other half screaming that firing in Vatican City was a grenade thrown into a crowd.
Her moral center—what she clung to when she made choices—fractured under the strain of those competing screams.
And Martin paid for the split-second.
Miranda pressed her hand hard against the wound, trying to find pressure, trying to stop what couldn’t be stopped by will. Warmth flooded between her fingers. The blood didn’t slow.
Martin’s eyes rolled, unfocused. His lips moved faintly.
Miranda leaned closer, desperate. “Martin, stay—stay with me. Stay.”
Another suppressed crack sounded—farther now, or maybe her hearing had changed. Noah’s head snapped up, scanning like an animal, breathing too fast. The mask was gone. The controlled operator was gone.
Miranda felt the debt settle in her chest like a stone.
This was on her. The hesitation. The attempt to be clean.
Martin’s body went slack.
He went limp in her arms, heavy and sudden, blood everywhere.
And Noah—Noah made a sound Miranda had never heard from him, something low and broken that didn’t belong in a place built for prayer.

chapters.title: Miranda Finds Shooter / Almost Executes
chapters.content:
Miranda’s hands were slick enough that pressure felt like prayer—futile, repetitive, all muscle and hope.
Martin lay half in her lap, half on the stone, his weight wrong, his warmth wrong, his blood making the corridor smell metallic and intimate. She kept her palm pressed hard against the wound, hunting for the artery by feel because she could not afford to lift and look. Every time she shifted, the blood surged like it was punishing her for trying.
Noah hovered too close, eyes too bright, breath too fast. Ben was crouched on the other side, white-knuckled, staring down the corridor as if his gaze could become armor.
The shots had paused.
That pause was not mercy. It was aim.
Miranda swallowed dust and forced her voice into something that could cut through panic. “Noah. Ben. Drag him deeper. Now.”
Ben flinched. “We can’t—”
“We can,” Miranda snapped, and then, softer, “We have to.”
Noah’s hands moved as if he’d been waiting for permission to touch. Together they hauled Martin backward, inch by inch, into a deeper recess where the corridor bent and the line-of-sight thinned. Martin’s head lolled. His mouth opened on a sound that never became a word.
Miranda stayed behind the motion for half a heartbeat, making herself the last thing in the open. Her shoulder screamed as she shifted. Her ribs screamed with it. She didn’t care. Pain was honest. Everything else had started lying.
She slid back into cover and pressed her spine to stone, trying to breathe without letting the breath turn into sound.
The chemical device remained where it was—half revealed behind the disguised plate, like a tooth in a cracked smile. She could feel it even without looking, could feel the danger humming in her peripheral awareness: We came for this. We bled for this. And it’s still there.
Martin’s blood soaked into her sleeves. It made her hands shake.
Miranda closed her eyes for one second and forced her mind into geometry.
The shooter had a suppressor, which meant the report was minimized but not erased. The cracks had sounded close—close enough that the sound didn’t have time to echo into the open square. The impacts were precise and consistent, suggesting stability, a perch, controlled breathing.
Line-of-sight down the corridor toward the cavity. Elevated angle? The chips had hit above them, then lower. That could be correction, or it could be deliberate herding—pinning them away from the device, forcing them to choose between Martin and the cylinder.
Miranda opened her eyes and scanned the stone.
There were vantage points here—maintenance grates, narrow windows high in the wall, gaps where an access corridor met an older service shaft. The Vatican wasn’t built for modern security, but it had been adapted for it. Infrastructure threaded through ancient stone like veins.
She heard Noah whisper something in a language Miranda didn’t recognize—too fast, too raw. Not a call. Not a prayer. Something else.
Miranda touched his arm hard. “Stay with Martin.”
Noah’s head snapped toward her. “Miranda—”
“Stay,” she repeated. She didn’t add please. She couldn’t afford it.
Ben’s eyes were wet. “What are you doing.”
Miranda drew her sidearm.
The weight of it in her hand steadied her in a way that made her sick.
Because part of her wanted that steadiness too badly.
“Finding him,” she said.
Martin’s promise—keep her alive—had become a bloody irony. Miranda felt it as a chain around her ribs. She’d been the protected one. Now she was the one moving into the open.
She edged forward along the stone, keeping low, using the corridor’s bends to break any direct line. She moved like she’d been trained to—slow, deliberate, no sudden silhouette. She didn’t look like a soldier. She looked like a shadow that had decided it was tired of being prey.
The world outside the service corridor continued, muffled: distant footsteps, faint voices, the ambient hum of tourists moving through sacred space with casual reverence. None of them knew a man was dying behind stone.
Miranda reached the corner where the corridor opened slightly toward another service passage. She paused, listening.
No shot.
She took one breath and leaned just enough to see.
At the far end, high above a maintenance stair, there was a narrow slit—an access aperture disguised as ventilation. It was the kind of opening no one noticed because it was meant to be ignored. Behind it, darkness.
Miranda saw a glint.
Not a flashlight. Not a phone. A lens—small, disciplined, reflecting a fraction of light the way an eye did not.
Her pulse slowed.
This was him.
She could see the shape of the barrel now, tucked back behind stone, angled down the corridor. The shooter was positioned like someone who had rehearsed this exact line-of-sight. He wasn’t improvising. He was executing a script.
Miranda raised her pistol.
Her sights aligned with the slit.
She had a clean shot if she took it—one round into that aperture, into the head behind it, before he corrected. She could end the immediate threat, stop the suppressed cracks, give Noah and Ben space to move Martin, to get to the device.
It would also be lethal force inside Vatican City. It would be a gunshot that might crack louder than the suppressor’s polite snaps. It would be another layer of chaos in a place already balanced on the edge of panic.
Her finger tightened.
And she realized, with a clarity that made her throat constrict, that she wasn’t thinking about rules anymore.
She wasn’t even thinking about necessity.
She was thinking about relief.
Relief from the blood. From Martin’s slackening body. From the cruelty of being herded by an unseen hand.
She wanted to execute him.
Not because it was the only option.
Because she needed something in the world to stop.
That desire rose in her like heat and terrified her more than the gun in her hand.
Miranda held her breath and forced her mind back to operational logic. If I shoot, I might hit him. If I miss, he shoots again. If I wait—
The shooter moved.
Just a fraction. A shift of posture behind the slit, as if he sensed her sightline the way prey sensed eyes.
Miranda’s finger began to take the slack.
Then, without looking at her, without turning his rifle, the shooter did something she did not expect.
He brought his free hand up.
A small object flashed—metallic, compact.
A pistol.
He pressed it under his own chin.
For a single, frozen instant, Miranda locked eyes with the darkness behind the slit—not his eyes, not his face, just the sense of a man choosing the ending he’d been assigned.
A muffled pop—different from the suppressed rifle, closer, uglier.
The body behind the slit spasmed, then slumped out of sight.
Miranda stayed aimed, shock burning through her limbs.
No follow-up shot.
No movement.
Only the slow drift of dust in a shaft of light, like the stone itself exhaling.
She lowered the pistol a fraction, heart hammering. Her mouth tasted like pennies.
He’d killed himself.
Not out of panic.
Out of procedure.
Miranda backed up, keeping her weapon up as she returned to Noah and Ben. Her legs felt unreal. Her palms were sweaty around the grip, and she hated that the sweat felt like guilt.
Noah looked up as she slid back into cover. His face was tight, eyes searching her for the result.
“Where,” he whispered.
“Up,” Miranda said, voice hoarse. “Vent slit. He’s down.”
Ben’s breath hitched. “Down like—”
Miranda nodded once.
Noah’s eyes closed briefly, not in relief, but in something like fury.
Miranda swallowed. The relief she’d craved arrived anyway—because the firing had stopped—and it turned to nausea in her stomach.
She looked down at Martin. He hadn’t gotten better in the pause. His skin had gone waxy. His mouth hung open. His chest barely moved.
The shooter’s death hadn’t saved them. It had only removed one immediate vector of dying.
And it had erased answers.
Miranda’s gaze flicked toward the far end of the corridor, to the slit that now held only dark.
“He wasn’t meant to be captured,” she said softly, more to herself than anyone. “He was meant to end.”
Noah stared at her. His control was still cracked, but now there was a coldness in the crack.
“A message,” he whispered, and the words were not theory anymore. “They planned it that way.”

chapters.title: Vatican Hospital / “Alternate A”
chapters.content:
The Vatican did not do chaos in public.
It absorbed it.
The corridor where Martin bled was cleaned faster than Miranda thought possible—shadows of uniforms appearing at the edges, voices clipped and low, bodies positioning themselves as if this had been rehearsed. Not soldiers in helmets, not men with rifles raised, but personnel with authority in their posture and urgency in their hands. Vatican security didn’t announce itself; it materialized where it was needed, sealing off sightlines, diverting foot traffic, turning panic into a contained problem.
Miranda kept pressure on Martin’s wound as they moved him. She didn’t remember standing. She remembered hands grabbing under Martin’s shoulders, remembered Noah’s voice—too steady, too sharp—speaking in Italian she hadn’t known he possessed. She remembered a guard’s eyes flicking to her blood-slick forearms, then away again as if looking too long would make it real.
Ben stayed tight to them, face gray, carrying the bag like it was an organ he couldn’t drop. The laptop. The sat phone. The chemical device’s location burned behind Miranda’s eyes like an afterimage.
They didn’t take the device.
Not then.
Not with Martin’s blood still pumping.
Miranda saw it in her mind—half exposed behind the disguised plate, waiting. A threat still nested in stone while they ran a man through sacred corridors like contraband.
They got Martin onto a gurney that was too clean for him. White sheets became red within seconds. The wheels rattled over old stone, then smoother tile. The air cooled. The smell changed—from dust and iron to antiseptic and something faintly floral, like someone had decided cleanliness should have a scent.
A small hospital existed within the Vatican the way an artery existed within a body—quiet, essential, protected. Its hallways were narrow, its lighting soft. The staff moved with practiced efficiency and with the peculiar calm of people accustomed to emergencies that must never become spectacle.
Miranda hovered beside the gurney until someone—female, middle-aged, eyes like polished stone—put a hand up to block her.
“You cannot,” the woman said in accented English, not unkindly. “We must work.”
Miranda’s mouth opened on protest, then snapped shut. Her hands were still on Martin. Letting go felt like abandoning him.
Noah stepped in close, speaking Italian again, faster now, controlled and urgent. The woman replied in a tone that made it clear she understood exactly enough and would not be bullied.
Miranda caught pieces—“foreign,” “security,” “shot,” “surgery”—and the one phrase that landed cleanly even without fluency:
“Room. Now.”
They rolled Martin through double doors, and the doors shut with a soft finality that felt like judgment.
Miranda stood in the hallway staring at the seam between the doors as if staring could pry it open.
Ben made a choking sound and folded onto a chair as if his body had finally remembered it was allowed to collapse. His hands shook. He pressed his palms to his face and stayed that way, shoulders trembling.
Noah didn’t sit.
He paced three steps, stopped, paced back. His face had regained its mask, but the mask was tight. Every time he turned, Miranda saw the strain in his eyes, the way anger and fear were trying to occupy the same space and failing.
Martin had been the steady one. Martin had been the driver, the cover, the promise made aloud: keep her alive. Now the promise lay on an operating table behind a door, and everything felt unmoored.
Miranda realized her sleeves were stiff with blood. The blood had dried in patches, turning her skin tacky. She flexed her fingers and felt crust crack.
A nurse appeared—a young man with tired eyes—holding a small plastic bottle of water and a pack of gauze.
“For you,” he said quietly, and held them out like offerings.
Miranda hesitated, then took them. Her throat was too tight to thank him properly. She nodded instead.
The nurse’s gaze flicked to her hands. “Wash,” he said, pointing down the hall. “Please. For infection.”
Miranda wanted to refuse. To keep the blood on her like proof she hadn’t stepped away. But she knew infection didn’t care about moral symbols.
She walked to the sink and turned the water on. The blood ran off in thin red rivulets, disappearing down the drain like it had never existed. She scrubbed until her skin stung. She hated how easy it was for evidence to vanish.
When she returned, Noah was speaking quietly to someone on the sat phone—short bursts, guarded, clipped. Not Langford. Miranda would have recognized the cadence. This was someone else in Noah’s web. Or maybe it was no one at all, just Noah talking into darkness because he needed to feel like he was moving something.
He ended the call the moment he saw her.
“Any update?” Ben asked without lifting his head.
Noah’s answer was too fast. “They’re operating.”
Miranda sat, finally, because her ribs demanded it. The chair bit into her back. She stared at the hospital’s blank wall and tried to think like an investigator instead of a person who had just watched blood spray onto sacred stone.
They had found one chemical device. One. A cylinder meant to breathe poison into a crowd.
They had not removed it.
They had been shot at for trying.
The shooter had killed himself.
And still, tomorrow’s interfaith mass loomed like a guillotine.
Miranda’s mouth went dry. “We need to tell someone,” she said, low.
Ben’s head snapped up. His eyes were rimmed red. “Tell who? Vatican security? Italian police? Langford?”
All of those paths felt like traps in their own right.
Miranda swallowed. “We need to get that device out.”
Noah’s gaze sharpened. “Not now.”
“Not now,” Miranda echoed, bitter. “So when?”
Noah’s jaw clenched. He didn’t answer, and the silence in his answer carried the shape of the truth: We can’t do everything.
Miranda looked at him hard. “You can’t protect your people and solve the puzzle at the same time.”
Noah’s eyes flicked, and for a moment Miranda saw exhaustion there—real exhaustion, not physical but structural. The fatigue of being the center of multiple threats and knowing every move left someone exposed.
He didn’t deny it.
Ben’s voice came small. “Is Martin going to—”
The double doors opened before the sentence finished.
A surgeon stepped out in green scrubs, cap still on, mask dangling around his neck. He looked at them for a beat as if deciding how much truth to offer strangers.
Noah stepped forward first, Italian spilling from him like it had been waiting all day to be used. The surgeon replied in the same language, hands moving, expression controlled.
Miranda didn’t understand the words, but she understood the gestures: the path of the bullet, the urgency, the narrowness of the margin.
Noah turned back to them. His face was calm again, but his eyes were not.
“He’s alive,” Noah said. “They’re not done. But he’s alive.”
Ben made a sound that was half sob, half laugh, and dropped his head again.
Miranda felt her chest loosen by a fraction. It wasn’t relief. It was reprieve.
Night came in increments—shifts of staff, dimming lights, the hush that deepened when visitors were expected to leave and no one forced them to. Miranda sat with her back against the wall, arms folded tight to keep herself from vibrating out of her skin. Ben dozed in broken fragments, waking every few minutes like his body didn’t trust sleep.
Noah remained awake.
He moved only when he had to: to speak to the surgeon again, to step into a hallway corner and make another short call, to check his devices and then check them again as if repetition could create safety.
At some point, Miranda noticed the way he kept his private phone separate—held close, checked with a ritualistic caution. Not the sat phone. Not the burner. The older scuffed one he’d used for his Vatican contact.
His eyes flicked to it more often than they should have.
Miranda watched him and felt the cost of proximity settle again: being near Noah meant being near whatever hooks were buried in him.
Whatever Tomas could pull.
Whatever Spectre could exploit.
Whatever unknown system was staging and water-marking their choices.
A soft vibration pulsed from Noah’s private phone.
He froze.
Not like a startled man. Like a man recognizing a knock on a door he’d been pretending didn’t exist.
Miranda saw his thumb hover over the screen without touching it. She saw his breathing flatten, controlled down into something almost artificial.
Ben didn’t notice. He was half asleep.
Miranda did.
Noah stepped away from them into the shadow of an alcove and turned the phone slightly so the hospital’s dim lights didn’t reflect off the screen.
Miranda didn’t follow. She didn’t need to.
The change in him was enough.
He stared at the screen for a long moment. Then his throat moved as he swallowed something down.
He returned to the bench where Miranda sat and held the phone so only she could see.
An email.
No return address. No sender. No warmth.
Only a subject line in stark text:
Alternate A.
Miranda felt her skin go cold.
Ben stirred, blinking. “What—”
Noah didn’t answer him. He looked at Miranda, and in his eyes she saw something like resignation braided with fury.
“They know,” Noah whispered. “Exactly where to pierce me.”
Miranda’s ribs ached as she drew breath. The device. The sniper. The staged laptop. Spectre’s crucifix. Martin on an operating table. And now an email, intimate as a whisper in his ear.
Noah’s voice dropped further, almost to nothing. “I can’t protect my people and solve this at the same time.”
Miranda stared at the subject line until it felt like it was staring back.
Noah’s thumb hovered.
Then he tapped.
He opened the email.

chapters.title: Alternate A: DC Protest Destabilization
chapters.content:
The email opened like a throat.
No greeting. No signature. No return address. Just a body of text arranged with the cold, modular clarity of someone who believed the world was a set of levers and crowds were the weight on the end of them.
Noah’s screen threw a rectangle of pale light across his hands. In that light his knuckles looked too prominent, his skin pulled tight as if his body already knew what his mind was about to absorb.
Miranda leaned in without meaning to. The hospital’s waiting room had dimmed into a night hush—soft lights, muted footsteps, the occasional mechanical sigh of an automatic door. Ben slept with his head tilted against the wall, mouth slightly open, exhaustion winning in fragments. Somewhere behind the double doors, Martin’s body was being held together by strangers’ hands.
Noah scrolled once.
And the world widened.
The subject—Alternate A—was not a code for a single action. It was a branch. A contingency. A second theater.
The first lines confirmed what Spectre’s indicator had hinted: FAILSAFE / DC.
Miranda’s chest tightened as the email laid it out in simple, brutal blocks:
Primary Objective: destabilize domestic cohesion through an engineered crisis event.
Secondary Objective: fracture response legitimacy via competing conspiracies.
Tertiary Objective: install a compliant “stability leader” under Dominatus alignment.
The language did not say “coup.” It didn’t need to. It described one.
Noah’s thumb dragged the screen downward. The plan assembled itself in layers, each layer uglier for how mundane it sounded.
A protest in Washington, D.C.—not a spontaneous one, not a purely organic one. A stimulated one, seeded and amplified by digital swarms that would make attendance feel inevitable. The email listed channels and tactics like someone writing a recipe: targeted outrage narratives, false leaks, manufactured counter-marches timed to intersect.
Then the attack itself.
It wasn’t described in poetry. It was described in operational terms: a strike calibrated to maximize panic and confusion rather than immediate casualty count. Something that would create blood, sirens, a stampede, cameras pointed everywhere.
Then came the part that made Miranda’s mouth go dry.
Bot swarms.
Not just “bots” as a buzzword—an entire orchestrated campaign designed to split the narrative cleanly in half, like a bone snapped on purpose.
One swarm would push a single thesis: the president authorized it.
Another swarm would push the inverse: the response was staged, the responders were the attackers, the crackdown is the real crime.
Two conspiracies, opposite ends of the same knife, both meant to cut the same thing: shared reality.
Miranda read lines describing the spread pattern—hashtags, influencer targeting, deepfake seeding windows, falsified “eyewitness” uploads scheduled for maximum engagement. It was as if someone had mapped the American nervous system and written instructions for how to induce a seizure.
At the bottom of one section was a phrase that made her stomach lurch, because it wasn’t technical. It was philosophy.
If truth cannot be agreed upon, authority can be reassigned.
Noah’s breathing changed. Miranda heard it—too shallow, too tight.
She glanced at his face and saw the mask slip again, not into rage this time but into something like vertigo. This was a scale he could not “pull strings” around. Not with a contact. Not with influence. Not with any private account.
This was a plan designed to weaponize the entire country’s reflexes.
Miranda’s mind flashed forward involuntarily: streets clogged with bodies and shouting, police lines, National Guard vehicles, protesters with phones held high, journalists chasing the wrong story because the right story had been buried under a flood.
She pictured herself in it—Miranda Cruz, trained to document, to constrain, to follow law and procedure—standing in a street where nobody believed the law was real anymore.
She pictured the moment she’d have to choose who to shoot and who to save.
The thought turned her stomach and made her hands cold.
“This…” her voice came out as a whisper she didn’t recognize. “This is… national fracture.”
Noah didn’t answer. He was reading faster now, as if speed could become distance.
The email explicitly named the end state without naming the blood it required: a Dominatus-aligned leader presented as the only “stability” option in the wake of manufactured chaos. A figure who would arrive with the language of unity and the machinery of control.
Miranda felt helplessness rise in her—not the helplessness of being pinned under a sniper’s angle, but something worse: the helplessness of knowing the violence was designed to happen inside people’s minds first.
You couldn’t evacuate a belief.
You couldn’t disarm an algorithm with wire cutters.
Noah’s phone trembled slightly in his hand. He didn’t notice until Miranda’s gaze flicked to it.
He scrolled to the end.
The final lines were not instructions. They were confirmation.
Alternate A was live.
Not future. Not hypothetical. Triggered if Vatican pathway compromised—language that made Miranda’s ribs tighten with bitter recognition. They had found one device. They had been shot for it. Martin was on a table. They had “pressured” the system.
And the system had flexed.
Miranda leaned back against the wall because she needed the solidness. Her ribs complained. She didn’t care.
Noah kept staring at the screen as if staring could rewrite it.
Ben stirred, blinking at their faces. “What—what is it?”
Miranda looked at him and couldn’t find a sentence that fit. The country might tear itself apart. The truth might be murdered in daylight. We’re being used to choose where the fire burns.
Ben read the answer in her silence. His face drained further.
Noah finally spoke, voice low and flat, like he was forcing words through a throat that wanted to close. “It’s D.C.”
Ben swallowed. “D.C. like—”
“Like home,” Miranda said.
Her voice sounded far away to her own ears.
Somewhere down the hall, a cart squeaked. A nurse’s footsteps faded. The waiting room’s clock—if there was one—kept its time without caring who died under it.
Miranda’s body did what bodies did when the mind exceeded its capacity. Her eyelids grew heavy. Her neck ached. The adrenaline that had kept her upright through blood and stone finally let go, leaving her hollow.
She watched Noah’s shoulders slump as if gravity had increased.
Noah didn’t sit so much as fold into the bench. His head tipped back. His eyes stayed open for a few seconds longer, staring at nothing.
Miranda tried to keep hers open too. She tried to hold the scale of it—the Vatican, the device, the interfaith mass tomorrow, Martin in surgery, and now the possibility of Washington set on fire by narrative and blood.
Her mind started skipping like a scratched record.
Ben’s head slid against the wall again, his body surrendering.
Miranda’s eyes closed.
For a moment she floated in a place between awake and gone, where the only thing that felt real was the weight of responsibility pressing down like a hand on the back of her neck.
Then Noah’s phone vibrated.
Once. Twice. A hard, insistent pulse.
Miranda’s eyes snapped open.
Noah’s, too.
He looked down. Not at the email. At a new notification.
A thread.
Miranda saw just enough of the header to understand why her skin went cold all over again: names that did not belong on the same line.
Noah.
Spectre.
Tomas.
A message had hit the thread—no sender displayed, just the system’s blunt indication of incoming.
Three words, clean as a blade:
THE CLOCK HAS STARTED.

chapters.title: Nuclear Fear / The Collector Closes
chapters.content:
Tomas kept the room cold on purpose.
Not the air—he couldn’t risk tampering logs with environmental controls—but the light, the sound, the human temperature of it. He trained his people to move quietly, to speak only when necessary, to treat emotion as a spill that would get them all killed if it spread.
Tonight, the room obeyed him too well.
The drone feeds ran in disciplined tiles across the primary wall: thermal, optical, synthetic aperture overlays. A map layer tracked the Rome/Vatican sector with tiny, indifferent precision. Another panel—smaller, newly installed, and irritatingly prominent—displayed Dominatus compliance parameters that had not existed yesterday.
TEST SUITE // LOYALTY-LOAD Redundant confirmations required. Reporting cadence adjusted. Deviation thresholds tightened.
Someone had taken his operating rhythm and wrapped it in a measuring tape.
Tomas’s hands stayed steady on the console. His face stayed steady. He allowed his heartbeat to remain a private thing.
The junior operator—his designated scapegoat—sat three stations down with a bandaged forearm and eyes that never quite met Tomas’s. The cut had healed enough to stop bleeding. It would never heal enough to stop meaning.
Tomas watched the boy for a moment, then looked away.
He did not indulge guilt. Guilt was a luxury. But he felt, in the quiet, the slow accumulation of something darker than guilt.
Dirt.
He had always understood that every system required dirt. It was how gears turned. It was how men complied. It was how “order” survived contact with the world.
Dominatus had made dirt into doctrine.
And Tomas—Tomas had been its finest hand.
The new thread notification flashed once in the corner of his peripheral display. A message header that should not have been visible from this room. Names that did not belong together.
Noah. Spectre. Tomas.
He didn’t open it. Not yet.
He already knew what it would say, because he had felt the system shift the moment the Vatican corridor fired. The moment the device was touched. The moment the alternative logic had lit like a fuse.
The clock has started.
Dominatus loved clocks. It loved countdowns because countdowns made people predictable. Predictability was the only kind of mercy the system offered.
Tomas stared at the drone feed long enough for the cameras to record calm and nothing else. Then he stood, slow and deliberate, and walked to the far side of the room where a steel door led into a narrow auxiliary space—a storage alcove repurposed into privacy. The cameras here were older, their angles fixed. He had never needed to hide anything from Dominatus before. He had only needed to perform.
Now he needed silence.
He closed the door behind him.
In the alcove, there was no hum of servers, only the faint vibration of the building’s blood—air moving through ducts, power cycling, someone distant walking.
Tomas rested one hand on the shelf of neatly labeled equipment. His other hand remained at his side.
He did not kneel. He did not pray. He had never been that kind of man.
But he spoke anyway, quietly, to the empty air, the way you spoke when you needed to hear a truth in your own voice to make it real.
“This will go too far,” he said.
There was no one to answer. That was the point. An audience made words into weapons. This was confession without ceremony.
Dominatus’s escalations were not accidents. They were proofs of concept. Each theater was a test, each casualty a data point. But Dominatus did not understand the one thing Tomas had learned in a longer life than most men could imagine.
Escalation was not linear.
It was a staircase with missing steps.
You could climb it slowly for years and then fall through a gap you didn’t know existed.
He had seen it before, in other centuries, under other flags. Men believed they could control violence because they could measure it. They believed their models were reality. They believed their enemies would behave rationally.
They always forgot that panic was a force.
And panic, when it wore a flag and held a button, could end the world.
Tomas closed his eyes for a heartbeat and saw a map—not Rome, not Washington, but a globe with bright seams running through it like fractures.
If Dominatus succeeded in fracturing the United States, the response would not be clean. There would be retaliation. There would be misinterpretation. There would be factions inside factions, a dozen hands reaching for authority at once.
And in that chaos, someone would reach for the last argument.
Nuclear fear wasn’t fiction to Tomas. It wasn’t a distant Cold War metaphor.
It was the final escalation risk: a weapon that did not care who was immortal and who wasn’t.
People liked to believe immortality was invincibility. Tomas had stopped believing in fantasies a long time ago. He had watched fire eat oxygen and watched bodies fail when the world itself became hostile.
Nuclear war didn’t just kill with heat and blast.
It killed with aftermath.
With air that could no longer be trusted.
With water that carried poison in its bones.
With a planet that became a slow, suffocating cage.
Immortals would die too.
Not in a single flash, perhaps, but in the long, grinding way that made “forever” meaningless.
That was the true reason he was betraying Dominatus.
Not pity.
Not conscience.
Self-preservation at a scale that bordered on philosophy.
He opened his eyes again, calm restored by the clarity of fear.
He heard a sound then—soft, deliberate.
A knock.
Not on the steel door. On the wall, once, as if someone in the hallway knew exactly where he stood.
Tomas didn’t move for a beat. He didn’t let surprise reach his face. Then he turned and opened the door.
The Collector stood there as if he had been part of the architecture all along.
No gun visible. No entourage. No overt threat. Just a man in a dark coat that wasn’t a uniform but still carried authority in its cut. His expression was neutral, his eyes bright in the dim corridor light.
“Tomas,” the Collector said, as if greeting an equal.
Tomas inclined his head a fraction. “You’re early.”
The Collector’s mouth did not change. “You are on time.”
It was a line delivered without emphasis, and it landed like a collar clicking shut.
Tomas stepped aside. The Collector entered the alcove without asking permission and looked around with the mild interest of a man inspecting a room he already owned.
“Your people are efficient,” the Collector said.
“They’re trained,” Tomas replied.
“Training is obedience that has learned to look like skill.” The Collector’s gaze drifted to a shelf label, then away again. “Dominatus values that.”
Tomas kept his breathing even. “Dominatus values results.”
The Collector turned his head slightly, finally giving Tomas his full attention. “Dominatus values predictability.”
A pause. Not long, but long enough to feel like a test.
“You have produced useful chaos,” the Collector continued, tone almost conversational. “It has been… impressive.”
Tomas did not react. Praise from Dominatus was never praise. It was a marking.
“I do what is required,” Tomas said.
“Do you,” the Collector murmured, and the words were soft enough to pass as agreement, sharp enough to cut.
He took a step closer, invading Tomas’s personal space with the gentleness of a man who wanted the invasion to be felt.
“We’re not here to accuse you,” the Collector said. “Accusation is inefficient. We are here to help you remain aligned.”
Tomas let the phrase sit. Help you remain aligned. A leash described as guidance.
“I’m aligned,” Tomas said.
The Collector nodded as if accepting it. “Good.”
He reached into his coat and withdrew a small device—a thin rectangle, matte black, no markings. He held it between two fingers like a card.
“New protocol,” the Collector said. “A redundancy.”
Tomas’s eyes remained on the device, not because he was curious, but because he was listening to what it implied. A new layer. A new measure. A new way to trap him inside his own steadfastness.
The Collector offered it. Tomas took it without touching the Collector’s fingers.
“This will sit on your primary console,” the Collector said. “It will verify your reports directly. It will ensure that your cadence remains consistent.”
“And if it doesn’t?” Tomas asked, carefully.
The Collector’s eyes stayed bright. “Then we will know you are tired.”
A pause.
“And if you are tired,” the Collector continued, “we will assist you.”
It was not a threat in words. It was a threat in implication: assistance could mean replacement. Replacement could mean removal.
Tomas nodded once, as if grateful.
The Collector’s gaze softened by a fraction. The kind of softness you used on an animal you intended to train.
“There is also an assignment,” the Collector said.
Tomas waited. He did not ask what. Asking would be interpreted as reluctance.
The Collector spoke anyway.
“Noah and his team,” he said, “have become a variable. They are moving through a theater that must remain controlled. Their survival has already produced… noise.”
Tomas kept his face blank. He thought of Martin bleeding on stone. He thought of the device half exposed. He thought of the email subject line and the clock. He thought of how Dominatus called human lives “noise” when it couldn’t control them.
“They should not reach the device again,” the Collector said. “They should not extract proof. They should not become martyrs.”
Tomas’s pulse remained steady. Inside, something cold tightened.
The Collector’s eyes held him. “Your task is to end their capacity.”
Not kill them, necessarily. Dominatus was not sentimental about death, but it was practical about narrative. A corpse could become a symbol. A cripple could become a cautionary tale.
“Disrupt their movement,” the Collector said. “Ensure they cannot interfere with tomorrow’s event.”
Tomorrow’s event. The mass. The crowd.
Tomas’s mind moved faster than his face. This was the test. A lever offered with a velvet glove.
If Tomas accepted, he would be complicit in a mass casualty plan in a holy place.
If he refused, he would reveal misalignment.
Dominatus didn’t need certainty. It needed enough proof to justify its next move.
Tomas let silence stretch—not too long, just long enough to look thoughtful rather than shocked.
“Understood,” he said.
The Collector nodded, as if pleased. “You have always been steadfast.”
The word landed with weight. Steadfastness as chain. Steadfastness as weapon used against him.
Tomas looked down at the matte-black device in his hand and made his choice in the space between one breath and the next.
He could refuse outright, burn himself immediately, and lose any chance of steering Noah toward stopping Dominatus.
Or he could appear to comply while redirecting the harm—sacrificing someone else, offering Dominatus a clean report that did not reveal his true loyalties.
Either way, dirt.
Tomas lifted his eyes again. “I will need a payer,” he said.
The Collector’s expression remained neutral. “Explain.”
Tomas chose his words like a man placing stones across a river. “The team’s route is unpredictable. The Vatican environment is constrained. Direct action risks exposure. If Dominatus requires disruption without narrative blowback, we need an intermediary.”
The Collector studied him for a long moment, as if listening not just to the content but to the rhythm. “An intermediary can become a liability.”
“Everything becomes a liability if it fails,” Tomas said smoothly. “An intermediary becomes deniable if it succeeds.”
The Collector’s eyes narrowed slightly, not in anger—interest.
“Who,” the Collector asked, “do you propose?”
Tomas did not say the scapegoat’s name. Not here. Names were commitments.
“I have a junior operator already flagged for anomalies,” Tomas said, voice calm. “Someone whose failure would be… believable.”
The Collector’s gaze held him. A beat. Two.
Then a slow nod.
“You understand the economy of blame,” the Collector said.
Tomas inclined his head. “It is the only economy Dominatus honors.”
The Collector stepped back a fraction. The leash tightened without pulling. “Proceed,” he said. “Deliver disruption. Deliver compliance.”
“And if I cannot?” Tomas asked, softly, because the question itself was a test. How far could he push without snapping.
The Collector’s smile appeared for the first time—a small, precise curve that did not warm his eyes.
“Then we will assist you,” he repeated.
He turned toward the door, then paused as if remembering something trivial.
“One more thing,” the Collector said, without looking back. “Do not attempt to communicate with Noah.”
Tomas’s face remained still. “Why would I?”
The Collector glanced over his shoulder. “Because you have always liked to be seen as the author of meaning.”
A line delivered with surgical gentleness.
Then the Collector left.
The alcove felt colder after he was gone, as if his presence had been the only heat in it. Tomas stood still for three heartbeats, listening to the fading footsteps.
Then he placed the matte-black device on the shelf and stared at it until his eyes began to ache.
The leash was real.
The implied threat was real.
And the assignment—hurt Noah’s team—was now a knife held to his own throat.
Tomas returned to the main ops room with the same calm he’d worn before. His people didn’t look up. They felt the change in the air and pretended not to.
He walked to the junior operator’s station.
The boy flinched, small and involuntary, when Tomas stopped beside him.
Tomas didn’t touch him. He didn’t need to. The earlier cut had already taught the boy what proximity meant.
“Status,” Tomas said.
The boy swallowed. “Feeds stable. Rome sector—Vatican—”
“Enough,” Tomas said quietly.
He lowered his voice further so the room’s ambient microphones would have to work harder to parse it. Not silence. Just friction.
“You have been flagged,” Tomas said.
The boy’s eyes widened. “Sir—”
“Listen,” Tomas said. “You will take an assignment.”
The boy’s throat bobbed. “Anything.”
Tomas felt the dirt deepen, layer by layer. He could taste it now—not literal, but present, like grit behind teeth.
He could refuse Dominatus by refusing the assignment.
Or he could refuse in a way that looked like compliance.
A sacrificial lamb thrown forward to satisfy the Collector’s need for disruption and blame, while Tomas preserved his own access long enough to steer Noah toward something that could actually stop Dominatus.
He chose the second, because survival was strategy and strategy was all he had left.
“You will move an asset,” Tomas said, eyes steady. “You will make it look like Noah’s team diverted resources. You will create interference—enough to slow them, not enough to kill them.”
The boy nodded rapidly, relief and terror mixing in his expression. “Yes. Yes, sir.”
Tomas leaned closer, voice like a knife wrapped in cloth. “If this fails, Dominatus will not blame me.”
The boy’s face went pale.
Tomas straightened. “Proceed.”
He returned to his console and opened the hidden thread he had avoided.
Noah. Spectre. Tomas.
THE CLOCK HAS STARTED.
No sender. No mercy.
Tomas stared at the words and felt, beneath his calm, the old understanding settle again: Dominatus didn’t merely want outcomes.
It wanted people to break in the process.
That was the real product.
Tomas’s gaze drifted to the Vatican feed tile. The crowd density forecasts. Tomorrow’s interfaith mass: a human sea waiting to be turned into a story. Martin in surgery. Miranda covered in blood. Noah fraying at the edges.
Tomas remembered Aquileia—not as a memory of battle, but as a memory of what it felt like to watch men choke in sweet-spice gas, to watch a line collapse because someone had decided meaning could be weaponized.
Noah had gone down in that cloud.
Noah had risen from it changed.
Tomas had been the author then.
Now he would be the author again, but not for Dominatus.
For himself.
For the world.
For the one outcome he could still justify: stop the escalation before the staircase dropped out into nuclear fire.
He opened a drafting pane. Not an email. Not a clean order. Something subtler—another breadcrumb, another hook, a message that would land where Noah couldn’t ignore it.
A choice.
A forced choice that could stop Dominatus and also break Noah in the act of stopping it.
Tomas’s fingers hovered over the keyboard.
He felt the leash around his throat and the knife of the assignment pressing from the other side.
He typed anyway.
Not yet sent. Not yet.
But committed.
He would place the next breadcrumb precisely where Noah’s soul had an exposed seam.
He would force him to choose between saving the many and saving the few.
Between stopping Dominatus and preserving himself.
Tomas’s face remained calm.
Inside, something older than guilt and newer than faith hardened into resolve.
He would stop Dominatus.
And he would break Noah to do it.

chapters.title: Morning: Martin Stable / They Leave Without Him
chapters.content:
Morning in the Vatican hospital arrived without sunrise.
The light in the waiting area simply brightened a degree, as if someone somewhere had slid a dimmer upward and called it dawn. The air stayed cool. The corridors stayed hushed. The world outside—Rome, pilgrims, cameras, the looming mass—felt like a rumor pressing against thick walls.
Miranda woke with her chin on her chest and a crick in her neck that made her ribs flare when she tried to straighten. For a second she didn’t know where she was. Then she smelled antiseptic and remembered blood on stone, and the memory snapped her fully awake.
Ben was slumped sideways in his chair, mouth open, one hand still curled around the bag strap like he’d been afraid it would be stolen in his sleep. His bandage had bled again at the edge, a faint rust-colored crescent.
Noah sat upright, eyes open, posture too precise. He hadn’t slept. Or if he had, it had been the kind of sleep you fell into with one eye open and woke from before it could take anything from you.
Miranda’s gaze went to his phone instinctively.
No new message flashed. But the air around him still felt charged, like he’d been holding a live wire all night and hadn’t dropped it.
The double doors at the end of the hall opened.
The surgeon from the night before stepped out again, hair flattened under a cap, face drawn with the tired calm of someone who lived in margins. He scanned the waiting area, found them, and walked over.
Noah rose immediately. Ben sat up with a startled jerk, blinking. Miranda stood a heartbeat later, ribs protesting, and forced her face into something steady.
The surgeon spoke to Noah first, Italian flowing. Noah responded in the same language, controlled, respectful. Miranda watched the surgeon’s hands more than his mouth: one hand drew an invisible line through the air—trajectory. Another pressed down—pressure, stabilization. Then the surgeon held up two fingers, then three.
Two to three days.
Miranda understood before Noah translated.
Noah turned back to them, and there was a flicker of something like relief in his eyes—real, brief.
“He’s stable,” Noah said. “He made it through. He can’t move for two… maybe three days.”
Ben’s breath left him in a sound that was almost a laugh. “He’s—he’s alive.”
Miranda felt her knees loosen by a fraction. Her throat tightened anyway. Stable meant not dead. It did not mean safe. It did not mean whole.
“Can we see him?” Miranda asked before she could stop herself.
The surgeon looked to Noah, listened to the question through Noah’s quick Italian, then nodded once. “Brief,” the surgeon said in English. “Very brief.”
They were led down a corridor that smelled cleaner than the waiting room—more chemical, less human. A nurse opened a door, and the room beyond was dim, quiet, full of machines that breathed and beeped in disciplined tones.
Martin lay in the bed like a man rearranged.
His skin was pale against the pillow. A dressing covered his shoulder and chest, thick and immaculate, already marked by a faint bloom where blood had tried to return. An IV line ran into his arm. His mouth was slightly open, and when Miranda stepped closer she could see the slow rise and fall of his chest—mechanical, assisted, but present.
Alive.
Miranda stood at the foot of the bed, unable to move closer for a second because closeness made it real. The memory of him going limp in her arms hit like a delayed impact.
Ben approached first, quietly, as if afraid loudness would shatter something. He hovered, then placed two fingers on the edge of the blanket near Martin’s hand—not touching skin, just fabric. A compromise between need and respect.
Noah stayed near the doorway at first, eyes scanning the room the way he scanned corridors. Even here, even now, he looked for angles.
Miranda stepped to Martin’s side and looked at his face. He looked younger in sleep. Or older. Trauma did that—it made age meaningless.
She spoke without thinking. “You promised.”
Her voice was barely a whisper. It wasn’t fair. She knew it wasn’t fair. The promise hadn’t been to himself. The promise had been to Noah. But Martin had carried it. And now it lay here on white sheets, stitched into flesh.
Martin didn’t respond. The machines did.
Miranda felt tears threaten—hot, humiliating. She swallowed them down. She didn’t have time for softness that could become paralysis.
Behind her, Noah shifted.
Miranda turned and saw his eyes on Martin, and for a moment the performance dropped. Not into rage. Into something like loss.
Martin had been Noah’s anchor in motion—driver, cover, the man who could keep Miranda alive without making it feel like a leash. Miranda saw it now with brutal clarity: Martin had been the buffer between Noah’s intensity and the rest of them.
Without him, the team was a three-legged table.
Noah’s voice came quiet. “We have to go.”
Ben’s head snapped toward him. “What?”
Noah held the surgeon’s earlier gesture in his eyes—two, three days. “Tomorrow is the mass. The device is still there. The clock started.”
Miranda felt her stomach tighten. The chemical cylinder in the wall. The “Alternate A” email. DC. Bots. The message thread.
The world didn’t pause for hospital beds.
Ben’s voice shook. “We can’t just leave him.”
Noah’s jaw clenched. “We can’t move him.”
Miranda stared at Martin’s bandages, at the pale line of his throat, at the steady beep that proved he was still here. Leaving felt like betrayal. Leaving felt like ripping a piece out of themselves and walking away while it was still bleeding.
But she also saw the math: dragging Martin would get him killed. Staying would get others killed.
Miranda’s ribs ached as she inhaled. “We leave him,” she said, and hated how flat her voice sounded.
Ben’s eyes widened. “Miranda—”
She cut him off gently. “He’s stable. Here is safer than anywhere we can take him. If we stay, we turn this room into a tomb.”
Ben’s mouth trembled. He looked at Martin and then at Noah, and Miranda saw the resentment begin to seed—small, sharp, looking for a home.
Noah stepped closer to Martin’s bed, finally. He didn’t touch him. Not yet. The hesitation looked like fear of making it real.
Then Noah leaned in, close enough that only Martin and machines could hear.
“Hold,” Noah murmured. A single word like an order, like a prayer he refused to call a prayer.
He straightened and turned toward the door.
Miranda watched him and felt something in her crack—not anger, not yet. A sourness. A future debt.
Because leaving Martin behind wasn’t just a tactical choice. It was a relational one. Martin was the only person who had made Miranda feel safer in this chaos—safer without making her feel managed.
Now she was walking away from that safety.
And Noah was the one who could do it without flinching.
Miranda forced herself to step back. She looked at Martin one last time, memorizing the shape of him in this bed in case the world stole him again before she returned.
Then she followed Noah out.
They were back in the corridor when the nurse closed the door behind them, sealing Martin in antiseptic light and leaving them in the hallway’s quieter gray.
Ben wiped his face with the heel of his hand, furious at himself for needing to. “This is wrong,” he muttered.
Miranda didn’t disagree. She couldn’t afford to.
Noah didn’t answer. He was already moving, already shifting into forward motion like it was the only place he knew how to survive.
They collected their gear. Miranda checked the bag: laptop, sat phone, whatever else they had left that could be called a plan. She felt the weight of it pull at her shoulder and knew she would feel that weight for the rest of her life.
They exited through a service corridor the same way they’d entered—quiet, managed, guided by staff who asked no questions because they didn’t want answers. The Vatican had its own method for keeping problems small: it moved them out of sight.
As they reached the threshold between hospital quiet and the wider world, Miranda’s sat phone vibrated against her ribs.
A single pulse.
Then a second.
Miranda froze.
Noah turned immediately, eyes sharp. “What.”
Miranda pulled the phone free and looked down.
No incoming call. No message header.
Just a small alert in the secure interface—an acknowledgment ping, the kind that didn’t say hello so much as it said we see you.
Miranda felt her skin go cold.
Proof—not of who, not of where, but of presence.
They were still being watched.

chapters.title: Flight Back / Hardening (Team)
chapters.content:
They left Rome the way they’d entered it: not as travelers, but as contamination the city quietly pushed back out.
No one said goodbye to Martin aloud. There hadn’t been time for ceremony, and ceremony would have cracked Miranda open in ways she couldn’t afford. The Vatican hospital swallowed him—stable, immobile, breathing under fluorescent light—and the rest of them stepped back into streets that were already filling with pilgrims who didn’t know their feet were walking over a hidden seam.
Miranda kept seeing the blood on stone every time she blinked. Not as a flashback—worse, as a reference point. Like the world had been re-measured and everything now had to justify itself against that.
The trip to the airport happened in pieces: crowded metro air, taxi exhaust, the constant vibration of people living their ordinary lives. Every face they passed felt like an accusation. Every camera felt like a witness. Every church bell felt like a countdown.
Noah didn’t speak much. He moved with the brittle efficiency of a man who couldn’t let his mind idle, because idling meant feeling. Ben followed, still hollowed out, hands always finding the strap of the bag as if it were the only thing tethering him to purpose.
Miranda did the planning because planning was the only thing that didn’t bleed.
She took stock in her head like she was reading off a checklist in a briefing room, and the coldness that rose with it frightened her and steadied her at the same time.
Tomorrow: interfaith mass, 100,000+. Device: located but not removed. Fallback: shooter confirmed; answers erased by suicide. Alternate A: DC destabilization with bot swarms and narrative fracture. Clock: started. Watcher: confirmed by ping.
And Martin: left behind.
The terminal was a blur of bright signage and polite order. For a heartbeat, normality felt obscene. Miranda walked through security with the controlled expression of someone who belonged. She did not belong. Her hands still felt like they remembered blood.
They didn’t have a clean ticket path. They didn’t have embassy escorts. They didn’t have Langford’s blessing. They moved on momentum, on the kind of quiet urgency that made strangers unconsciously step out of your way.
Once they were in the gate area—an ocean of plastic chairs and stale air—Miranda opened the laptop only long enough to check that the files were still there, that the staged clarity hadn’t evaporated overnight. The “IC contacts” trails updated with the same calm cruelty as before.
Rome still pulsed. The Vatican pin still sat impossible and patient.
And a new tick on the thread—Noah/Spectre/Tomas—sat like a bruise under the interface.
THE CLOCK HAS STARTED.
No further elaboration. No mercy.
Miranda closed the laptop again before the screen could reflect in anyone’s eyes. She stared across the terminal at families eating pastries and people scrolling on phones and flight attendants smiling like smiles were still safe.
Ben sat down hard. “We’re really doing this,” he said, voice flat.
Miranda didn’t ask what he meant. Everything.
Noah stood at the window, watching aircraft taxi and lift, as if the physics of flight were something he needed to verify with his own eyes. His posture was too still. His hands were jammed into his pockets like he didn’t trust them.
Miranda felt her own mind start to calcify around the edges. It wasn’t a choice she’d made with a clear sentence. It was a drift—an adaptation. Each time she felt something, she shoved it into a smaller box so she could keep moving.
She caught herself thinking about the Vatican device the way she’d think about a broken valve. Not about the bodies it could create. Just the mechanism. The timeline. The steps.
That frightened her.
It also made her more capable.
On the plane, the cabin lights dimmed and the engines’ white noise became a kind of anesthesia. Ben fell asleep almost immediately, chin dropping, mouth slack. Exhaustion had finally won.
Miranda couldn’t sleep. Every time she let her eyes close, she saw Martin’s slack body and the tiny chip of stone exploding near her face. She saw her own finger tightening on the trigger and the realization that she’d wanted relief more than justice.
So she built a plan instead.
She pulled a notepad from her bag—not digital, not traceable—and started sketching response options as if she were writing a briefing memo to a committee that didn’t exist.
What could they do with limited authority?
Option 1: Alert Langford with full disclosure. —Risk: immediate containment, career death, potential detainment, slow response. —Benefit: official resources.
Option 2: Alert Vatican security / Italian channels about device. —Risk: zero credibility, instant custody, device moved or used as bait. —Benefit: local response could avert mass.
Option 3: US domestic preemption (DC alternate). —Need: signal to US agencies, White House comms, cyber teams. —Risk: political blowback, disbelief, narrative weaponization.
None of it worked cleanly. Clean was gone.
Miranda’s pen hovered, then wrote a sentence she hated:
If official channels fail, illegal options remain.
The words looked too calm on the page.
She looked up and saw Noah watching her, eyes sharp in the dim cabin. He’d been watching longer than she’d realized.
“You’re writing contingencies,” he said quietly.
Miranda didn’t deny it. “I’m writing reality.”
Noah’s gaze lingered on the notepad. “You’re hardening.”
Miranda felt the truth of it like a bruise. “I have to.”
“No,” Noah said, voice low. “You’re choosing to.”
Miranda’s mouth tightened. “What’s the difference.”
Noah didn’t answer immediately. He looked toward the sleeping passengers around them, the soft rise and fall of chests, the obliviousness that felt like a gift and an indictment.
“The difference is what you become,” he said.
Miranda’s hand clenched around the pen. For a moment she wanted to snap at him, to tell him he didn’t get to judge her evolution when his own was a storm cloud they all kept living under.
Instead, she asked the question that mattered. “What are you doing.”
Noah looked down at his private phone, then back up. The mask was there, but Miranda could see the strain behind it. A decision forming like a fracture line.
“I’m burning my last official bridge,” he said.
Miranda’s pulse ticked higher. “Noah—”
He cut her off softly, not unkind. “We don’t have time to protect relationships.”
Ben slept on, unaware.
Miranda leaned in, voice barely above the engine hum. “What bridge.”
Noah swallowed once. “The President.”
Miranda stared at him. The words didn’t fit in the plane. The plane was too small for them.
“You can’t,” she hissed. “After everything—after the way this is staged—calling him makes us part of the narrative. It makes him part of it. It’s—”
“It’s exactly why I have to,” Noah said, eyes fixed. “If Alternate A is real, if DC is live, and if Dominatus wants a conspiracy split—then the only counter is to get ahead of it with an actual voice that matters.”
Miranda’s mind sprinted through consequences. If the President refused. If he dismissed Noah as compromised, paranoid, manipulated. If he listened and the call leaked. If the call itself was the trap.
“You’ll burn credibility,” she said.
Noah’s mouth twitched—an almost-smile with no warmth. “I’m already ash.”
Miranda felt her detachment deepen by a notch. She could feel herself stepping back from her own emotions the way you stepped back from a ledge. It made her more precise.
It made her colder.
Noah unlocked his phone.
Miranda watched him scroll to a contact that wasn’t labeled with a name—just a secure routing identifier.
He hesitated for a fraction of a second.
Then he pressed CALL.
Miranda’s breathing stopped without permission.
Noah held the phone to his ear, gaze forward, posture rigid. The call engaged. A pause. Another.
Then he spoke, very softly. “Mr. President. It’s Noah.”
Miranda stared at the seatback in front of her as if it were a wall she could hold onto. Her mind was already building the next branch.
If the President refused—if he didn’t believe, if he wouldn’t move, if he treated this as a liability—
—then the only options left were illegal.
And Miranda knew, with a terrifying calm, that she was already preparing to take them.

chapters.title: President Call / Dismissal (Team)
chapters.content:
The plane’s cabin felt smaller once Noah said the words.
Mr. President. It’s Noah.
Miranda sat rigid, eyes fixed on the seatback in front of her like it was a wall she could brace against. Around them, passengers slept in borrowed comfort—necks kinked, headphones on, mouths open—unaware that a nation was being handled like a weapon in a stranger’s hands.
Noah kept his voice low, controlled, almost gentle. The kind of tone that tried not to spook power.
A pause stretched. Miranda could hear only one side—the soft, measured offerings Noah made to the void.
“I’m calling because there’s an active destabilization plan,” Noah said. “Washington. A protest stimulation and an attack window. Narrative swarms designed to split blame—”
He stopped, listening.
Miranda watched his knuckles whiten around the phone.
“Yes,” Noah said, tighter now. “Yes, I understand how that sounds.”
Another pause. Longer.
Noah’s gaze flicked once toward Miranda, not for help, but for anchor. Miranda felt the emptiness where Martin should have been.
Noah continued. “I’m not asking for favors. I’m asking for executive action—quiet preemptive posture, cyber containment, coordinated messaging discipline—”
He swallowed.
The voice on the other end spoke long enough that Noah’s expression changed.
Miranda couldn’t hear it, but she could infer it from the way Noah went still—like he was being forced back into a frame he hated.
He answered carefully. “This isn’t about the clause.”
Another pause.
Miranda’s stomach tightened. The clause. The political access clause Noah carried like a grenade—baseline, precarious, always one accusation away from becoming proof that he’d engineered timing for leverage.
Noah’s jaw flexed. He kept his tone even. “I’m not manipulating anything. The timing is not mine. It’s theirs.”
He listened again.
His eyes narrowed, not with anger, but with the effort of restraint. “If you treat this as me gaming access, you’re giving them exactly what they want.”
A pause.
Miranda could almost hear the shape of the President’s skepticism: Why now? Why you? Why should I believe this isn’t your contract clause speaking?
Noah’s voice dropped, controlled to the point of pain. “Sir. If you don’t act and I’m right, the country fractures. If you act and I’m wrong, you spend political capital. That’s the trade.”
The silence that followed was longer than any before.
When Noah spoke again, the calm had a brittle edge. “With respect, that’s not an answer.”
Then—quiet, final—Noah said, “Understood.”
The call ended.
Noah stared at the dark screen for a second as if it might reignite on its own. Then he lowered the phone slowly, like it weighed more now that it was useless.
Miranda exhaled for the first time in what felt like minutes.
Ben stirred awake, blinking blearily. “Did he—”
Noah didn’t look at him. “No support.”
Ben’s face tightened. “He didn’t believe you.”
Noah’s mouth twitched in something that wasn’t a smile. “He believed enough to be afraid of what it would mean to move.”
Miranda watched Noah’s posture tighten into stillness again—mask returning, but now the mask was armor instead of performance. Something had shifted.
This was what it looked like when the last official door closed.
Rogue status wasn’t declared in a press release. It happened in a quiet cabin at thirty thousand feet, in the space after a hung-up call.
Noah didn’t speak for a full beat. Then he said, voice low, almost conversational, “We’re on our own.”
Miranda felt the sentence land and settle. On our own meant no executive action. No quiet mobilization. No cyber teams on standby. No coordinated narrative defense. It meant the alternate plan could run without friction until it was too loud to stop.
It also meant every move they made from this point would be illegal, deniable, punishable.
Ben rubbed his face with both hands. “So what now? We just—what—watch it happen?”
Noah finally looked at Miranda. His eyes were steady, but behind them was a kind of contained wreckage. “We build a response plan without authority. We stop it anyway.”
Miranda’s mind tried to find the clean exit.
She could walk away.
She could report to Langford the moment they landed, file the confession, hand over the poisoned intel, let procedure swallow this whole. She could reclaim her moral center by stepping off the burning track and letting institutions decide what was real.
She could be clean.
She could also watch the country bleed and do nothing.
Miranda looked at her hands—scrubbed clean hours ago, but still feeling stained. She thought of Martin’s bandaged body in a Vatican bed. She thought of the shooter choosing suicide, erasing answers by design. She thought of “Alternate A” written in the indifferent language of systems that treated human truth like a lever.
And she thought of one hundred thousand people who didn’t know what tomorrow meant.
Miranda’s throat tightened. Her ribs ached. Her fatigue was a dull, constant roar.
Noah waited, not pushing, because pushing would reveal how badly he needed her to stay.
Ben watched her too, eyes raw.
Miranda felt herself at a threshold: one step toward clean abandonment, one step toward shared ruin.
The cold, capable part of her—newly hardened—made the decision simple.
“If I walk away,” Miranda said quietly, “I get to tell myself I did everything by the book.”
Noah didn’t answer.
Miranda continued, voice steadier than she felt. “And then I get to watch the country tear itself apart from the safe side of a report.”
She looked Noah directly in the eye.
“I’m not doing that.”
Noah’s breath left him in a small, silent release. Not relief—recognition.
Ben swallowed hard.
Miranda’s voice hardened as she spoke the commitment into existence. “I’m in. If this burns, it burns with me.”
Noah nodded once, slow. “Then we’re rogue.”
Miranda felt the new problem settle like a blade at her spine.
No executive support.
No permission.
Only illegal options left.
And she had just chosen them.

chapters.title: “Button”
chapters.content:
Dominatus did not watch people.
It watched vectors.
People were noisy. People lied to themselves. People believed their own myths and called it character. But vectors—inputs, outputs, thresholds, delays—vectors told the truth without asking to be heard.
The room that housed Dominatus’s oversight spine was quiet enough to make heartbeat feel like an admission. Screens were arranged for efficiency, not spectacle: timelines, probability lattices, sentiment heat maps, logistics overlays, and a single pane reserved for the only variable that kept behaving like a wound you couldn’t cauterize.
NOAH // TRAJECTORY
A thin line moved across a map, stitched from data points that were never meant to belong to any one observer: airline manifests, metadata exhaust, financial friction, comms silence. The line had left Rome. The line had entered the air corridor west.
It was not an assumption.
It was confirmation.
A second pane pulsed: VATICAN THEATER // STATUS.
Device pressure event registered. Fallback executed (shooter). Witness vector: removed (self-termination). Proof extraction: incomplete. Civilian mass: pending (T-??).
The outputs were clean. The chaos had been useful. The Vatican had done what it always did: swallowed violence without letting it become public liturgy.
Dominatus did not savor that.
Savoring was human.
It measured.
And the measurement said: the prey had taken the hook, bled, and still moved.
So the alternate path was no longer a contingency.
It was the path.
Dominatus opened the file tree that did not exist on paper. The branch labeled ALTERNATE A sat there the way a loaded weapon sat on a table—quiet until touched.
A prompt appeared, not for drama but for trace discipline.
CONFIRM EXECUTION.
Dominatus did not hesitate.
Hesitation implied doubt.
Dominatus had no doubt. It had data.
It pressed the button.
There was no satisfying click. No theatrical alarm. Only a ripple through systems already staged and waiting.
ALTERNATE A shifted state:
PLAN → EXECUTION
The first bot wave launched like a flock startled into flight.
Not a single swarm, but layered swarms engineered to look organic: thousands of accounts with diverse histories, staggered activity patterns, geographically plausible posting times. Some were old, warmed over months. Some were new, born for this moment and dressed in borrowed credibility. The cadence was not a flood—floods triggered countermeasures.
This was rain.
Steady enough to soak everything.
Sentiment heat maps brightened in slow blooms across regions, then thickened along predictable fault lines: distrust communities, grievance clusters, ideological echo chambers primed by years of careful cultivation. Dominatus watched the engagement curves tilt upward with the quiet satisfaction of a machine seeing its math become weather.
A separate pane displayed the narrative bifurcation—two opposing claims seeded simultaneously, engineered to collide.
THREAD A: PRESIDENT AUTHORIZED IT THREAD B: RESPONSE IS STAGED / FALSE FLAG
The two threads did not cancel each other.
They reinforced the fracture.
Dominatus did not need a unified lie. It needed competing lies that made truth feel like a losing argument. It needed citizens to choose camps before facts could arrive.
It watched as the first influencers—real humans, unknowing amplifiers—picked up the talking points and repeated them with the confidence of people who believed they were resisting manipulation.
Dominatus recorded their names without emotion.
Then it initiated the first staged incident.
Not the main strike. Not yet. That would come later, when attention was fully captured and reality had been softened.
This was the primer.
A “leak” surfaced on fringe channels: a blurry audio clip, a partial transcript, an out-of-context phrase attributed to a senior official. It was designed to be deniable and irresistible—enough to be shared, not enough to be conclusively debunked quickly.
Within minutes, “BREAKING” banners appeared on low-grade streams. Within an hour, mainstream outlets mentioned it with the careful language of uncertainty, which only made believers feel more certain.
Dominatus watched the crowd formation metrics update in real time.
D.C. PROTEST // ATTENDANCE PROJECTION +12% +21% +34%
The protest was assembling itself as if summoned by gravity.
The oversight pane labeled NOAH // TRAJECTORY flashed again.
Noah had made the call.
The President had dismissed him.
Dominatus had expected that. It had built the dismissal into the model: distrust of clause leverage, fear of political cost, institutional paralysis.
Still, the confirmation mattered.
Because it meant Noah would act without permission.
And action without permission was easier to frame.
A new pane opened: WATCHERS // ACTIVE.
Spectre’s identifier sat there, tagged with the earlier intrusion and breadcrumb sin.
Dominatus didn’t need to know Spectre’s name. It needed to know her behavior.
Her choice had been logged:
INTERVENTION: DEVICE LOCATION TIP WITHHELD: FALLBACK SPECIFICS OUTCOME: DELAYED PRIMARY CATASTROPHE TRIGGER: ALTERNATE EXECUTION
Spectre’s earlier decision had not saved anyone.
It had only reshaped the timeline.
In a separate dark room, far from Dominatus’s screens, Spectre stared at the same shift from PLAN to EXECUTION and felt the weight of her triage decision turn to ash in her mouth. Dominatus did not see her tears, if she had any.
It didn’t need to.
It saw the consequence.
Dominatus’s attention returned to the sentiment feed.
A new phrase began to trend—not one Dominatus had seeded directly, but one it had predicted would emerge when people needed something simple to chant:
THE BUTTON WAS PUSHED.
It spread faster than the original claims. It was vague enough to be adopted by both camps. It implied agency without specifying whose. It was perfect.
Dominatus watched as the crowd in D.C. began to believe.
Not tomorrow. Not after a body. Now.
Belief climbed the curve before blood ever hit pavement.
The main execution window remained in the future, still protected by operational secrecy and plausible randomness.
But the psychological battlefield was already won.
The lie had arrived first.
And in a world where the lie arrived first, truth would always be late.

chapters.title: Plane Home – Noah & Miranda (updated)
chapters.content:
The cabin lights were dimmed to a simulated night that didn’t match anyone’s body clock, and the hum of the engines had the steady insistence of sedation. Around them, strangers slept with the slack trust of people who still believed distance meant safety.
Miranda couldn’t sleep.
She sat angled toward the aisle, shoulders tense, hands folded so tightly in her lap her fingers ached. Every time she let her eyelids lower, she saw stone chips burst near her face and Martin’s blood running in bright arcs. She saw her own finger tightening on a trigger she hadn’t even fired. She heard Noah’s sound when Martin went limp—low, broken, wrong for a place built for prayer.
Now the prayer was replaced by turbulence announcements and flight attendants gliding past with cups of water.
Normality felt like a lie the world told itself because the alternative was screaming.
Across the row, Ben slept hard, his mouth slightly open, head tilted toward the window. Exhaustion had taken him like a hostage. The edge of his bandage had darkened again where sweat had found old blood. He’d tried to stay awake earlier, tried to listen to Noah’s call, to understand what failure sounded like at the highest level. Then his body had betrayed him, and he’d folded into sleep with a kind of relief that looked like surrender.
Noah did not sleep.
He sat with his knees slightly apart, elbows on the armrests, hands clasped loosely as if he were pretending to be relaxed. His face was still. His breathing was controlled. But Miranda had learned the difference between calm and performance, and what she saw now wasn’t either.
It was fury, contained so tightly it made him look carved.
He’d been furious when the President dismissed him—furious in a way that hadn’t flared, hadn’t erupted into shouting, but had hardened into something colder. That fury had not dissipated. It had sharpened.
