And then the channel hissed, as if the air itself wanted to swallow the coordinates before she could catch them.

chapters.title: Bots = Blame
chapters.content:
The rally kept chanting even after the danger was gone.
That was the part Miranda couldn’t stop thinking about—the way the crowd never knew it had crossed a cliff edge and come back. The way a mass of people could be saved from a mechanism and still remain hungry for a story that wanted blood.
She moved along the perimeter in the aftermath with her posture deliberately loose, the practiced calm of someone who belonged near barricades. The adrenaline in her veins had nowhere clean to go now that the sniper window was dark. Her hands still felt the recoil of her pistol shots, small and sharp. Her forearm stung where grit and stone had kissed skin.
Elaina’s deniable team drifted out of the maintenance corridor like they’d only been checking a utility issue. No sirens. No stampede. The crowd kept flowing.
Less blood.
Miranda told herself that mattered.
And then the other clock struck.
Her burner buzzed first, vibrating against her ribs with frantic insistence. The sat phone followed a heartbeat later—an echo like someone in the network wanted to be sure she noticed.
Miranda stepped into the shadow of a service alcove and looked down.
The screen was already full before she could open anything.
Clips.
Not one clip. Dozens. Hundreds. The kind of flood that didn’t arrive as a wave but as rain that instantly became a river.
A shaky phone video of the crowd turning their heads at the sound of Ben’s shot—captioned as gunfire from “federal agents.” A blurred frame of a man running—captioned as “shooter escaping,” then reposted with a different caption: “crisis actor.” A cropped audio snippet of someone shouting near a barricade—remixed with a siren sound layered in, posted as “they’re poisoning us.”
Miranda’s eyes scanned fast, mind trying to triage the impossible.
THREAD A was already screaming:
THE PRESIDENT ORDERED IT. EXECUTIVE FALSE FLAG. THEY WANT MARTIAL LAW.
THREAD B answered with equal velocity:
STAGED RESPONSE. THEY’RE LYING ABOUT AN ATTACK THAT NEVER HAPPENED. THE REAL CRIME IS THE COVER-UP.
There were bot fingerprints everywhere—identical phrasing, synchronized posting, accounts with absurd engagement curves that no real human could sustain. But bots weren’t the point anymore.
The point was that real humans were sharing.
Real humans were believing.
The lie had arrived first, just like Dominatus wanted. And now it was self-propagating, feeding on fear and righteous certainty.
Miranda watched the engagement numbers spike and felt something in her chest hollow out.
She’d stopped the device.
She’d dragged a sniper’s attention onto herself.
She’d kept civilians alive.
And the country was fracturing anyway—just with less blood on pavement.
Elaina’s voice crackled in her ear. “Miranda, you seeing this?”
Miranda’s throat tightened. “Yes.”
Elaina sounded angry, breath fast. “They’re calling it a fed op. They’re saying the President authorized chemical dispersal. They’re—Jesus—”
Miranda closed her eyes for a half second, then reopened them on the feed.
She couldn’t arrest a story.
She couldn’t handcuff a narrative.
She could cuff a person. She could neutralize a vial. She could put a bullet into a window frame. She could even die to keep a crowd safe.
But she couldn’t stop the public from believing the lie once it became part of them.
That realization hit harder than any bullet.
Saving lives hadn’t saved truth.
It hadn’t saved trust.
It hadn’t saved the nation’s shared reality.
Miranda felt devastated in a way she hadn’t felt since she’d started hardening—because devastation required acknowledging you’d wanted something better than survival.
She had wanted it to mean something.
It did mean something. People were alive.
But now, watching the feeds fracture, she understood the enemy’s elegance: Dominatus didn’t need bodies if it could turn the living into enemies of each other.
Ben’s voice came through, tight. “Miranda. Noah’s getting hammered online. They’re posting his face.”
Miranda’s pulse snapped up. “Where is he.”
“Moving,” Ben said. “He’s—he’s trying to keep ahead of the surge.”
Miranda scrolled faster.
There—an old photo of Noah, scrubbed from some archive and reanimated with fresh captions.
DARKWELL WAS THERE. HE KNEW. WHY WAS HE AT A ‘PROTEST’ WITH A WEAPONIZED EVENT?
A video clip—grainy, distant, but unmistakably him, moving near a side corridor. The angle made it look like he was directing someone.
The clip was being posted by accounts that shared the same writing cadence, the same punctuation, the same phrase ordering. The watermark Tomas had built into his breadcrumb—Miranda didn’t know it existed, not explicitly—but she could feel the architecture of framing in how quickly the narrative latched onto Noah as a handle.
A scapegoat with a face.
A story with a villain.
Miranda’s phone buzzed again with a new alert—this one not from social feeds but from an official comms channel she hadn’t expected to still have access to.
A push notification, sterile and formal:
STAND BY FOR DIRECTIVE. DO NOT ENGAGE MEDIA.
Langford’s shadow, reaching from afar.
Miranda’s jaw tightened. The temptation to smash the phone against stone rose and passed.
She kept scrolling.
Another clip: a man’s voice, edited and re-edited until it sounded like he was confessing. The caption read:
THE PRESIDENT ORDERED IT. HE’S COVERING IT UP.
And then, beneath that, another post:
DARKWELL CONTRACT. 30-DAY WINDOW. WHAT WAS HE PROMISED?
Miranda’s stomach turned.
They weren’t just blaming. They were building a scaffolding for an official narrative. Something that could be adopted by power because it served power: we were misled, we were manipulated, we had to contain him.
Her sat phone vibrated—one hard pulse.
Miranda froze.
She lifted it, thumb hovering. The interface displayed a single line of incoming text—no warmth, no sender identity, just the kind of language that belonged to institutions deciding the story was now the priority.
A White House routing tag.
And beneath it, a brief, cold directive that felt like a door closing:
CONTAINMENT PROTOCOL INITIATED.
Miranda’s chest tightened.
Containment didn’t mean truth. It meant burial.
It meant the West Wing would do what it always did when a crisis threatened to become a story that could break legitimacy: it would bury the details, scrub the edges, and hand the public a scapegoat that made the chaos look manageable.
Someone had to pay.
And Noah was already trending.
Miranda swallowed, tasting metal.
Elaina’s voice came again, urgent. “Miranda—what do we do?”
Miranda stared at the feed where Noah’s face kept replicating. She felt sick with the clarity of it.
They had won the physical fight.
The narrative weapon had fired anyway.
And now the White House was moving—not to expose Dominatus, not to unify truth, but to bury everything and choose a payer.
Likely Noah.

chapters.title: Noah Cut Loose / New Terms (endgame)
chapters.content:
Noah didn’t feel the shift in the crowd when it happened.
He felt it in his phone.
The rally’s noise still pressed from every direction—chants, whistles, the slap of flags in wind corridors that had done exactly what Miranda said they’d do. People milled like nothing had nearly killed them. The physical clock had been defused.
The narrative clock was now a wildfire.
Noah moved along an exterior service lane that ran behind a line of temporary fencing, head down, posture loose enough to look like staff. Every few steps his eyes flicked upward to windows and rooflines on instinct. He didn’t trust that one sniper meant there wasn’t another. He didn’t trust that a neutralized vial meant there wasn’t a second device nested somewhere else.
Trust was a fantasy. Only patterns were real.
And right now the pattern said: containment.
His private phone vibrated—one pulse, then another, the cadence of a call that had too much authority behind it.
Miranda was in his ear a second later, clipped and controlled. “Noah, you’re trending harder. White House containment posture is live.”
“I know,” Noah muttered, not slowing. “I can feel it.”
“Get out,” Miranda said. “Now.”
Noah didn’t answer. He was already moving, already counting exits. He was already tasting the bitter irony: he’d saved strangers in the open and now the system would punish him for being visible.
The phone buzzed again, and Noah glanced down.
A secure number.
Not Spectre. Not a routed crucifix. Not Tomas’s poisoned breadcrumb.
This one had a certain kind of formatting Noah recognized the way you recognized a weapon by silhouette.
West Wing.
He answered.
“Hodge,” Noah said, not as a greeting. As a warning.
Hodge’s voice came through clean and cold, the kind of calm that wasn’t calm at all. It was control masquerading as civility.
“Darkwell,” Hodge said. “You’re out.”
Noah kept walking, turning a corner into deeper shadow. “That’s not how the contract works.”
A faint pause—Hodge letting Noah hear himself talk before stepping on his throat.
“You failed your thirty-day deliverable,” Hodge said. “Or if you prefer the language your lawyers wrote: you did not deliver within the agreed timeframe.”
Noah’s jaw flexed. Deliverable. Timeframe. Words that took lives and turned them into calendar entries.
“No,” Noah said, voice low. “You’re rewriting.”
“We’re terminating,” Hodge corrected. “Your protections are revoked.”
Noah felt the phrase land like a door slamming shut in his spine.
Protections revoked meant more than losing access.
It meant the system had just decided he was no longer useful as a controlled anomaly. He was now a liability.
And liabilities got eaten.
Miranda’s voice hissed in his ear. “Noah—what is it?”
Noah didn’t answer her yet. His attention stayed on the line.
Hodge continued, as if reading from a memo written long before Noah was born. “You have X days to depart U.S. jurisdiction. Voluntarily. Quietly.”
Noah’s eyes narrowed. “And if I don’t.”
A breath. The faintest hint of satisfaction, quickly buried.
“Then you become fair game,” Hodge said. “No resources. No containment. No obligations on our end to keep you alive. And anyone near you becomes collateral by proximity.”
Noah’s chest tightened.
Collateral.
It was the word the system used when it wanted to pretend it didn’t mean names.
Noah thought of Miranda on the perimeter, grit on her cheek, standing between a sniper and a crowd. He thought of Ben with a rifle, hands shaking. He thought of Elaina, dragged into mud by Miranda’s choice. He thought of Bryce’s knife-line back at the manor, Sarah’s wet eyes. He thought of Martin—stable but broken—under Vatican lights.
Anyone near him becomes collateral.
Hodge wasn’t threatening him.
Hodge was isolating him.
“You’re doing Dominatus’s work for them,” Noah said quietly.
Hodge’s response was immediate, bored. “Spare me.”
Noah felt the old fury surge—hot, violent, seductive. The urge to burn something just to feel control again.
He swallowed it down so hard it hurt.
“This is political,” Noah said. “You’re burying the truth and choosing a payer.”
Hodge’s voice stayed flat. “I’m choosing stability.”
Noah’s laugh came out harsh and humorless. “Stability built on lies collapses anyway.”
Hodge didn’t rise to it. He didn’t need to. He held power in his calm.
“Your clock is not our clock,” Hodge said. “We don’t have the luxury of your… theater.”
Noah’s eyes went cold. Theater. The word made him see Dominatus’s screens, their sentiment maps, their buttons.
Hodge continued, “You have X days. After that, the apparatus that has been politely holding its breath around you will exhale.”
Noah felt Miranda’s silence in his ear. She knew what that meant as well as he did.
Hodge’s final words were the cleanest cut. “Don’t make this messy. You were never meant to be permanent.”
The call ended.
Noah stared at the dark screen for one heartbeat, two.
Then he lowered the phone.
Miranda’s voice came fast, tight. “Noah. Tell me.”
Noah found his voice again, but it felt like it came from somewhere deeper than strategy.
“Hodge just revoked my protections,” Noah said. “Thirty-day window—done. I have days to leave, or I’m fair game.”
Miranda swore—soft, vicious. “That means—”
“It means they’ll scapegoat me,” Noah said. “Officially or quietly. It means anyone standing next to me becomes a target.”
Ben’s voice cut in, breath sharp. “Noah, we need exfil.”
Miranda’s voice steadied. “We can get you out. We go quiet. We survive. We keep our heads down until—”
Until what? Noah thought. Until the country finished tearing itself apart? Until Dominatus installed someone “stable”? Until Martin healed and Bryce forgave him and the narrative stopped eating?
There was no until.
Only choices.
Noah stood in the shadow behind the barricades while the rally roared on, louder now because the lie had arrived first. He watched people wave flags and chant slogans and believe whatever their feeds told them to believe.
He could go quiet and survive.
He could vanish into whatever dark infrastructure still existed for men like him.
He could protect the people near him by leaving them behind.
Or he could stay and fight—stay and try to break the machinery even as the system turned him into a villain, even as every move he made would endanger Miranda, Ben, Elaina, Sarah, Bryce… everyone.
Noah’s protector identity cracked again, splitting into two ugly truths:
Protecting them might mean leaving.
Saving strangers might mean sacrificing his own.
Miranda’s voice came quieter, like she sensed the fracture widening. “Noah… we can run.”
Noah closed his eyes for a fraction of a second and saw Martin on the table again. Saw Bryce’s accusation. Saw the crowd that would never know what had almost happened.
He opened his eyes.
His voice came out low, controlled, dangerous in its steadiness.
“Do we go quiet,” Noah said, more to himself than to Miranda, “and survive…”
He paused, listening to the roar of the country’s nervous system turning on itself.
“…or do we stay and fight,” he finished, “and make everyone near me pay for it.”

chapters.title: Tomas’s POV of the battle (highlights only)
chapters.content:
Tomas watched the crowd swell the way other men watched the weather.
Not with awe. Not with fear. With calculation.
From this height, the city was geometry—streets feeding bodies into a wide, flag-dotted basin, barricades and magnetometers acting like valves, police lines like sutures trying to keep the pressure from splitting. Every chant, every sign, every raised phone was a data point. The rally had a name, a slogan, a permission slip stamped by bureaucrats who still believed paper mattered.
None of that mattered.
A crowd was a creature. It had lungs. It had a pulse. It had a mind that did not belong to any one of its cells. Give it a story to bite down on, and it would chew until the bone cracked.
He rolled the earpiece between his fingers and did not put it in. Let the humans chatter. Let them pray into radios and pretend their words were steering anything. The plan had been set long before these people woke up and dressed themselves in their convictions.
The plan had been set before this country was a country.
Dominatus had always preferred the grand gesture—the clean, bright rupture that made lesser minds kneel in the ash. They mistook spectacle for control. They mistook a single day of terror for a century of obedience.
Idiots.
A plan that took a century to mature was medium term. There were plays that took longer than that to pay off—seeds buried under empires, watered by wars, harvested by heirs who never knew why they were hungry.
Dominatus had forgotten that.
This plan—this one—was not just loud. It was suicidal. It played too close to the edge of the blade. It assumed the world would take the hit and wobble and settle into a new shape.
It might. It might also shatter. And if it shattered, there would be nothing left to rule. Nothing left worth ruling.
Ash did not obey.
He did not care about the humans in the basin below him. They were loud animals in shoes, easy to frighten, easier to inflame. They would trample each other to death and blame the other side for their own panic. They would do it while filming, while screaming about justice, while begging strangers online to witness them.
He did not care if ten thousand died.
He cared that Dominatus had forgotten what they were.
Immortals did not build kingdoms to stand in the rubble with no one left to kneel.
He shifted his weight, boots scraping grit. The rooftop was unfinished, a skeletal crown of rebar and poured concrete with temporary fencing and tarps flapping in the wind. A construction project stalled by money, by permits, by politics—by all the little human frictions that meant nothing on a long enough timeline.
Down there, the first wave of chanting rolled like surf.
He could feel it before he moved a muscle. The edge of anger, bright as wire. The hunger to belong to something bigger than the self. The desire to be righteous, to be seen as righteous, to be absolved by volume.
He smiled without humor.
There were snipers in place. There were devices tucked where they would turn a street into a throat. There were men on the ground whose only job was to shove in the right moment, to make the first punch unavoidable, to give the crowd permission to become a stampede.
Dominatus called it Phase. They loved their labels.
Tomas called it waste.
He lifted his hand to the metal frame of the roof access door, resting his palm against the cold surface as if it steadied him. It didn’t. Nothing steadied him. He had learned long ago that steadiness was not peace. It was a decision. A posture.
He closed his eyes and reached—not outward into the wind, but inward, toward the place where his own emotion lived like a buried animal.
That was the trick.
You didn’t throw power like a spear. You bled it into the world like dye.
Anger was the easiest color.
He let a thread of it loosen. Not too much. If he poured himself into the crowd, his own people would feel it too. They always did. Humans were porous. They soaked it up and became an extension of the storm.
Sometimes they broke.
He had broken them before.
He thought of Oran for a moment—stone collapsing, dust and screaming, a brother’s face in glass—then pushed it away. Memory was indulgence. Memory made you sentimental. Sentiment was the leash lesser life forms used to pull you into their pit.
He let the anger go anyway. A controlled leak. A measured spill.
Below, the chant sharpened.
He could hear it change even at this distance, the syllables tightening like fists. The rhythm lost its humor. People stopped smiling and started spitting. A man on the edge of the crowd turned his head as if hearing a new instruction, and his shoulders rose. A woman beside him frowned, not understanding why she was suddenly furious at the stranger who bumped her elbow.
A bottle lifted.
Not thrown yet. Just lifted, as if the hand holding it was waiting for permission.
Tomas opened his eyes and watched the ripple travel.
It moved like a disease, clean and predictable. Argument. Retort. Shove. A cellphone held high to record. A second shove, harder, because now the crowd had an audience.
A police officer raised a hand, palm out, as if his gesture could stop momentum.
Someone screamed, “They’re pushing us!”
Someone else screamed back, “You’re lying!”
And just like that, the crowd found its two sides. Found its enemy. Found its reason.
A chant twisted into a roar.
Tomas breathed out slowly through his nose. The air tasted of tar and distant exhaust.
Good.
Not because he wanted blood. Blood was cheap. Blood was abundant. Blood did nothing but soak into asphalt.
Good because the creature had woken up. Because the creature was now moving in the direction he needed it to move.
Dominatus wanted a detonation. Tomas wanted a vector.
His phone vibrated once in his pocket.
He didn’t check it.
He already knew who it was. Or rather, what it was.
The Collector. The Auditor. The Mouth. Whatever title they wore this decade, it was always the same function: a hand that reached out of the faceless dark and reminded you that you belonged to it.
He waited until the vibration stopped. Until the device went still again, like a heart holding its breath.
Then he reached into his pocket, retrieved it, and looked.
No name. No number. No identifier beyond the cold weight of inevitability.
A single line of text:
REPORT.
He stared at it for two seconds longer than he needed to, then slid the phone back into his pocket without replying.
Let them wait.
A century was medium term. Forty-five seconds was an eternity.
Below, the first bottle flew.
It arced high and bright and shattered against a barrier, glass and liquid spraying. The crowd surged in delight and outrage at the same time. The sound that rose wasn’t fear. It was permission.
Tomas felt it land in his own chest like a tuning fork.
This was the part Dominatus loved. The moment the world tipped.
This was also the part that would get them killed if they weren’t careful.
He didn’t have to look to know the devices were still where they’d been placed. He didn’t have to imagine the wind patterns. He’d studied them days ago. He’d watched the forecasts. He’d seen the angles of the buildings, the way the streets funneled.
Dominatus thought the gas was the point.
The gas was the amplifier.
The point was the story.
The bots were already in motion. He could feel them the way you could feel a storm coming—an electronic pressure shift in the air. Rumors seeded. Clips spliced. Accounts with flags in their profiles howling in unison about who had done what. A narrative scaffold erected faster than any human mind could dismantle it.
Truth didn’t matter.
Only velocity.
He watched a pocket of the crowd tighten near a barricade where a cluster of young men had pressed forward, faces red, eyes bright. One of them shoved a woman aside with a careless elbow. She stumbled, caught herself, turned and screamed something that was swallowed by the roar.
The men laughed.
Tomas let his anger thread drift toward them, a touch heavier now. Not enough to make them violent. They already were. Enough to make their violence contagious.
The woman’s hands balled into fists.
She swung.
A punch landed, awkward and furious, and the man she hit blinked in surprise before his own face twisted into joy. He hit back, harder, and someone screamed, and suddenly the crowd was no longer a crowd. It was a fight looking for excuses.
Beautiful.
Pathetic.
His earpiece crackled from a pocket on the roof where one of his subordinates had left a burner radio. He ignored it. The human voice rose and fell in panicked bursts.
They always panicked when control slipped.
Men like that wanted to believe they were in charge because they held the gun. But the gun was never the point. It was the punctuation.
Tomas moved to the edge of the roof and looked down at the glittering river of people. There were flags. There were signs. There were faces lifted to the sky as if the sky cared.
A single thought flickered through him—an old one, an unwanted one.
Noah should be here.
Not in the sense that he should be watching this spectacle. In the sense that the only reason Tomas was doing any of this was because Noah would not move unless forced.
Noah would not walk into the dark and confront Dominatus on his own. He would argue. He would hesitate. He would try to save everyone. He would waste time negotiating with systems that didn’t want to be saved.
Noah was always choosing the dog over his family.
He had chosen lesser life forms again and again and called it virtue.
Tomas called it betrayal.
He flexed his jaw, feeling the scar line pull. There were eras where he had fantasized about cutting that softness out of Noah like rot. There were eras where he’d believed pain could teach Noah what love couldn’t.
Pain had taught him nothing.
Not permanently.
It had only made Noah more stubborn.
And still Tomas hadn’t given up. He couldn’t. Giving up would mean admitting that all of this—the centuries, the blood, the empires, the careful shaping—had been for nothing more than hatred.
Hatred was a childish fuel. It burned hot and quick.
Tomas’s fuel was something older.
Obsession.
A need that tasted like salt and iron.
He wanted Noah to look at him and finally stop flinching away into human tenderness. He wanted Noah to meet him where they belonged—above the lesser life forms, above the petty morality of the dying.
He wanted his brother to choose him.
He wanted it so badly it made him sick.
So he made the world sick instead.
His phone vibrated again.
REPORT.
Still no reply.
Let them tighten their leash. Let them send their instrument. Let them threaten him with exile, with pain, with punishment.
He would accept exile. He had lived without a home for longer than any of them could imagine. He would accept pain. Pain was honest.
What he would not accept was annihilation.
Dominatus’s plan flirted with escalation that would turn nations into reflex. Reflex would turn into missiles. Missiles would turn into fire that did not care whether you were immortal or not. Fire was democratic. Fire did not respect bloodlines.
Fire made everyone ash.
Tomas would not let them burn the board.
Not because he cared about the pieces. Because he cared about the game.
Because he cared about winning.
He glanced at his watch. A human device, a human habit. He kept it because time mattered even if it did not scare him.
Two minutes until the next phase marker. The first narrative wave would crest. The first physical spark would force a response. Once response happened, the cameras would capture it. Once cameras captured it, the story would calcify. Once the story calcified, it would not matter what really happened.
Humans would tear at each other for years over a lie they couldn’t admit they loved.
All as planned.
Mostly.
There was always a variable. There was always a flaw in the machine.
That flaw’s name was Noah.
And Noah was not here yet.
Tomas reached into his coat and pulled out a second phone—burner, cheap, anonymous. He thumbed it awake.
A single contact saved. No name. Just a number he’d memorized centuries ago, in a language that no longer existed.
He typed without looking at the screen.
COORDINATES SENT. GO NOW.
Then, because he couldn’t help himself, because cruelty was the only way he knew how to be honest, he added:
DON’T BE LATE.
He hit send.
The phone went dark.
Somewhere across the city, a man would feel his device buzz and his stomach tighten and his mind snap into focus like a blade.
Good.
Noah would come.
Not because Tomas asked. Not because Tomas needed him. Noah would come because he couldn’t stand the idea of strangers dying on his watch. Noah would come because his weakness was also his compulsion.
Tomas would use that compulsion the way he used everything else.
He would point it. He would aim it. He would fire it at the only target that mattered.
Dominatus.
And if Noah’s team died along the way?
If Miranda Cruz bled out on a rooftop?
If Martin’s heart stopped in a foreign hospital?
If Ben took a bullet meant for someone louder?
Tomas would not shed a tear.
Their lives were coins. Their deaths were weights. Their suffering was leverage.
Noah’s suffering was the only currency that bought movement.
A roar rose from the crowd as something else happened—an officer shoved, a protestor fell, a camera caught the angle just right. The creature found a new reason to surge.
Tomas felt his own anger thread catch and stretch. For a moment it tugged at him, trying to pull him down into the same animal frenzy he was feeding.
He cut it off. Not with scissors. With will.
He was not one of them.
He never would be.
He took a step back from the ledge and looked out across the city, across the avenues and rooftops and flagpoles, across the place where Noah would arrive and make his predictable choices.
Tomas smiled again, and this time there was teeth in it.
He didn’t want a quiet victory. Quiet victories were for bureaucrats and cowards.
He wanted the fight.
He wanted to feel Noah’s hands on his throat and know—absolutely know—that Noah had finally stopped pretending he could keep his hands clean.
He wanted Noah to break.
Because if Noah broke, Noah might finally become what Tomas had always believed he could be.
Not a shepherd of lesser life forms.
A king.
The phone in his pocket vibrated a third time. He ignored it.
Let the cabal wait.
He listened instead to the city.
To the rising hysteria.
To Rodney’s speech translated into sirens and screams.
To the creature waking up and turning its hunger into motion.
And somewhere in all that noise, beneath the human panic and the human rage, Tomas felt the shape of it—the approaching presence, the familiar gravity of his brother’s attention being dragged, unwilling and inevitable, toward the trap Tomas had built.
“Come on,” Tomas murmured to the wind, the words too soft for any human ear.
“Choose.”

chapters.title: Exit Interview
chapters.content:
## Gray Manor
Miranda's phone buzzed on the nightstand. She looked at the screen.
Mami.
She'd been avoiding this call for three weeks.
32 missed calls. 68 texts. Each one increasingly frantic.
She'd managed a quick text—I'm ok, call you soon, Love you—but nothing else. All that had done was start the guilt. Why don't you have time for your mother?
Miranda took a breath. Picked up.
"Mami."
"Mija." Her mother's voice was warm. Beaming with love. Judgmental love, but only for the moment, until her mother had extracted her pound of flesh. "You have time now?"
"I'm sorry. It's been—"
"Busy. I know." Her mother's tone shifted—not cold, but pointed. "Too busy to call your mother for three weeks. Too busy to let me know you're alive. But not too busy to send a text that says nothing."
"Mami, I—"
"Do you know what I thought? When I didn't hear from you? When you didn't answer?" Her mother's voice cracked slightly. "I thought something happened. I thought you were hurt. I thought—" She stopped. Took a breath. "You can't do that to me, Miranda. You can't disappear like that."
Miranda closed her eyes. "I know. I'm sorry."
"You're sorry." Her mother's voice softened, but the edge was still there. "You're always sorry. But you still do it."
"I know."
A pause. Then, quieter: "Are you eating?"
"I'm eating, Mami."
"Good." A pause. "And... are you sleeping?"
Miranda hesitated. "Yes."
"Without the pills?"
Miranda's jaw tightened. "I don't need them anymore."
"Since when?"
"Since I started the new job."
Her mother was quiet for a moment. "That's good, mija. That's... that's good."
But her voice didn't sound like she thought it was good.
"Hector wants to know if you're coming for Sunday dinner."
Sunday dinners were always a thing. Her step-father always made sure that everyone was home with no TV at 6pm for dinner. No TV, just family, unless the Mets, Jets or Knicks were on. Then it was a raucous chorus of disappointment and cheers. Her family loved the underdogs. Since they had moved to Frederick to be closer to her after the incident. The Mets, Jets, and Knicks, are now the Orioles, Commanders, and Wizards. Still underdogs, just underdogs that had been good in recent memory. She hadn’t been to one in close too six weeks. 
"Yes. Mami."
"Good. Hector misses you."
Miranda couldn’t help but smile. Hector misses me. The familiar bloom of warmth started in her chest. She really needed to hug her mother right now. She fought back tears. I’ll see her Sunday. 
"Tell him I miss him too."
"You can tell him yourself. Sunday. Six o'clock."
"I'll be there."
"How's Emilio?" Miranda asked.
"He's fine. Working at the warehouse in Hagerstown now. Good hours." Her mother's voice softened further. "He asks about you."
"Tell him I'm good."
"Are you?"
There it was.
Miranda sat up. "Yes, Mami. I'm good."
"You left your job."
"I did."
"In the middle of the night."
"It wasn't the middle of the night. It was…"
"You didn't tell anyone. You just left."
Miranda exhaled. "I had to. There were... complications."
"Complications." Her mother's voice sharpened. "What kind of complications?"
"Political ones. Some very powerful people asked me to cross lines I wouldn’t cross. It wasn't safe for me to stay. It wasn’t safe for anyone in the family."
“I’m proud of you for not doing something you knew was wrong.”
Silence.
Then: "And now?"
"Now I have a new job. A better one."
"Better how?"
Miranda leaned back against the headboard. "I’m working for a…” How the hell do I say what Noah is, without saying WHAT Noah is? “Well mom, he’s a billionaire. My new job is more than double my government salary. Full benefits. Medical, dental, and legal coverage for all of us. He even put a trust in your name. If anything happens to Hector’s pension, it's covered. If anyone comes after the family legally, it's handled."
Her mother was quiet.
Miranda kept going. "I had to sell my townhome; it wasn’t safe. The money's already transferred. I'm living somewhere safer now. Somewhere no one from the old job can find me."
"Where?"
"A manor. My boss's estate."
"You're living with your boss?"
"It's not like that. It's huge. I have my own space. It's just... safer. And it saves money."
Her mother didn't respond right away. “Safer?” She paused again. “What are you mixed up in Mija?”
“I’m ok mom, it’s less dangerous than my old job.” Miranda winced, hoping her mother wouldn’t taste the lie, “The risks were political; it was better to just take myself off the board entirely.”
Miranda waited, her stomach in knots. Somehow, this felt more dangerous than Mali.
Finally: "This boss. What's his name?"
"Noah."
"And he did all of this? The trust, the house, the legal coverage?"
"Yes."
"Why?"
Miranda’s mouth dropped. Why did he? 
"Because I'm good at my job. Because he values me."
"And what do you do for him?"
"I do a lot of what I did before, just for a foundation."
“You stop traffickers?”
“Not exactly. I’m fighting bad guys. Still helping people, just differently.”
"This is dangerous work?"
"Sometimes."
"And he pays for your family's legal protection because you're good at your job?"
Miranda's jaw tightened. "It's not just—"
"I'm not saying you're not good at what you do, mija. I'm asking why he's paying for it."
"Because I left my career for him. Because I burned bridges. Because he knows what that costs."
Her mother was quiet again.
Then: "Have you met anyone who used to work for him?"
Miranda blinked. "What?"
"Anyone who worked for him before. Who left. Have you met them?"
"I... no. But people don't leave. Everyone's been there for years. Ten, fifteen, twenty years. No one wants to leave."
"Why not?"
"Because it's a good job, Mami. Because they're taken care of. Because—"
"What do they do when they're finished?"
Miranda stopped.
"What?"
"When they're done working for him. When they retire. What do they do?"
"I don't know. I haven't asked."
"You haven't asked."
"No. Why would I?"
Her mother didn't answer.
Miranda felt a flicker of irritation. "Mami, what are you trying to say?"
"I'm not trying to say anything. I'm asking."
"Asking what?"
"How long do people usually stay?"
"I told you. Years. Decades."
"And then?"
"And then what?"
"And then what happens to them?"
Miranda opened her mouth. Closed it.
She didn't know.
Her mother's voice softened. "I'm not saying he's bad, mija. I'm not saying he's controlling you. I'm just asking... what does 'after' look like?"
Miranda felt something tighten in her chest. "After what?"
"After this job. After him. If you ever want to leave—what does that look like?"
"I don't want to leave."
"I know. But if you did."
Miranda was quiet.
Her mother kept going, gently. "Who are you to people who've never met him? What do you tell the next person who asks what you do? What's on your résumé?"
Miranda opened her mouth to answer.
Then stopped.
What was on her résumé?
Tracked immortal threats across the world using satellite imagery. Researched historical artifacts for events that are only known to exist because my employer lived them thousands of years ago. Assessed risks from entities that don't officially exist.
She couldn't write any of that.
And the skills that were transferable—data analysis, pattern recognition, threat assessment—those were things she'd been doing for years before Noah. They weren't special. They weren't worth double her salary.
What made her valuable to Noah was that she understood his world.
And no one else would.
"I don't know," she said quietly. "I haven't thought about it."
"Why not?"
"Because I'm not leaving, Mami. I just got here."
"I know." Her mother paused. "You loved your old job, but you left it. You burned those bridges. You said so yourself."
"That was different."
"How?"
Miranda didn't answer. How could she? “I can’t really say mom.”
Her mother was quiet for a moment. Then: "I worked in the garment district for fifteen years. You know that."
Miranda frowned. "Yes."
"Good pay. Good benefits. The women I worked with, we were family. I loved that job."
"I know."
"But when the factory closed, I didn't know what to do." Her mother's voice was careful. Even. "All my skills were for that one place. All my references were from that one place. I was fifty-two years old, and I didn't know how to explain what I did to anyone who hadn't worked in garment."
Miranda was quiet.
"I'm not saying your job is the same," her mother continued. "I'm just saying... I didn't think about what came after until there was no factory to go back to. And by then, I didn't know how to be anything else."
Miranda stared at the ceiling.
Her mother's voice softened. "I've seen this before. Not the same. Just... similar."
"What?"
"Someone takes care of you. Pays for everything. Protects you. Teaches you. And you're grateful. And you should be. But then one day you realize you don't know how to leave. Not because they won't let you. But because the world outside doesn't have a place for you anymore."
Miranda felt her throat tighten.
"That's not what this is."
"I'm not saying it is."
"He's not like that."
"I believe you."
"Then what are you saying?"
Her mother was quiet for a long moment.
Then: "I'm saying I don't know what happens when it ends. And I don't think you do either."
Miranda stared at the coffered ceiling in her room in the estate, on a bed bigger than her childhood bedroom. She could hear a small army of landscapers mowing outside. 
She tried to think of a response.
I have money saved. The trust. The salary.
All Noah's.
I have skills. Experience. A career.
In a world no one else knew existed.
I have references. Martin. Sabrina. Ben.
All of them worked for Noah.
I have—
Nothing.
She had nothing that was hers.
Silence.
She thought about what she'd have to say in an interview.
Where did you work for the last year?
She couldn't say Noah's name. Couldn't explain what the “organization” did. Couldn't describe the job or the accomplishments.
She'd have to lie.
Or leave a gap.
And either way, she'd be explaining why she left a promising career in federal law enforcement to work for... what? A private security consultant? A philanthropist? A man with no public profile and no official organization?
It would sound insane.
Or worse—it would sound like she'd been doing something illegal.
Her mother's voice was soft. "Okay."
"Okay?"
"Okay. You don't know. That's okay."
Miranda felt something crack in her chest. "Mami—"
"You're an adult, mija. You make your own choices. I'm not going to tell you what to do."
"But?"
"But I love you. And I worry. That's what mothers do."
Miranda closed her eyes. "I know."
"Come to Sunday dinner."
"I will."
"And take a nap. You sound tired."
"I will."
"I love you."
"I love you too, Mami."
Her mother hung up.
Miranda sat in the dark, phone still in her hand.
She thought about what her mother had asked.
What does 'after' look like?
She didn't know.
She thought about the people she'd met. Martin. Sabrina. Ben. All of them had been with Noah for years. A decade or more.
She'd never asked what they did before.
She'd never asked if anyone had left.
She thought about the trust. The legal coverage. The medical expenses. The townhome sale.
All of it real. All of it generous. All of it justified.
But none of it was hers.
It was Noah's.
And if she left—
She stopped the thought.
She wasn't leaving.
She'd just gotten here.
Miranda had grown up with her mother's worries the way other people grew up with background noise. Constant. Familiar. Usually wrong.
When her brother lost his job at the pizza place, her mother had decided it was because the owner didn't like Puerto Ricans, not because Emilio came in late and high and never apologized for either. Racism was easier to blame than her son.
This is just like then. She wants to see what she wants to see. She thinks I’m still broken after Bill. 
She set her phone down and looked outside at the grounds of Gray Manor. The manicured lawns, forest beyond them, and the Blue Ridge Mountains beyond that. She took a cleansing breath; her heart was racing. It helped slow it down. 
Why does mom stress me out like that?

chapters.title: Inevitable
chapters.content:
## Covert Facility on Gray Manor’s Grounds July 31, 11:08 PM
It had been a week since the attack thwarted on the capital. Noah used the excuse of nursing his wounds. Even he knew that was a lie. He’d been avoiding Tomas. 
He swiped his card and put his thumbprint on the panel. The door clicked open, and he entered. There weren’t many rooms here. This facility was rarely used. In fact, there was only one resident. 
There was a guard watching the monitors while a Nationals game played on the radio. He yawned as Noah entered, and then quickly hid it when he saw who it was. 
Noah pretended not to notice. “How is he?”
“Still quiet, sir. The motion sensors haven’t tripped at all.”
“Very well. That’s going to change tonight. Go to the main house and bring back some food, refreshments, and fresh clothes. Tell them it’s for me.”
“Understood. Sir, are you sure you don’t need some backup.”
“Positive, this man poses no threat to me. Turn off the recording please.”
He did as instructed, and Noah watched him drive away.
He opened the door to the room. 
There he was, all four limbs strapped to a metal table sunk six inches into the concrete. The cords and braces could take over 1000 lbs of force before failure. 
His head lay over the edge of the table. The wrought iron fence tip was still poking through his skull on both sides.
Noah put on some medical gloves and pulled. It didn’t budge; the coagulated blood and brain matter had formed a seal. He took a deep breath, twisted, and pulled. 
The bar came free, and Noah dropped it to the ground. He sat in the chair in the corner, where Tomas would see him immediately. He waited. The longer you had been dead, the longer it took to resurrect.
Fifteen minutes later, Tomas began to stir. 
Five minutes after that, his eyes opened. 
Tomas smiled at Noah.
Noah stood up and punched him in the face, hard.
His neck snapped, and Tomas went limp again.
Noah growled and punched Tomas’ chest. “Ilū iparrasūni.”
He pulled out the headrest from the table and Tomas’ head flopped on it.
Ten minutes later he was back.
“That was rude brother.”
Noah hadn’t been watching this time. He was looking at his phone. He looked up at Tomas.
“Give me one reason not to put that bar through your skull again and bury you in concrete.”
Tomas smiled. 
Noah’s hand clinched where Tomas couldn’t see it.
“Because Dominatus is already a mile ahead, and you don’t even know you’re in a race. You don’t even know where to start.”

chapters.title: Epilogue
chapters.content:
## Two weeks later… 11:06 PM, Gray Manor Basement
The door beeped, and the lock disengaged. Miranda stepped into the most comfortable and posh SCIF she had ever seen. 
It looked less like a secure facility and more like an English lord’s library someone had sunk into the bedrock. Floor‑to‑ceiling bookcases climbed the paneled walls, their shelves sagging with leather spines gone the color of old tobacco. Carved woodwork crowded every surface—scrollwork, lion heads, a dented crest over the cold marble fireplace—catching the low amber light of a single shaded lamp. This didn’t look like a room where secret work was conducted.
A worn Persian rug swallowed her footsteps. The leather chaise in the center of the room shone with the soft gloss of decades of use, surrounded by little islands of side tables stacked with books and ashtrays. The air smelled faintly of dust, scotch, and beeswax polish. The only concession to the twenty‑first century was the hum of hidden ventilation and the unobtrusive security hardware; everything else said Oxford don, not classified bunker.
She had been living here for weeks and still wasn’t used to Noah’s idea of “operational.”
The corner held a standard, government-issued cabinet safe. Its harsh, gray, utilitarian exterior camouflaged by a teak surround. A Keurig sat atop the safe with a small selection of pods. 
A mahogany bar cabinet sat across from the fireplace. Crystal, a single bottle of scotch. She took it and a tumbler to one of the smaller desks—antique rosewood, inlaid and carved, the kind of craftsmanship she’d only ever seen in museums. The chair was a modern ergonomic mesh thing with perfect lumbar support.
She poured a generous double.
“Well… time to go down the rabbit hole.”
The folder waited on the blotter. Diagonal orange stripes: SPECIAL ACCESS REQUIRED. Martin had left it there for her, “We have a copy of the full, unredacted program brief. Don’t ask how we have it, you’re not cleared for that. Read it and ask me any questions you have.” he’d said. 
Miranda took a long swallow. The scotch burned clean and deep. She looked at the bottle again, “Damn. Okay.” She topped her drink off and sighed.
She opened the folder. The first page was the usual “If you read this any further, you have officially signed your life away.”
ORACLE VEIL (OV) access must be granted only to individuals with a demonstrated need-to-know…Unauthorized disclosure… may result in… loss of clearance, and criminal prosecution…
She’d seen variations of this a hundred times. It still made her shoulders involuntarily tighten.
She flipped the page.
TOP SECRET//SAR-ORACLE VEIL//NOFORNSPECIAL ACCESS PROGRAM BRIEF: ORACLE VEILORIGIN DATE: 11 February 1858STATUS: ActivePRIMARY DOMAIN: Strategic Prophetic IntelligenceASSET DESIGNATION: IMMORTAL – ORIONLOCATION: Gray Manor (SOX-104 Facility), near Leesburg, Virginia
“Wait…  what?” She read it back to herself three times, saying it aloud the last, “Eleven February 1858.” 
“That’s a typo, right?” 
Something told her it wasn’t. 
She started skimming. Then, her eyes slowed.
…prophetic capabilities of a non-mortal intelligence asset……provided highly accurate, non-linear predictive intelligence since his arrival in the United States in 1858…
“It’s not a typo.” She rubbed her forehead, her hand shaking as she took another drink. 
…influenced every major geopolitical decision made by the United States since the Civil War…
She exhaled, realized she’d been holding her breath.
“Okay,” she muttered. “Sure. Why not.”
She turned the page and started to skim again.
Big, bold subheading.
SUBPROGRAM: (TS//SAR//OV//EV) ECLIPSE VEILPurpose: Identify, monitor, and assess potential immortal entities other than Orion…
“How many of these assholes are there?”
Her eyes caughtmore black programs as she scanned down the page.
STONE DRAGON – Chinese state-sponsored immortal research programs…COLD SIBYL – Russian-affiliated immortal entities… Directorate-S… figure known as “Yurodivy” (“The Holy Madman”)…CHIMERA SANCTUM – fringe cults, doomsday groups that worship Immortals or seek to become them…
She stopped, blinked, and went back over the names.
“You can become one of them?” she whispered.
Another page. A heading midway down hooked her eye.
1936 – Treaty of Singular Accord (FDR administration)…Official U.S. Ambassador to the Immortals…
Then,  THREAT MATRIX. Columns. Presidents. Incidents. Her gaze snagged on names and eventualities.
President: Abraham Lincoln – Ignored Vision:… war extended by 28 months… additional estimated 140,000 casualties… “missed opportunity for a faster, more integrative Reconstruction”…
She swallowed.
“Jesus, Abe. What the fuck?”
Lower down:
OV‑093E – removal of Iranian Prime Minister Mohammad Mossadegh…Ripple effects: Iran–Iraq War… rise of Hezbollah… Iranian nuclear ambitions… U.S.–Iran enmity lasting through six presidencies…
Miranda sighed, rubbing her eyes.
“Fifty years of conflict and death. It could have been avoided?”
She skimmed faster now, catching only the hooks:
July 13, 1945 – OV‑075T – ground invasion of Japan… 500,000 U.S. casualties… mass civilian deaths…
Another page:
OV‑313B – 1998 – commercial airliners hijacked, weaponized, simultaneous attacks on U.S. soil…OV‑291O – Abbottabad, Pakistan – delay vs. strike scenarios…Tier 0 Asset – Preserve Predictive Integrity…
POTENTIAL DEFECTION RISK: no specific loyalty to the United States… motives “questionable,” “self-serving”…
Her eyes stung. The words started to blur.
END OF FILETOP SECRET//SAR-ORACLE VEIL//NOFORN
She glanced up at the world clock on the wall. Fourteen time zones. DC read 01:22.
The scotch bottle on the desk was more than half gone. She couldn’t remember pouring that many. She leaned back in the chair and quickly realized she had, indeed, had that many. 
“What the fuck am I into here?” she whispered.
Miranda stood up. She braced herself. Her world was spinning in front of her, and the scotch was threatening to exit. She wasn’t entirely sure it was all from the drinks. 
She slowly put the folder away, spun the lock, killed the lights, and walked upstairs to her room. Over the last weeks, she’d been scared, exhausted, hopeful, even awed. Never all at once. Her heart was pounding through her fatigue.
I’m in over my head. What am I doing? How can I get out of this?
She arrived at her room and lay in bed, staring at the ceiling.
I can’t. It’s too late. I can’t go back. I can’t unknow this.
Sleep never came that night.
